It is an oligolectic specialist, and in Amsterdam it turns up wherever wild or garden Campanula grows in parks and verges. Females nest in hollow plant stems and small pre-existing cavities. The default insect placement of flower-visitor and flower-bobber captures its foraging behaviour well, and no override is needed.

Lamb's Quarters is one of Amsterdam's commonest annual weeds of disturbed, nutrient-enriched ground: allotment edges, building sites, pavement margins, and rubble dumps. As a classic nitrophilous ruderal it colonises freshly turned bare soil far more readily than established park turf, making ground-urban the dominant habitat. It also volunteers on the margins of park meadow areas and community gardens, so ground-park applies as a secondary. The mealy-white leaf surface is a recognisable field-mark in summer.

Chionaspis gleditsiae (and related armoured scale species) infest the bark of Gleditsia triacanthos (honey locust), a popular Amsterdam street tree chosen for its tolerance of urban stress and compacted soils. Adult females are permanently sessile beneath flat, white, elongate scale covers on bark and small branches, drawing sap; infestations cause twig dieback and bark discolouration. Override from the insect default to tree-bark / idle-only as a tree-feeding microorganism on Gleditsia.

Chironomid midges are the dominant aquatic macroinvertebrates of Amsterdam's canals and ponds, with larvae living in bottom sediments where they process organic matter and form a critical food base for fish, waterfowl, and bats. Adults emerge in vast numbers on warm evenings, forming the dancing mating swarms visible over canal surfaces and park ponds throughout spring and summer. As aquatic insects, water-edge and water-body correctly replace the flower-visitor default: larvae are water-body, emergence and adult swarming are water-edge. The sky-looper secondary captures the aerial mating swarm behaviour; idle-only covers adults resting on vegetation near water.

This row groups four small songbird species recorded around Cupressocyparis (Leyland cypress) hedges in Amsterdam: greenfinch, dunnock, wren, and robin. All four use dense conifer hedges intensively — greenfinches nest and roost in the foliage, wrens and dunnocks forage in the low scrubby base, robins hold territories in hedge-and-shrub habitats. Tree-canopy is the best available tag for all four since the hedge canopy is the dominant vegetation element; tree-flitter matches the short flights between perches. No override required; the default is the least-wrong single tag for this multi-species row.

Greenfinch and house sparrow are both common Amsterdam residents recorded on Styphnolobium japonicum (Japanese pagoda tree), a street tree that produces seed pods relished by seed-eating birds. Greenfinches are predominantly canopy feeders on seeds; house sparrows forage on the ground but also perch and feed in canopy. In the context of a Styphnolobium observation, both species are in the canopy, and the small-songbird default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter is a reasonable fit for the combined row. No override required.

This row aggregates greenfinch (Chloris chloris) and goldfinch (Carduelis carduelis) observations on Laburnum and Liquidambar street trees in Amsterdam. Both are small to medium finches that forage primarily in the canopy: greenfinches eat seeds from buds and Liquidambar fruits, goldfinches target seed heads. They are resident in Amsterdam year-round and move in small flocks between adjacent trees, matching the small-songbird default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter without modification.

The broad centurion is a striking soldier fly with metallic green and bronze iridescence; adults are regular visitors to umbellifers and other open flowers in parks and gardens across Amsterdam, where they bask and feed on pollen and nectar. Larvae develop in moist, decaying plant matter in soil. Despite being in family Stratiomyidae rather than Megachilidae or Apidae, the adults' flower-visiting behaviour matches the insect default of flower-visitor and flower-bobber well. No override is needed.

Choristoneura tortricid moths (budworm complex) attack the new shoots and needles of Larix (larch) and occasionally spruce in northern European plantations and ornamental settings; Larix is used in Amsterdam parks and the Amsterdamse Bos. Young larvae mine needles; later instars spin together new growth and feed within the shelter. The caterpillars are sedentary within their web-bound feeding shelter in the canopy and the adult moths are nocturnal. Override from the insect default to tree-canopy / idle-only as a tree-feeding microorganism on Larix.

The Chorthippus biguttulus species complex are medium-sized field grasshoppers that live almost entirely in dry to moderately dry grassland, park edges, and rough vegetation; they are not flower visitors. Males produce a characteristic buzzing stridulation while perched on grass stems or bare ground. Their movement is predominantly short hops followed by long stationary periods, making idle-only the most honest animation. Ground-park replaces the insect default of flower-visitor because these orthopterans never visit flowers to forage.

The Field Grasshopper is one of the more heat-tolerant British and northwest European grasshoppers, found in dry, short-grassland patches in Amsterdam parks, dunes, and sunny roadside verges during summer. It stridulates loudly on warm afternoons and makes short, powerful jumps when disturbed, covering considerable ground between resting on bare earth or low stems. The insect default flower-visitor / flower-bobber does not fit: this is a ground-dwelling grass-feeding species with no meaningful flower association. Ground-park / idle-only captures the resting-and-stridulating posture; park-roamer as secondary reflects its short bounding hops across the sward.

The Black-headed Gull is the commonest and most urban-adapted small gull in Amsterdam, present in vast numbers on the IJ, the Amstel, park ponds, and across the city's built fabric throughout winter and in smaller numbers in summer. It loafs and rests on open water surfaces, drifting with currents, but also wheels in noisy flocks over the city, patrols market streets for scraps, and follows canal boats. Water-surface is the dominant habitat where it spends most of its resting time, while anywhere captures its true generalist urban character. Sky-looper covers the aerial circling behaviour common when the species is agitated or prospecting, while water-drifter covers stationary loafing. The default bird tag of tree-canopy / tree-flitter is entirely wrong.

Chromaphis juglandicola is a specialist aphid that feeds on the undersides of Juglans (walnut) leaflets, forming pale-yellow colonies that excrete heavy honeydew and can cause early leaf-fall. Juglans regia is an occasional tree in Amsterdam parks and larger gardens. The aphid does not use an alternate host and remains on walnut from spring until autumn leaf-fall; colonies are effectively stationary on the leaf underside. Override from the insect default to tree-canopy / idle-only as a tree-feeding microorganism on Juglans.

The Rosemary Beetle is a jewel-like leaf beetle with metallic purple and green stripes, introduced from southern Europe and now well established in Dutch urban gardens. Adults and larvae feed on rosemary, lavender, thyme, and sage, making them a regular presence on ornamental herb plantings in Amsterdam parks and courtyards. Despite its name it is not a rosemary specialist and ranges across a variety of aromatic shrubs. It is predominantly a phytophagous crawler that rests on its host plants; flower-visitor does not apply. ground-park and idle-only reflect its plant-clinging, sedentary adult lifestyle.

Chrysomela populi and C. tremula are chunky red-and-black leaf beetles whose adults and larvae feed openly on Populus (poplar) leaves; poplar is common along Amsterdam's canals, in the Amsterdamse Bos, and as street plantings. Larvae congregate on leaves and can skeletonise entire branches; adults overwinter in bark but are prominently visible in the canopy during summer. Despite adults being mobile on the leaf surface, their movement at pixel-art scale is imperceptible. Override from the insect default to tree-canopy / idle-only as a tree-feeding microorganism on Populus.

The Little Snipefly is a predatory rhagionid fly of damp, shaded park margins and woodland edges where it perches on low vegetation waiting to capture small insects. It is not a flower-feeder and has no meaningful relationship with blooms, so the default flower-visitor / flower-bobber combination does not apply. Adults perch motionless on leaves with the characteristic head-down stance of the family. Ground-park captures its preferred damp vegetated margins in Amsterdam's parks and canal-side green strips; idle-only is the correct movement for a sit-and-wait predator.

The Black Snipefly is a slender rhagionid fly that favours damp habitats — wet meadow margins, canal banks, and lush vegetation alongside water. Adults are typically seen resting motionless on leaves with their legs held out in a characteristic posture; they are predatory but hunt small invertebrates without elaborate movement. In Amsterdam the most likely sightings are along canal and ditch edges where vegetation is rank and humid. water-edge is the dominant placement, with ground-park as a secondary for vegetated margin areas away from standing water. idle-only captures the resting-on-leaf adult behaviour.

The Garden Grass-Veneer is a common crambid grass-moth found across Amsterdam parks, lawns, and canal verges wherever fine grasses grow, which is nearly everywhere in the city. Adults rest during the day with their characteristic slim, rolled-wing posture flat against grass stems, relying entirely on crypsis; they fly at dusk and in low light but do not visit flowers. The larvae are grass-root feeders, completing their full life cycle in lawn turf without reference to flowers at all. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is wrong for this specialist grass-moth; ground-park and idle-only accurately describe its daytime ecology in Amsterdam's parks and verges.

Chicory is a tall, blue-flowered perennial of roadsides, railway embankments, and disturbed urban ground, making ground-urban the more accurate dominant habitat over the plant default of ground-park. Its vivid sky-blue ray florets open only in the morning and are highly attractive to bees and hoverflies. In Amsterdam it colonises dry, compacted soils along verges, cycle paths, and neglected plots, thriving in the nitrogen-rich conditions of urban margins. The tap root is deep and drought-tolerant, allowing it to persist in poor urban substrates. Observations are most frequent from July to September when it is in full bloom.

The White Stork is a large migratory bird that passes through and occasionally breeds near Amsterdam, foraging in open grasslands, park meadows, and wet fields for large invertebrates and small vertebrates. In the Amsterdam context it is most often seen in open grassy areas such as Amsterdamse Bos and surrounding polder meadows rather than dense tree canopy. Water-edge is a genuine secondary habitat as it also wades in shallow water and damp margins. The park-roamer animation captures its slow, deliberate walking gait through open green. It returns from its African wintering grounds in late March and departs by August or September.

Cinara cedri is a large, grey-brown conifer aphid that feeds on the shoots and young needles of Cedrus (cedar) trees, producing copious honeydew that coats the ground beneath and supports sooty mould. Cedar trees — Cedrus atlantica, C. libani, and cultivars — are planted in Amsterdam parks such as the Vondelpark and Sarphatipark. Colonies cluster on young shoots and remain on cedar without alternating to another host plant. Override from the insect default to tree-canopy / idle-only as a tree-feeding microorganism on Cedrus; consistent with the related cedrobium-laportei cedar aphid row.

The cypress aphid is a large, grey-brown phloem-feeding aphid that colonises the bark of Chamaecyparis, x Cupressocyparis, and Thuja in Amsterdam parks and garden streets. Colonies cluster tightly on stems and branchlets, barely moving except to probe with stylets, so idle-only is the correct animation. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber does not apply here; these aphids never visit flowers and spend their entire lives anchored to bark tissue. Tree-bark as the dominant habitat places them correctly on the trunk and branch areas of the host sprite.

The speckled larch aphid is a large lachnid that feeds in dense colonies on the bark of young larch shoots and the main stem of Larix trees. In Amsterdam it is recorded from the single Larix specimen in the dataset and appears in spring and early summer. Colonies are sedentary, covered in a fine wax bloom, and show no foraging movement, making idle-only the correct animation tag. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden because this species is strictly bark-feeding and never associated with flowers.

Cinara pinea and C. pini are among the largest European aphids, forming conspicuous brown colonies on the bark of Pinus shoots and branches. They feed by piercing bark to tap phloem sap and remain anchored in place, occasionally attended by ants. In Amsterdam pines — including those in Vondelpark and the Amsterdamse Bos — colonies persist through the growing season with minimal positional movement, so idle-only is the correct movement class. The insect category default flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden in favour of tree-bark / idle-only because these are entirely bark-bound organisms.

Enchanter's-nightshade is a shade-loving perennial herb of damp, humus-rich soil under tree canopy in parks, cemeteries, and woodland edges. It favours undisturbed ground beneath mature trees, exactly the conditions Amsterdam's older parks and the Amsterdamse Bos provide in abundance. The plant spreads slowly by underground rhizomes and produces small white flowers in summer that attract tiny flies and midges. The category-default ground-park / none pairing is accurate and needs no override.

Creeping Thistle is one of Amsterdam's most widespread and persistent wild plants, occurring in parks, allotments, canal verges, and any disturbed or neglected green space. It spreads aggressively via horizontal roots and wind-dispersed plumed seeds, rapidly colonising open ground. Its small, pale lilac flower heads are intensely attractive to bumblebees, honey bees, butterflies, and many beetle species from July through September. Despite being considered a weed in managed settings, it is ecologically valuable as one of the highest-yield nectar plants in the urban flora. The default ground-park placement is appropriate for Amsterdam observations.

Spear thistle is a biennial herb of rough grassland, park edges, disturbed ground, and roadsides throughout Amsterdam; it is one of the most common thistles in the city. Its large purple flower heads are highly attractive to bumblebees, butterflies, and goldfinches. The plant default of ground-park and none describes its placement well. No override is needed.

Clepsis consimilana is a small tortricid leaf-roller moth whose larvae feed inside rolled leaves of a wide range of deciduous trees and shrubs common in Amsterdam, including linden, hawthorn, and rose — all abundant in the city's parks and canal-side plantings. Adults rest by day on foliage and bark in the canopy layer, relying on their mottled tan-and-cream pattern for camouflage; like most tortricids they fly only after dark and do not feed at flowers as adults. Tree-canopy is the correct habitat as the entire life cycle is tree-associated, and idle-only captures the motionless daytime resting posture. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is wrong for this canopy-resting leaf-rolling moth.

Clepsis dumicolana is a small tortrix moth whose larvae feed in rolled or tied leaves of shrubs and low trees — blackthorn, hawthorn, and various Rosaceae — making it a canopy-edge insect in Amsterdam hedgerows and park shrub layers. Adults are relatively small, pale-straw to ochre moths that rest motionlessly on foliage during the day and fly at dusk. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is inappropriate: this species is not a flower visitor and spends most of its life cycle in leaf-rolled feeding shelters. Tree-canopy / idle-only reflects the larval feeding niche and the adult resting behaviour; ground-park is a secondary for occasional adults found resting low in vegetation.

The Potato Mirid is a plant bug (Miridae) that feeds on the soft stems, flowers, and developing seeds of a wide range of herbaceous plants, including nettles, thistles, and potatoes. In Amsterdam it is found in parks, rough grassland, road verges, and overgrown urban corners wherever tall herbaceous vegetation grows. Adults are moderately mobile, moving between plant stems rather than true flower-visiting. The dominant habit is ground-park herbaceous vegetation with some movement between plants; flower-visitor is a weak secondary because it is found on flowering herbs, but it is a piercing-feeding bug rather than a true pollinator.

Closterotomus trivialis is a mirid plant bug found on various flowering plants in Amsterdam parks and rough grassland, typically feeding on the soft tissues and developing seeds of Asteraceae, Apiaceae, and similar composites and umbellifers. Adults are green-brown with subtle patterning and are most often found on flower heads or upper stem leaves of tall herbs in summer. The insect default flower-visitor / flower-bobber is a reasonable fit since this species genuinely spends its time on flowering plants; tree-canopy is added as a secondary because it can also be found on low-growing shrubby vegetation in the park understorey. Idle-only is added as secondary movement for the long periods spent motionless on a flower head.

The seven-spot ladybird is Amsterdam's most familiar aphid predator, observed on Tilia where it hunts lime aphid colonies in the canopy. Adults and larvae cling to leaves and stems, pausing frequently between aphid catches, so their net positional change is negligible and idle-only captures the on-screen animation well. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden because the primary habitat is leafy canopy rather than flowers, and the movement is predatory stalking-in-place rather than flower-bobbing. Tree-canopy placement snaps the sprite to the leaf zone of the nearest Tilia, which matches the field observation context.

This combined entry covers canopy-foraging ladybird beetles and lacewings observed across Juglans, Larix, Magnolia, and Parrotia in Amsterdam. Both groups are aphid and soft-bodied insect predators that spend most of their time perched on leaves or moving slowly between prey clusters; lacewings in particular rest motionless during the day. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden: neither ladybirds nor lacewings are primarily flower-associated in their feeding behaviour. Tree-canopy and idle-only place the sprite realistically in the leafy zone of the host tree.

The hawfinch is a large, powerful finch recorded on Carpinus (hornbeam) in Amsterdam, where it exploits the hard nutlets with its outsized bill. It is a shy, upper-canopy species that moves between trees in short direct flights, fitting the bird category defaults of tree-canopy and tree-flitter well. In Amsterdam it appears mainly during autumn and winter passage, favouring parks and old avenues with mature hornbeam and cherry. The default tags are appropriate without modification.

The Azure Bluet is a common and instantly recognisable damselfly with powder-blue and black banding along the abdomen, one of Amsterdam's most frequent waterside insects in summer. Adults perch motionless on emergent vegetation at canal and pond edges (idle-only dominant), making short hovering sallies over the water surface when hunting small insects or courting. Mating pairs form a wheel and oviposit into submerged or floating plant stems at the water surface. Habitat is overridden to water-edge dominant with water-surface secondary; movement keeps idle-only as dominant with the short hover-and-dart captured approximately by flower-bobber (the closest available class for short positional bobs without long transits).

The larch casebearer moth is a tiny micro-moth whose larva mines individual larch needles from inside a portable silken case, spending the entire larval period on the needles of Larix without ever visiting flowers. In Amsterdam it is associated with the limited Larix stock and is detected mainly through the characteristic needle-mining damage. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden because the organism is a strict foliage miner; tree-canopy and idle-only correctly place the sprite in the needle zone of the host larch and animate it as a near-stationary larval case.

Case-bearer moth caterpillars (family Coleophoridae) recorded on Cornus in Amsterdam are leaf-mining or leaf-fastening larvae that carry a portable case and graze foliage from a fixed position. They are firmly anchored to the host leaf surface and do not travel across the canopy, making idle-only the fitting movement class. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden because case-bearers are foliage microhabitat specialists, never flower feeders. Tree-canopy habitat correctly resolves the sprite to the leafy layer of the Cornus host.

This entry covers saproxylic (dead-wood-dependent) beetles associated with Hericium fungus and beech wood decay on Fagus in Amsterdam. Saproxylic beetles spend their larval phase tunnelling inside dead or decaying timber and adult beetles cling to or hide under bark; they are not flower visitors. The insect category default is overridden to tree-bark / idle-only, which correctly places the sprite on the trunk area of the host Fagus and animates it as a nearly immobile bark-surface beetle. Beech with veteran character in the Amsterdamse Bos provides the primary habitat for this guild in the city.

This entry covers the leaf-litter and soil-surface community of springtails and oribatid mites beneath Fagus in Amsterdam. Unlike their bark-dwelling relatives, these organisms live in the decomposing beech leaf litter and humus layer on the ground. Ground-park is the correct habitat, placing the sprite in the park polygon rather than on the tree trunk. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden because this is a litter/soil community; idle-only matches the below-threshold movement of mites and springtails at game sprite scale.

Bark-crevice springtails and psocids (bark lice) recorded on Ginkgo in Amsterdam occupy the fissured bark microhabitat, feeding on algae, lichens, and fungal spores on the bark surface. Psocids are tiny and move slowly and cryptically across bark; springtails in crevices show minimal net displacement at sprite scale. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden because both groups are strictly bark-surface organisms. Tree-bark habitat places the sprite correctly on the trunk area of the host Ginkgo.

Bark-dwelling springtails (Collembola) are tiny hexapods recorded across a diverse range of Amsterdam tree genera including Cercidiphyllum, Metasequoia, Parrotia, Paulownia, Platanus, Populus, and Quercus. They live in bark crevices, feeding on algae, fungi, and decaying organic matter on the bark surface. Their movements are too small and fast to represent as positional sprite movement at the map scale, so idle-only is the practical animation choice. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden because springtails are bark-microhabitat organisms, not flower visitors.

Colletes is a genus of solitary mining bees (Colletidae) that nest in sandy or bare soil burrows while foraging on flowers, often showing strong oligolecty on specific plant families. In Amsterdam, species like Colletes daviesanus and C. hederae are recorded on composite flowers and ivy in parks, cemeteries, and sandy urban soils. The flower-visitor/flower-bobber default fits well for foraging adults, with a sky-looper secondary to capture transit flight between foraging patches. Ground-park is added as a secondary for the nesting behaviour in bare soil.

The nut-tree tussock moth (Colocasia coryli) is a noctuid whose caterpillars feed on Carpinus (hornbeam) foliage in Amsterdam parks and avenues. The larva is a foliage-resting caterpillar that moves only slowly between leaves; the adult moth is nocturnal and rests immobile on bark or leaves during the day. Neither the larval nor adult stage involves flower-visiting, so the insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden in favour of tree-canopy / idle-only, which places the sprite in the foliage zone of the host hornbeam and renders it as a near-stationary caterpillar or resting moth.

The Western Jackdaw is a common corvid in Amsterdam that nests in building cavities, church towers, and chimney stacks, making wall-and-roof its primary habitat anchor for breeding and roosting. Outside the nest it is a true urban generalist, foraging on pavements, in parks, along canal banks, and in tree canopies — essentially anywhere food is available, particularly in the city centre around Centraal Station and the old church towers. Its movement alternates between short tree-to-tree or roof-to-roof hops (tree-flitter pattern) and deliberate ground walking while pecking for scraps (urban-walker). The default bird tag of tree-canopy / tree-flitter only partially captures this; wall-and-roof as the dominant habitat and the dual movement classes better represent Amsterdam Jackdaw ecology.

This combined entry records wood pigeons and corvids (jackdaws, crows, or magpies) together on Metasequoia and Nyssa in Amsterdam. Wood pigeons roost and forage in the canopy while corvids — especially hooded crows and jackdaws — regularly descend to paved areas to forage. The dual habitat of tree-canopy and anywhere reflects this genuine split, and the dual movement of tree-flitter and urban-walker captures the between-tree flight of pigeons alongside the pavement-walking of corvids. The bird default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter is extended rather than replaced to account for the corvid ground-foraging component of this mixed observation.

This three-species observation on Ginkgo in Amsterdam spans an urban generalist mix: wood pigeon (a canopy roosting bird), Eurasian magpie (equally at home in trees and on pavements), and hooded crow (a ground-foraging corvid). Because the three species span such distinct ecological roles, anywhere is the most honest dominant habitat rather than forcing tree-canopy. The dual movement of tree-flitter and urban-walker captures the between-tree movement common to all three and the pavement-foraging typical of the two corvids. The bird category default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter is overridden to reflect the strong corvid ground element in this mixed group.

Wood pigeons and collared doves are both canopy-roosting and canopy-nesting species frequently observed together in the dense evergreen foliage of x Cupressocyparis (Leyland cypress) in Amsterdam. Both species spend long periods sitting motionless in the canopy, making idle-only the dominant animation, with tree-flitter as a secondary for short movements between adjacent trees. Tree-canopy is the correct habitat; the bird category default movement of tree-flitter is demoted to secondary because the defining observation context for this combination is roosting in dense cypress foliage, not active flitting.

This combined entry covers wood pigeons and roosting blackbirds and other passerines observed in the dense canopies of Cercidiphyllum, Chamaecyparis, Cornus, Ilex, Liquidambar, Taxodium, Thuja, and Zelkova across Amsterdam. The common observation context is roosting birds settled in dense foliage, making idle-only the dominant movement animation; tree-flitter is the secondary for when individuals move between adjacent trees. Tree-canopy is the correct habitat for all species in this group since the primary observation is roosting in the leafy layer, not ground-foraging. The default tree-flitter movement is demoted to secondary because the roosting focus makes idle-only the dominant animation.

Field Bindweed is a persistent climbing perennial with white or pink funnel-shaped flowers that twines through fences, scrambles over pavements, and colonises disturbed ground, road verges, and rubble throughout Amsterdam. It is one of the most difficult urban weeds to eradicate due to deep vertical roots. The dominant habitat is ground-urban because of its strong preference for paved edges, rubble, and built-up disturbed ground, with ground-park as a secondary for park-margin and verge populations. Movement stays none.

Hedge Bindweed is a vigorous climbing perennial that scrambles through hedges, fences, and waterside vegetation across Amsterdam's parks and canal banks. It produces large white trumpet-shaped flowers through summer that attract pollinators. The default ground-park placement is appropriate as its roots anchor in vegetated soil even while its stems climb; the map sprite would show the plant at its ground origin. It is one of the commoner tall-herb species along the edges of Vondelpark and the Amstel river bank.

The dock bug is a large, brown coreid bug that feeds on the developing seeds of docks (Rumex) and sorrels, plants that grow abundantly in park margins, canal banks, and rough grassland throughout Amsterdam. It is a slow, cryptic feeder that relies on camouflage rather than rapid movement, making idle-only the appropriate animation. Ground-park is the dominant habitat because its host plants are almost always found in vegetated park-edge or rough ground settings. The flower-visitor insect default does not apply; it is a seed-sucker, not a pollinator.

The garden snail is one of the most familiar molluscs in Amsterdam, found in parks, gardens, canal-side vegetation, and even pavement edges wherever there is damp soil and decaying plant matter. It is a generalist herbivore–detritivore that moves so slowly it is effectively stationary from an animation standpoint, matching the mollusc default of ground-park and idle-only. Activity is predominantly nocturnal or after rain. No override is needed; the category defaults describe this species accurately.

Dogwood is a native deciduous shrub common in park margins, woodland edges, and scrubby areas throughout Amsterdam and the wider Dutch lowlands. It is a woody plant but not classified as a tree in the roster — it grows as a multi-stemmed shrub rarely exceeding 4 metres — so ground-park is the correct habitat rather than tree-rooted. Its deep crimson autumn stems make it a popular ornamental choice in parks such as Amsterdamse Bos. The plant default (ground-park, none) fits exactly and no override is needed.

The carrion crow (Corvus corone) and its sibling taxon the hooded crow (Corvus cornix) are the dominant large corvids of Amsterdam, recorded here on Juglans (walnut). These birds exploit almost every urban habitat: they roost in tree canopies, forage on pavements, lawns, canal banks, and rubbish, and cache food on rooftops. Anywhere is the correct habitat class, and the dual movement of tree-flitter and urban-walker captures the between-tree hops and the deliberate pavement stalking that characterise daily crow behaviour. The bird category default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter is overridden because the ground-foraging component is too significant to omit.

Rooks (Corvus frugilegus) and carrion crows together in Platanus (plane tree) avenues are a characteristic winter sight along Amsterdam's canal streets and in parks like the Vondelpark. Rooks nest colonially in tall trees and commute to short grassland and pavements to probe for invertebrates, while carrion crows are year-round urban generalists. Anywhere correctly covers both species' promiscuous habitat use; tree-flitter and urban-walker together capture the tree-roosting and pavement-foraging that alternate throughout the day. The bird category default is overridden because the ground-foraging component is essential to both species' ecology in the city.

This entry captures a crow and magpie roosting assembly recorded in Gleditsia (honey locust) in Amsterdam. Crows (Corvus corone / C. cornix) and Eurasian magpies (Pica pica) are Amsterdam's archetypal urban corvids, equally at home perching in tree canopies and foraging on pavements, lawns, and canal banks. Anywhere is the correct habitat because neither species has a single dominant microhabitat. The dual movement of tree-flitter and urban-walker reflects the roost-to-pavement commute these birds make daily; the bird category default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter is overridden to include the ground-foraging component that corvids demonstrably express.

The sycamore lace bug is a North American tingid invasive now widespread in continental Europe and well established on London plane (Platanus) in Amsterdam. Adults and nymphs feed gregariously on the undersides of Platanus leaves, causing characteristic pale stippling; they remain on the leaf surface for extended periods and only move slowly between feeding sites. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden to tree-canopy / idle-only because this species is strictly a foliage undersurface feeder on a single host genus, with no association with flowers.

The white-spotted pinion is a noctuid moth whose caterpillar feeds inside the developing buds and leaves of elm (Ulmus), an increasingly rare host in Amsterdam following Dutch elm disease but still present in some parks and canal streets. The larva mines buds in spring and the adult rests cryptically on bark or among leaves during the day, making idle-only the correct animation for any daylight sprite. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden to tree-canopy / idle-only because the species is wholly dependent on elm foliage and has no flower-visiting stage relevant to map animation.

This combined entry covers the goat moth (Cossus cossus) and clearwing moths (Synanthedon spp.) whose larvae bore through the wood and bark of Castanea and Salix in Amsterdam. Both species spend several larval years tunnelling inside the trunk, and adults rest briefly on bark surfaces after emergence before dispersing. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden in favour of tree-bark / idle-only because the larval stage — the ecologically dominant phase — is entirely within the bark and wood, and the adult stage on-tree is also a resting posture. The goat moth is notable for the pungent goat-like odour of its larvae, which can sometimes be detected at infested willows along Amsterdam canals.

Crambus pascuella is a grass moth (family Crambidae) whose larvae feed on grass roots in park lawns, meadow margins, and grassy verges — exactly the habitat where adults are also flushed when walking through the turf. Adults rest in characteristic head-down posture on grass stems, wings rolled tight around the body like a cigarette. Ground-park is the clear dominant habitat for both larval and adult stages. The default flower-visitor / flower-bobber profile is entirely wrong; idle-only captures the crypsis-while-resting strategy correctly.

Crambus perlella is a small grass-veneer moth whose larvae develop in the roots and stems of grasses; adults rest head-downward on grass stems in parks and rough grassland, relying on cryptic patterning for camouflage. They are not flower visitors and do not forage openly, so the insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is replaced with ground-park and idle-only. Adults are active at dusk and night but spend the day completely motionless in grass. The resting posture on a grass blade is the animation target.

Crassa unitella (formerly Borkhausenia unitella) is a small oecophorid moth strongly associated with dead wood, bark debris, and dry plant litter in sheltered spots — often in and around buildings, wood stores, and dead timber in parks. Like its family relative Hofmannophila pseudospretella it rests motionless against surfaces during the day. Its larvae feed on decaying vegetable matter and dead wood rather than live flowers, so the default flower-visitor / flower-bobber assignment is inappropriate. Ground-urban with idle-only covers its low-profile, resting-adult urban presence in Amsterdam.

Hawthorn is a thorny deciduous shrub or small tree with white blossom in spring and red haws in autumn, widely planted in Amsterdam park hedgerows, cemetery borders, and scrubby urban green space. Although it can grow into a tree, it is far more frequently encountered as a shrubby hedging plant on the ground-park layer and is catalogued as a plant rather than a tree in this roster. Its dense thorny growth provides nesting cover for birds and its flowers and berries are valuable wildlife resources. The plant defaults of ground-park and none apply without modification.

Smooth Hawk's-beard is a common annual or biennial of short, grazed or mown grassland, roadside verges, and park lawns — one of the characteristic small yellow composites of Amsterdam's open green spaces. It tolerates heavy mowing and regular disturbance, persisting in the gaps between pavement slabs and along park path edges. The yellow flower heads attract small bees and hoverflies. The default ground-park / none pairing is accurate and needs no override.

The scalloped oak is a medium-sized geometrid moth whose larvae feed on the foliage of oaks, hawthorn, blackthorn, and other broadleaved trees; adults rest by day in a characteristic leaf-mimicking posture pressed against bark or among dead leaves. In Amsterdam it occurs in parks with mature trees. Tree-bark replaces the insect defaults because adults spend daylight hours motionless on bark surfaces, not visiting flowers. Idle-only is the appropriate animation for a cryptically resting moth.

This entry covers the cryptogamic crust of foliose lichens and cushion mosses colonising the bark of Ginkgo in Amsterdam. Ginkgo's rough, deeply fissured bark provides an excellent substrate for both groups, and the clean-air-sensitive lichen community in Amsterdam is increasingly diverse as air quality improves. The lichen category defaults of tree-bark and none fit perfectly: cryptogams are sessile organisms permanently adhered to the bark surface with no movement at any life stage. No override is needed.

The Water Betony moth (also known as the Striped Lychnis) is a nocturnal noctuid whose caterpillars feed on figwort and mullein in parks and rough ground. As an adult it rests motionless on vegetation or bark by day, which makes flower-visitor the wrong habitat and idle-only the correct movement over flower-bobber. The larvae are often spotted on their foodplants in summer, making the ground-park habitat the most relevant placement context. This is a moth-at-rest species and should receive the static-perch treatment in animation.

The chestnut weevil (Curculio elephas) is a long-snouted curculionid whose larvae develop inside the nuts of Castanea (sweet chestnut). The female drills into developing chestnuts using her elongated rostrum and lays eggs inside; the larvae feed within the nut and emerge in autumn to pupate in soil. Adult weevils cling to foliage and nuts in the canopy and move very slowly. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden to tree-canopy / idle-only because all activity is centred on the nut-bearing canopy, not on flowers, and the adults' positional movement is negligible at sprite scale.

Acorn weevils (Curculio glandium and the related C. elephas on oak) are classic canopy-foraging curculionids whose females use their very long curved rostrum to bore into developing acorns on Quercus to deposit eggs. Amsterdam's numerous oak-lined streets and park oaks support a resident population, most visible in late summer when adults are active on foliage and acorns. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden to tree-canopy / idle-only because the entire life cycle revolves around acorns in the canopy, and adults move only slowly across leaf and twig surfaces.

The nut weevil (Curculio nucum) is a hazel-specialist curculionid whose larvae develop inside hazelnuts (Corylus). The female drills through the nut shell with her long rostrum to lay a single egg per nut; larvae then consume the kernel. Adult beetles feed on hazel leaves and buds in the canopy during spring and are sluggish walkers, spending most of their time stationary. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden to tree-canopy / idle-only because the ecological focus is entirely on hazel nuts and foliage, and the adult's on-tree behaviour is characteristically immobile.

This entry covers the diverse community of over 100 weevil species (superfamily Curculionoidea) associated with willows (Salix) in the wider Amsterdam region, including canal-bank pollard willows and park specimens. Willow weevils include leaf-rollers, gall-formers, and bark-miners; all life stages are confined to the willow host, either on leaves and shoots in the canopy or in stems and bark. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is overridden to tree-canopy / idle-only because this guild is comprised of leaf and tissue feeders, not pollinators, and adults on willow foliage barely move at map sprite scale.

The Common Whitethroat is a summer-visiting warbler that breeds in scrubby hedgerows, bramble thickets, and rough vegetation at or near ground level, moving up into low tree canopy to sing from exposed perches. In Amsterdam it is recorded during spring migration and occasionally breeds in parks with dense scrub and overgrown edges. It is more a ground-scrub bird than a true high-canopy species, so tree-canopy is dominant for placement purposes (it perches in shrubs/low trees) with ground-park secondary for its skulking foraging behaviour. Movement is tree-flitter (hops between shrub perches) with idle-only secondary when it pauses to sing.

This entry captures Blue Tit and Great Tit observations specifically associated with hazel (Corylus) trees in Amsterdam. Both species exploit hazel catkins for seeds in late winter and systematically probe hazel twigs for aphids and moth larvae in summer. Their behaviour is identical to the main slug: rapid canopy foraging with frequent hops between branches and nearby trees. The tree-canopy habitat and tree-flitter movement defaults apply without modification. Being observed on a single Corylus genus makes no difference to how they are animated or placed.

Blue Tits and Great Tits are among the most abundant and visible birds in Amsterdam's parks and street trees. They forage actively through the canopy of deciduous trees — especially oaks, maples, lindens, and elms — picking insects and invertebrates from leaf surfaces and crevices. Both species hop rapidly between branches and flit to neighbouring trees every few seconds, making them ideal candidates for the tree-flitter animation. In Amsterdam they are present year-round and are strongly associated with the 17 genera listed in observations. The default bird tags fit perfectly without any override needed.

A broader family-level grouping (Paridae) recorded on Liriodendron, Magnolia, Malus and Ostrya in Amsterdam. Blue Tit and Great Tit are the dominant members of this family in the Netherlands and their canopy habits are consistent across all four host genera. They glean insects from foliage and buds, flitting rapidly from branch to branch and tree to tree throughout the year. The default bird tags — tree-canopy habitat and tree-flitter movement — are accurate and no override is needed for this paridae aggregate slug.

Blue tit (Cyanistes caeruleus) and great tit (Parus major) are Amsterdam's most abundant and visible small songbirds, recorded across a wide range of host trees including Aesculus, Amelanchier, Carpinus, Ilex, Robinia, and Styphnolobium. Both species are quintessential canopy foragers that move restlessly between trees in short arcing flights, perfectly matching the bird category defaults of tree-canopy and tree-flitter. They are year-round residents found in virtually every Amsterdam park, garden, and tree-lined street. No override is needed; the default tags describe this pair exactly.

Cydia illutana and related larch cone moths spend their larval stages inside larch cones and shoot tips, making them almost entirely canopy-bound on Larix trees in Amsterdam. Adults are small nocturnal tortricid moths that rest motionless on bark or foliage during the day. Because they spend their lives either concealed inside plant tissue or resting still, flower-visitor and flower-bobber defaults are completely wrong; tree-canopy with idle-only captures their actual presence on the tree without the motion they simply do not perform. They are an override from the insect default.

The codling moth is the classic apple and pear fruit borer, with larvae tunnelling inside the developing fruit of Juglans, Malus and Pyrus. In Amsterdam's urban orchards and park trees these moths are present throughout summer, but adults are cryptic night-fliers that rest immobile on bark or foliage during daylight. The larval stage is hidden entirely within fruit tissue and the adult rarely moves when at rest. Tree-canopy placement is correct since the lifecycle is centred on the fruit-bearing canopy; idle-only replaces the inappropriate flower-bobber default because there is no meaningful positional movement to animate.

Cydia splendana is a tortricid moth whose larvae bore into developing sweet chestnut nuts (Castanea) to feed on the kernel. In Amsterdam this species is observed on the relatively rare Castanea trees in parks and avenues. Adults are small, subtly patterned moths that rest immobile on bark or foliage and are almost never seen in active flight during daylight. The canopy of the host chestnut is the correct placement zone, and idle-only replaces the insect default of flower-bobber since these moths are sedentary during any visible portion of their lifecycle.

The Mute Swan is one of Amsterdam's most iconic waterbirds, a permanent resident on the canals, Amstel, and larger city ponds. It spends nearly all visible time on the water surface, drifting slowly along canal stretches or grazing submerged vegetation with its long neck (water-drifter dominant). Between foraging bouts it tucks its head under a wing and drifts motionless (idle-only secondary), matching the taxonomy note example exactly. The secondary water-edge habitat covers nest-building on canal banks and aggressive territorial patrolling on foot near the nest. Overridden from the default tree-canopy with the canonical example values from BEHAVIOR_TAXONOMY.md.

Oak gall wasps (Cynipidae) are a diverse family of tiny parasitoid wasps whose larvae develop entirely inside galls they induce on Quercus leaves, buds, catkins, acorns or roots. Amsterdam's oak trees host dozens of cynipid species producing the characteristic spherical, spangle, cherry and artichoke galls visible in parks and along streets. Adults are minute, dark wasps that are rarely visible in flight; the observable manifestation of their presence is always the stationary gall structure anchored in the canopy. Tree-canopy and idle-only therefore replace the insect defaults, as no flower-bobbing behaviour is relevant.

The Southern Marsh-orchid is a native terrestrial orchid that grows in damp, nutrient-poor grasslands, marshes, and the wet margins of ditches and canals, making water-edge the dominant habitat. It is one of the more frequent wild orchids in the Amsterdam region, found in nature reserves, damp meadow remnants, and dike sides in and around the city. Its tall spikes of purple-pink flowers appear from May to July and are important for solitary bees. Being an orchid it is non-mycorrhizal-fungus-dependent for germination, and its populations can be sensitive to drainage changes and grassland management. The ground-park secondary covers damp amenity grassland sites within parks.

Dasineura pyri is a tiny gall midge whose larvae cause the characteristic inward rolling of pear leaf margins on Pyrus trees. The insect spends its larval and pupal stages entirely hidden within the curled leaf tissue in the canopy. Adult midges are extremely small and fragile, emerging briefly in spring to mate and oviposit but otherwise imperceptible during any visible window. Tree-canopy is the correct placement since all observable signs — the rolled, discoloured leaves — are in the canopy; idle-only replaces flower-bobber as there is no animated movement meaningful at sprite scale.

The Pantaloon Bee is a striking solitary mining bee, named for the enormous pollen-collecting hairs on the females' hind legs. It nests in extensive burrows in sandy, sparsely vegetated ground — dunes, sandy path edges, and open park soils — and is among the most dramatic excavators in the bee world, kicking out visible fans of sand. Adults visit flowers for pollen and nectar but spend most of their observed time at or near their nest entrances in open ground. ground-park placement reflects nesting habitat; idle-only captures nest-entrance hovering and resting. The default flower-visitor would miss the critical ground-nesting behaviour.

Wild Carrot is a biennial umbelliffer with distinctive flat-topped white flower heads, often with a single dark purple floret at the centre, found in Amsterdam on dry, chalky or sandy verges, park meadows, and dike edges. It is the wild ancestor of the cultivated carrot, with a thin, woody, pale taproot. The compound umbel flowers from June to September and is a major resource for short-tongued insects including many hoverflies, small wasps, and beetles. When the seeds are set the umbel curls inward to form a characteristic bird's nest shape. The default ground-park placement suits the open, dry grassland and verge habitats where it is typically observed around Amsterdam.

This combined slug groups the Great Spotted Woodpecker with the Eurasian Nuthatch, both recorded on the beech (Fagus) tree in Amsterdam. The Nuthatch is a compact bird that runs head-first down trunks and along major branches probing bark crevices for insects and cached nuts — a lifestyle almost identical in placement terms to the woodpecker. Both species are bark specialists who also spend time in the canopy and make short flights between nearby trees. Tree-bark is the dominant habitat with tree-canopy as secondary; tree-flitter is the primary movement with idle-only secondary for the frequent stationary foraging postures of both species.

The Great Spotted Woodpecker is a striking black-and-white bird resident in Amsterdam's larger parks and along Betula and Cedrus avenues. It spends the great majority of its foraging time clinging to trunks and major branches, drilling for beetle larvae and extracting seeds from cones, making tree-bark the dominant habitat over the canopy default. Between foraging bouts it flies to a neighbouring tree with an undulating flight, so tree-flitter is retained as primary movement, while the long stationary drilling sessions warrant idle-only as a secondary. This pair of habitat and movement overrides distinguishes it from ordinary canopy songbirds.

Deraeocoris ruber is a predatory mirid plant bug with striking red and black patterning, common in Amsterdam parks and urban gardens on nettles, willowherb, and various shrubs where it hunts small insects and mites. Despite its name and category as a plant bug it is notably carnivorous, patrolling the upper stems and flower heads of tall herbaceous plants and low shrubs. The insect default flower-visitor / flower-bobber partially fits since it is found on flowering plants, but the dominant behaviour is predatory ambush from stems rather than nectar-foraging. Tree-canopy / idle-only captures the ambush-predator posture on stems; ground-park secondary and flower-bobber secondary reflect observations on tall herbs and flower heads.

Maiden Pink is a low-growing perennial of dry, sunny grassland, road verges, and sandy park edges, producing bright pink flowers with toothed petals in summer. It is a ground-level forb that is firmly in the ground-park category; it has no aquatic or wall associations. In Amsterdam it is found in the drier, sun-exposed corners of parks and along the edges of cycle paths and verges where soil competition is low. The plant default (ground-park, none) is the correct assignment and no override is needed.

This slug represents the same guild of armoured scales and mealybugs observed on Nyssa trees in Amsterdam. Nyssa (tupelo) is an uncommon ornamental in the city and the scales found on it behave identically to those on other genera: they are bark-anchored, sedentary sap-feeders that never move at a scale meaningful for animation. Tree-bark placement and idle-only movement are the appropriate overrides from the insect defaults of flower-visitor and flower-bobber, consistent with the diaspididae slug above.

Armoured scales (Diaspididae) and mealybugs (Pseudococcidae) are sap-sucking hemipterans that attach to bark, twigs and sometimes leaves of Celtis and Cercidiphyllum trees in Amsterdam. Armoured scales in particular are permanently sedentary once settled, secreting a waxy protective cover and spending their entire feeding life fixed to the bark surface. Mealybugs move very slowly when young but are functionally stationary at sprite scale. Tree-bark is the correct dominant habitat — not flower-visitor — and idle-only replaces flower-bobber entirely, as these insects perform no meaningful locomotion.

The Foxglove is a tall biennial or short-lived perennial with distinctive tubular purple-pink (occasionally white) flowers arranged on a long spike, poisonous throughout and the original source of digitalis heart medicines. In Amsterdam it is found in parks, woodland edges, and gardens, typically favouring slightly acidic, well-drained soils in light shade or woodland clearings. It is a striking plant popular in biodiversity planting schemes in urban green spaces. The plant defaults of ground-park and none apply without modification.

Perennial Wall-rocket is a yellow-flowered crucifer with a strong, pungent smell that is characteristic of disturbed urban ground — railway ballast, cracked pavements, ruined walls, and rubble. It is a classic urban coloniser rather than a park plant, thriving where soil is thin, nutrient-poor, and well-drained. In Amsterdam it appears in pavement cracks, along fences, and on the edges of cycle paths. ground-urban overrides the default ground-park because this species actively prefers built-up, disturbed substrates over vegetated park soil. Movement is none.

Pine sawflies are hymenopteran larvae that feed gregariously on pine needles (Pinus) in tight clusters, often stripping entire branches in Amsterdam's park pines. The larvae are the most conspicuous life stage — bright green or yellowish caterpillar-like grubs sitting motionless in rows on needle bundles. Adult sawflies are short-lived, unobtrusive and rarely visible. The entire observable lifecycle occurs within the tree canopy on needles and shoots; idle-only captures the gregarious sedentary larval clusters, replacing the insect defaults of flower-visitor and flower-bobber which are entirely inappropriate.

Small Teasel is a biennial herb that favours shaded, moist ground at woodland edges, along canal banks, and in park margins throughout the Netherlands. Unlike the common teasel it prefers shadier, damper situations and is locally scarce. As a non-tree vascular plant it takes the plant category default of ground-park and none, which accurately describes its placement at the base of hedges and park edges. No override is needed.

This aggregate slug covers the diverse community of flies, bees, wasps, beetles and other insects observed visiting hawthorn (Crataegus) blossom in Amsterdam. Crataegus is a major urban flowering shrub and small tree whose heavy white-scented blooms in May attract a wide range of pollinating insects. The assemblage — hoverflies, solitary bees, bumblebees, beetles — all exhibit classic flower-visitor behaviour, moving from bloom to bloom within and between trees. The insect defaults of flower-visitor habitat and flower-bobber movement fit this group perfectly and no override is required.

The red-barred tortrix is a small leaf-rolling moth whose larvae feed inside rolled leaves of a wide range of broadleaved shrubs and trees including Prunus, Rosa, and Crataegus; adults rest flat against foliage or bark in the tree canopy during the day. It is not a flower-forager, so the insect defaults of flower-visitor and flower-bobber are replaced with tree-canopy and idle-only, which reflect where adults are reliably found in Amsterdam parks. The species is polyphagous on woody plants, making tree-canopy a stable dominant habitat.

The lesser stag beetle is a saproxylic beetle whose larvae develop inside the rotting heartwood of dead or dying ash (Fraxinus) stumps and roots in Amsterdam's parks. Adults are chunky, black beetles that are most often found clinging motionless to dead wood or stumps at ground level and on lower trunks. Tree-bark is the dominant habitat because the beetle is tightly associated with decaying wood surfaces, with ground-park as a secondary since adults are frequently found on the soil surface near decaying roots. Idle-only replaces flower-bobber as these beetles are essentially sedentary at the scale relevant to the animation system.

The sycamore aphid is one of the most common and visible aphids on Acer (maple) trees throughout Amsterdam, forming dense colonies on the undersides of leaves from spring through autumn. Like all aphids it is essentially stationary once feeding — individuals sit immobile piercing leaf veins and exuding honeydew that drips onto pavements below. Tree-canopy is the correct placement since colonies occupy the leaf surfaces; idle-only replaces the insect default of flower-bobber because the aphid performs no flower-visiting or meaningful locomotion at sprite scale.

The Oriental chestnut gall wasp is an invasive cynipid from East Asia now established in parts of the Netherlands, including Amsterdam where it attacks sweet chestnut (Castanea). It induces distinctive green-and-red galls on buds, leaves and catkins in the canopy, where the larva develops entirely concealed inside the gall tissue. Adults emerge in summer as tiny, inconspicuous wasps that mate and oviposit rapidly before dying; the visible manifestation is always the stationary gall in the canopy. Tree-canopy placement and idle-only movement override the irrelevant insect defaults.

Dysaphis plantaginea (rosy apple aphid) and Aphis pomi (green apple aphid) are two common aphid species that colonise apple (Malus) shoots and leaves in Amsterdam's parks and urban gardens. Rosy apple aphid causes characteristic leaf curling and distortion while green apple aphid forms dense colonies on young shoots; both species are sedentary feeders that remain stationary on the plant tissue. Tree-canopy captures their presence in the leafy zone of the host apple tree, and idle-only replaces the default flower-bobber since neither species visits flowers or moves meaningfully at animation scale.

Dysaphis pyri and its close relative D. plantaginea are pear-specialising aphids observed on Pyrus trees in Amsterdam. They colonise leaf undersides and shoot tips from spring, causing the leaves to curl and redden in characteristic clusters that make colonies easy to spot in the canopy. These aphids are sedentary feeders — once settled on a feeding site they remain essentially stationary, piercing the phloem and extracting sap. Tree-canopy and idle-only are the correct overrides from the insect defaults, consistent with all other aphid slugs in this batch.

This aggregate covers aphid species — primarily Dysaphis sorbi and woolly aphids (Eriosoma) — recorded on rowan and whitebeam (Sorbus) trees in Amsterdam. Dysaphis sorbi colonises leaf undersides producing leaf curls, while Eriosoma species create the distinctive fluffy white woolly masses on bark and shoots. Both are sedentary feeders anchored to the plant surface; woolly aphids can occupy either bark or canopy zones but canopy is dominant for the observable leaf-curling species. Tree-canopy and idle-only override the insect defaults, as with all other aphid slugs in this batch.

Viper's-bugloss is a biennial wildflower found on dry, disturbed ground in Amsterdam — roadsides, railway embankments, former industrial sites, and sandy park edges — where it forms striking colonies of vivid blue flower spikes in summer. It is a calcareous or sandy-soil specialist and is not particularly common in the city centre, though it appears reliably along the fringes of Amsterdamse Bos and on disturbed substrates near the ring roads. As a non-aquatic vascular plant rooted in the ground it fits the plant category default of ground-park / none exactly. It is also one of the most important nectar plants for bumblebees and specialist solitary bees in Amsterdam, though that is a property of its visitors, not of the plant itself.

The birch shieldbug is a medium-sized green and brown shieldbug strongly associated with birch (Betula) trees, where adults and nymphs feed on developing catkins and seeds in the canopy. In Amsterdam it is found in parks with mature birch trees, typically sitting immobile on leaves or bark during the day. Although adults can fly, they are rarely seen in sustained flight and spend most of their visible time resting or slowly feeding. Tree-canopy is the correct dominant habitat over the insect default flower-visitor, and idle-only replaces flower-bobber as the shieldbug's typical demeanour is thoroughly sedentary.

Elatobium abietinum (green spruce aphid) and Cinara pilicornis (large spruce aphid) are two major aphid pests of Norway spruce (Picea) found in Amsterdam's parks and avenues. Elatobium colonises the older needles of lower branches in dense, inconspicuous colonies that cause yellowing and premature needle drop; Cinara forms larger visible clusters on young shoots. Both are sedentary needle-feeders that move negligibly at sprite scale. Tree-canopy (the needle zones of the spruce crown) and idle-only are the correct overrides from the insect flower-visitor defaults.

The Common Plume is a plume moth (Pterophoridae) whose larvae feed on Field Bindweed and related Convolvulaceae. Adults rest with wings rolled into a characteristic T-shape on stems of their host plant or nearby fencing, and are common wherever bindweed grows in Amsterdam — roadsides, garden fences, rough ground, and park margins. It can be found year-round as adults overwinter. The flower-visitor default does not apply; habitat overridden to ground-park with ground-urban secondary to reflect its close association with urban weed habitats; movement overridden to idle-only.

Enarmonia formosana (cherry bark tortrix) larvae bore into the bark and cambium of Prunus trees, creating characteristic gummy entry wounds on trunks and main branches. Argyresthia pruniella larvae mine cherry fruitlets. Both moths rest motionless on bark surfaces during the day. The dominant habitat for this pairing is tree-bark rather than canopy because the most diagnostic and damaging life stage — the bark-boring larva — is anchored to the trunk surface. Idle-only replaces flower-bobber; adults are nocturnal and the observable bark-damage signs are completely stationary.

This aggregate covers moths in Geometridae and Notodontidae — including the spectacular Kentish glory (Endromis versicolora) — whose larvae feed on birch (Betula) foliage in Amsterdam. Geometrid larvae are the classic "inchworm" caterpillars that rest on twigs mimicking small twigs; notodontid larvae also rest on foliage with characteristic postures. Adults of both families are large, cryptically patterned moths that rest motionless on bark or foliage during daylight. Tree-canopy is the dominant habitat for the leaf-feeding larval stage, and idle-only captures the characteristic still-resting behaviour of both larvae and adults.

Enoplognatha ovata and the closely-related E. latimana are small comb-footed spiders (Theridiidae) that build small tangled webs on flower heads, bramble, and low shrubs — they are almost diagnostic of flower and seed heads in summer and are frequently photographed on umbellifers and thistles. Flower-visitor is the appropriate habitat override from the arachnid default of anywhere, since the spider genuinely lives on or near blooms to intercept visiting insects. It stays put in its web, so idle-only movement is correct and unchanged. In Amsterdam these spiders are common in park margins, rough grassland, and hedgerows throughout July and August.

Great Willowherb is a tall, robust perennial herb that grows almost exclusively in wet places — canal banks, ditch margins, pond edges, and other permanently damp or waterlogged ground throughout Amsterdam. Its strong association with water margins means the plant default of ground-park is replaced with water-edge, which better describes where it is reliably found in the city. The species spreads vigorously by rhizomes along canal banks and is one of the most conspicuous bankside plants in summer. Movement remains none as it is a static plant.

The Broad-leaved Helleborine is a native orchid that has made a surprising adaptation to urban life, colonising shaded parks, pavement cracks under tree cover, and even car parks. In Amsterdam it is regularly recorded in Vondelpark and other shaded public green spaces, often growing directly beneath old trees. It produces nectar with mild narcotic properties that intoxicates visiting wasps, its primary pollinators. Despite its orchid family, it does not require specialist mycorrhizal conditions in the urban setting and spreads readily. Default ground-park and none tags fit.

The Marmalade Hoverfly is the most abundant and recognisable hoverfly in Amsterdam, present from early spring right through to autumn wherever flowers are open. Adults are dedicated nectar and pollen feeders, visiting umbellifers, open-faced composites, and a wide range of ornamental flowers in parks and canal-side plantings. They hover with the characteristic stationary precision of syrphids before dropping onto a bloom, then bob to the next nearby flower — a perfect match for the flower-bobber class. It is explicitly named in the taxonomy as an example of the flower-visitor / flower-bobber default and the insect category default fits exactly.

Canadian Fleabane is a North American annual introduced to Europe in the 17th century and now a common weed of disturbed, dry, and often paved urban ground. It thrives in pavement cracks, rubble, and railway ballast and is rarely found in manicured park lawns. Its tiny white flowers appear from mid-summer and produce masses of wind-dispersed seeds. In Amsterdam it is a quintessential ground-urban species, familiar on streets and footpaths. The override from ground-park to ground-urban reflects its strong preference for hard-standing and impoverished urban substrate over vegetated park soil.

Eriophyes tiliae is the mite responsible for the distinctive nail-like or pouch-like galls (Aceria-type ceratonea or unguicula forms) that project from the upper surface of linden (Tilia) leaves throughout Amsterdam. These mites are invisible to the naked eye and spend their entire lives within the gall tissue or on leaf surfaces. The visible feature — the red or green nail galls dotting the leaf — is firmly within the canopy of Tilia street trees that line most of Amsterdam's boulevards. Tree-canopy overrides the arachnid default of anywhere, while idle-only is retained as movement.

Eriophyes gall mites are microscopic arachnids observed on hawthorn (Crataegus) in Amsterdam, where they induce various leaf-surface galls and erinea (felt-like patches of abnormal leaf hairs). These mites are far too small to see individually; their presence is indicated by the visible gall structures on leaves in the canopy. The arachnid default of anywhere and idle-only is partially correct in movement but wrong in habitat — tree-canopy is far more specific and accurate for these strictly leaf-surface mites. Habitat is overridden from anywhere to tree-canopy; movement idle-only is retained from the default.

The Stripe-winged Drone Fly is a medium-sized hoverfly in the genus Cheilosia / Eristalis complex, regularly seen visiting umbellifers, thistles, and other open flowers in parks and along water edges. Adults are strong flyers that move actively between flower heads to feed on pollen and nectar, fitting the flower-visitor habitat and flower-bobber movement class precisely. Its larval stage is aquatic (rat-tailed maggot in stagnant water), but adult observations — which are what the creature simulation will show — are firmly on blooms. The insect defaults apply perfectly and no override is needed.

The Common Drone Fly is a robust, honeybee-mimicking hoverfly found throughout Amsterdam from early spring to late autumn, abundant wherever open flowers are available in parks, garden strips, and canal-side plantings. Its larvae are aquatic "rat-tailed maggots" that develop in stagnant, oxygen-poor water such as polluted ditches and canal margins — but the adults that Amsterdam observers record are the flower-visiting forms living above ground. Adults hover at flowers, feed on pollen and nectar, and move between blooms with short directional flights matching the flower-bobber pattern. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber fits correctly.

The lime aphid is among the most abundant and noticeable aphids in Amsterdam, forming dense colonies on the undersides of linden (Tilia) leaves from late spring through summer. It produces copious honeydew that falls as sticky rain on cars and pavements beneath linden avenues — a well-known nuisance in the city. Colonies are stationary; individual aphids pierce the leaf phloem and feed without moving for extended periods. Tree-canopy is the exact correct placement for this canopy leaf-feeder, and idle-only replaces the insect default flower-bobber as no pollinating or flower-visiting behaviour occurs.

Euceraphis betulae is a large, elegant aphid found on silver birch (Betula) trees in Amsterdam's parks and along birch-lined paths. Unlike many aphids it tends to occur in scattered individuals rather than dense colonies, but it is still a sedentary canopy feeder that sits immobile on leaf undersides extracting sap. Adults are winged but flights are brief dispersal events rather than sustained visible movement. Tree-canopy captures the leaf-surface foraging zone correctly, and idle-only replaces the inappropriate insect defaults of flower-visitor and flower-bobber.

Eudonia delunella is a small crambid moth whose larvae feed on mosses and liverworts growing on old walls, stone surfaces, and rooftops in urban areas — it is effectively a wall-specialist. The adult rests cryptically on the same mossy wall surfaces during the day. This is a genuine wall-and-roof organism; Amsterdam's older brick canal-side buildings, bridges, and stone walls provide ideal habitat. The default flower-visitor / flower-bobber insect pairing is completely wrong here; wall-and-roof with idle-only captures the species' biology accurately.

Eudonia lacustrata is a small crambid moth whose larvae feed on mosses and algae growing on tree bark, old walls, and shaded stone surfaces. Adults rest motionless on these same mossy surfaces during the day, blending into the texture. In Amsterdam they are recorded from shaded park trees, cemetery walls, and old brick buildings where moss has established. The flower-visitor/flower-bobber default does not apply at all; habitat overridden to tree-bark (dominant) with wall-and-roof secondary; movement overridden to idle-only.

Hemp-agrimony is a tall perennial herb that grows almost exclusively in wet habitats — canal banks, pond margins, fens, and damp ditches. In Amsterdam it is a characteristic plant of water-edge environments and is one of the most important late-summer nectar sources for butterflies and bumblebees. Water-edge replaces the plant default of ground-park to reflect its obligate preference for damp, waterside ground. Movement remains none as it is a static plant.

The Migrant Aphideater is a medium-sized hoverfly with broad yellow abdominal bands, one of the most abundant syrphids in Amsterdam parks and gardens during summer where its larvae prey on aphid colonies on shrubs and trees. Adults are avid nectar feeders, hovering beside umbellifers, composites, and flowering shrubs, making short precise moves between bloom clusters. As a migrant it crosses substantial distances on clear days, appearing suddenly in great numbers in late summer when southerly winds bring individuals from continental Europe. The insect default flower-visitor / flower-bobber fits well; a secondary sky-looper is added to reflect its long migratory transit flights, and tree-canopy captures the larval aphid-colony habitat.

The Cabbage Bug is a brightly patterned shieldbug (Pentatomidae) that feeds on brassicaceous plants, piercing stems, leaves, and seeds of wild and cultivated cabbages, hedge mustard, and related plants. In Amsterdam it is found on ruderal patches, road verges, allotments, and park margins wherever crucifers grow. Adults and nymphs move slowly between host plant stems and spend much of their time stationary. The flower-visitor default does not apply; habitat overridden to ground-park with ground-urban secondary; movement overridden to idle-only.

The Peregrine Falcon nests on ledges of tall Amsterdam buildings and church towers, most famously the Westertoren and structures in the port area, making wall-and-roof the dominant placement habitat. From these high perches it launches into powerful stooping dives over open city sky, justifying sky-only as a secondary habitat. Sky-looper is the dominant movement class for the long soaring and hunting circuits it flies over open water and parks; idle-only is secondary for the extended periods it perches motionless on a building edge scanning for prey.

Fascista cercerisella is a small gracillariid moth whose larvae fold and tie the leaves of Cercis (Judas tree) in Amsterdam. The aggregate also covers the broader community of some 10–19 Lepidoptera larval species associated with this ornamental tree. All these larvae feed and shelter within folded or mined leaf tissue in the canopy, and adults are inconspicuous micro-moths that rest motionless on foliage or bark. Tree-canopy is the correct habitat for this leaf-feeding guild; idle-only replaces flower-bobber, as none of these species exhibit flower-visiting behaviour as their dominant trait.

The Purple hairstreak is an oak-specialist butterfly whose adults spend nearly their entire lives in the canopy of Quercus trees, rarely descending to ground level. In Amsterdam they are tied to street-side and park oaks, where they feed on honeydew secreted by aphids rather than seeking flowers. Their flight is a short fluttering movement among the upper branches, making flower-bobber the closest animation analogue despite the non-flower food source. The override from the insect default reflects their strict canopy residency and lack of interest in ground-level flowers.

Fomitopsis betulina (formerly Piptoporus betulinus) is a bracket fungus exclusive to birch trees, forming large kidney-shaped fruiting bodies on the bark of Betula trunks in Amsterdam parks and cemeteries. The Mycetophilidae fungus gnats listed here breed inside the decaying bracket, living out their larval stages within the sporocarp tissue — effectively co-located with the fungus on bark. As a fungal organism the category default of tree-bark / none applies cleanly, with the gnat association considered incidental to placement. The bracket is strictly non-motile and the static sprite is appropriate.

The common earwig is a nocturnal generalist that shelters under loose bark on urban trees such as Parrotia, Paulownia and Pyrus, then forages at night on the ground among leaf litter and low vegetation in Amsterdam parks and gardens. Its movement is below the animation threshold — earwigs creep very slowly and shelter motionlessly during daylight hours, fitting idle-only. The override from the insect default (flower-visitor / flower-bobber) is warranted because earwigs are bark-sheltering detritivores rather than flower pollinators, with a secondary ground-park tag reflecting night-time foraging.

Red wood ants build large mound nests at the base of Pinus trees and forage extensively over the ground in parks and woodland patches in and around Amsterdam. While they do ascend pine trunks and canopy to tend aphids and harvest resin, their dominant placement is the ground-park mound and foraging trails, making ground-park the primary habitat. The park-roamer animation captures their constant ground movement, with idle-only as secondary for workers motionless on the mound surface. The override reflects their ground-dominant ecology, distinct from canopy-tending ants.

This entry covers wood ants and sugar-tending Formicidae observed on Acer trees in Amsterdam, primarily tending aphid colonies in the canopy for honeydew. Workers travel from ground nests up into the canopy and back, so both tree-canopy (dominant — where they are visible tending aphids) and ground-park (where nests and foraging trails are found) apply. Their movement on the tree surface is a slow crawl below the animation threshold, making idle-only appropriate. The override from the insect default reflects their canopy-tending ecology rather than flower-visiting.

This entry captures ants (exemplified by Lasius niger) observed specifically in the canopy of Liriodendron tulip trees, tending aphid colonies and harvesting honeydew. Their placement is tree-canopy because the observational context is on-tree honeydew feeding rather than ground nesting. On the trunk and branch surfaces they move slowly enough to be rendered as idle-only. The override from the insect default reflects a tree-canopy microhabitat association rather than the flower-visitor default for insects.

Frankia alni is an actinobacterium that forms nitrogen-fixing root nodules on Alnus (alder) trees, which line many Amsterdam canals and parks. The organism is entirely endosymbiotic within root tissue — invisible above ground but inseparable from the host tree's root zone. Placement at the tree coordinate with tree-rooted is the most accurate representation available in the vocabulary, though the organism technically lives below ground. Movement is none since bacteria are rendered as static sprites. The override from the other default (anywhere / idle-only) pins it correctly to its host tree.

The Ash is one of Europe's most important broadleaved trees, historically widespread in Dutch parks, cemeteries, and streets. It is a fast-growing, light-demanding species with distinctive compound leaves and winged seeds (keys) that persist through winter.
