Amsterdam's ash population is under severe pressure from Ash Dieback (Hymenoscyphus fraxineus), a fungal disease causing crown dieback and mortality across the city. The GBIF category in the batch CSV is listed as "plant," but Fraxinus excelsior is unambiguously a tree; category is overridden to tree with tree-rooted placement and none movement.

Chaffinch and Brambling are common finches in Amsterdam's beech-dominated parks, observed feeding in Fagus canopy and descending in flocks to forage on fallen beech mast on the ground below. The dual habitat reflects this well-documented alternation: canopy for singing, roosting and invertebrate gleaning, ground-park for seed foraging under the trees in autumn and winter. Accordingly tree-flitter is dominant but park-roamer captures the ground-feeding flocks. The override is warranted because these species demonstrably split time between canopy and ground more than a default small passerine would.

This entry groups chaffinch, goldfinch, greenfinch, siskin and various Sylvia warblers commonly observed in Chamaecyparis conifers in Amsterdam parks and cemeteries. All are classic small songbirds that forage and shelter within tree canopies and hop briskly between branches and neighbouring trees. The default bird tags of tree-canopy and tree-flitter fit this diverse grouping exactly. These species form the core of the bird default category in the taxonomy.

The Eurasian Coot is a defining waterbird of Amsterdam's canal and pond network, spending most of its time swimming and diving on open water across the city's parks and urban waterways. It regularly hauls out onto bank lawns and grassy verges to graze, making ground-park a genuine second habitat. On water the coot drifts with canal currents and paddles steadily, while on land it walks with the lurching gait characteristic of rail family members. The species is highly visible year-round and nests in emergent vegetation along water edges. The secondary park-roamer movement captures its frequent bank grazing, following the Coot example given explicitly in the taxonomy.

Lady's Bedstraw is a low-growing perennial herb of dry, calcareous grassland, road verges, and sunny park margins; in Amsterdam it occurs mainly on sandy or gravelly ground in parks and along cycle-path edges. It produces clusters of small yellow flowers from June to September. The plant default of ground-park and none fits without modification. No override is needed.

The Common Moorhen is a rail family bird abundant along Amsterdam's canals, in park ponds, and in any vegetated water margin across the city. Unlike the Coot it is more secretive and tied to emergent vegetation at the water's edge rather than open water, so water-edge is the dominant habitat. However it also ventures well into adjacent park lawns and verges to pick for food, making ground-park a genuine second habitat. Its movement along canal banks is a slow, nodding walk characteristic of water-edge-stalker, while its forays onto grass are covered by park-roamer. The default bird tag of tree-canopy / tree-flitter is entirely wrong for this ground-and-water species.

The Eurasian Jay is one of Amsterdam's most characteristic park birds, strongly associated with Quercus, Aesculus, Juglans and Pterocarya — all mast-producing trees from which it harvests and caches nuts. Jays are conspicuous in the canopy during foraging bouts but spend substantial time on the ground in parks searching for cached acorns or gleaning fallen nuts, making a dual habitat tag essential. Tree-flitter captures their bold canopy hops between adjacent trees, while park-roamer reflects their deliberate ground walks between caching sites. The override from the bird default is necessary given the jay's distinctly bimodal canopy-ground lifestyle.

Gasteruption wasps are slender, long-necked parasitoid wasps whose adults are among the more distinctive flower visitors on umbellifer blooms — they are regularly seen on Daucus, Anthriscus, and Angelica in Amsterdam parks and canal banks. Their larvae parasitise solitary bees nesting in dead wood or hollow stems. Adults move between flower heads with deliberate, hovering approaches (flower-bobber), and spend long periods stationed on a flower head with the abdomen curved beneath (idle-only). The insect default of flower-visitor/flower-bobber is an excellent fit; no override needed.

This entry covers geometrid and noctuid moth caterpillars that feed on Acer, Picea, Tilia and Zelkova foliage in Amsterdam street trees and parks. As larvae they are sedentary leaf-feeders clinging to foliage, making tree-canopy and idle-only the accurate tags. Adult moths are nocturnal and seldom visible during daytime simulation hours, so the caterpillar life stage dominates the placement logic. The override from the insect default (flower-visitor / flower-bobber) reflects their strict leaf-eating caterpillar ecology and lack of flower-visiting behaviour.

Dove's-foot Crane's-bill is a softly hairy annual or biennial with small pink flowers, found on short grassland, lawns, and path edges throughout Amsterdam's parks and green spaces. It tolerates mowing and compacted soil, making it a familiar sight on amenity turf alongside daisies and clovers. The deeply divided leaves and pink petals are characteristic even in depauperate specimens. It is an important early-season nectar plant for small bees. Default ground-park and none are correct.

Meadow Crane's-bill is a handsome perennial with large violet-blue flowers and deeply divided leaves, typical of Amsterdam's parks, cemetery verges, and damp meadow remnants. It flowers from June to September and is an important nectar source for bumblebees and other long-tongued insects. The beak-like seed capsules that give cranesbills their name eject seeds explosively when ripe. It prefers calcareous or neutral, moderately fertile soils, and in Amsterdam it is most often found in the richer sward of older parklands and maintained verges. The default ground-park placement is entirely appropriate.

Herb-Robert is a small, strongly scented annual or biennial cranesbill that thrives in shaded, disturbed ground: it is a familiar sight at the base of walls, in cemetery corners, under hedges, and in the deep shade beneath canal-side trees in Amsterdam. It colonises gaps in tree-root zones and base-of-wall rubble (hence tree-bark as a secondary habitat — it literally grows pressed against wall and bark surfaces). The delicate pink flowers attract small solitary bees in summer. Ground-park is the dominant placement as it needs soil, but the secondary tree-bark/wall association is biologically real and visually distinctive.

Wood Avens is a common perennial of shaded and semi-shaded park margins, woodland edges, canal-side hedgerows, and damp spots throughout Amsterdam, named after its urban tolerance (the species epithet urbanum). Its small yellow flowers appear from late spring and are followed by hooked burr-like fruits that attach to animal fur and human clothing for dispersal. The plant grows readily in the shadow of park shrub layers and under canal-side trees, making it one of the most reliably-observed ground herbs in Amsterdam. The default ground-park / none applies without modification.

Spruce sawflies (Gilpinia hercyniae and relatives) are specialist defoliators of Picea, with larvae feeding gregariously on spruce needles in the canopy. In Amsterdam, isolated ornamental Picea in parks and gardens can host these sawflies, though outbreaks are rare in urban settings. The larvae are essentially sedentary on foliage and move only to adjacent needle clusters, making idle-only the correct movement tag. The override from the insect default places them in tree-canopy rather than as flower-visitors since they are strict conifer foliage specialists.

Ground-ivy is a low, mat-forming perennial of shaded and semi-shaded ground in parks, under hedges, and along woodland edges; it is extremely common across Amsterdam's parks and canal-side green spaces. The creeping stems root at nodes and form extensive patches in moist, nutrient-rich soil. The plant defaults of ground-park and none describe its placement accurately without any override needed.

The mulberry leaf-webber is a crambid moth whose larvae spin silk webs around Morus foliage, feeding within the webbed nest in the canopy. Amsterdam has a small but growing population of Morus trees as ornamentals and this specialist moth has been noted on them. Larvae are entirely canopy-resident and sedentary within their web shelters, making idle-only the correct movement. The override from the insect default is warranted: this is a leaf-webbing tree specialist, not a flower-visitor.

The Box Bug is a medium-sized squash bug (Coreidae) strongly associated with box (Buxus sempervirens) but also feeding on hawthorn, dogwood, and other woody shrubs in parks and gardens. It has expanded its range northward in recent decades and is now regularly recorded in Amsterdam on ornamental shrubs, hedge plantings, and park shrubbery. Adults and nymphs sit motionless on stems and leaf surfaces, feeding by piercing the plant tissue. Habitat overridden to tree-canopy (dominant, for its shrub/low-tree association) with ground-park secondary; movement overridden to idle-only.

Gracillariid and nepticulid leaf-mining moths have larvae that tunnel between the upper and lower epidermis of Ostrya leaves, leaving characteristic blotch or linear mines visible to observers. Ostrya carpinifolia (hop-hornbeam) is a relatively rare street tree in Amsterdam but does appear in specialist plantings where these micromoths follow. The mining larvae are entirely immobile within the leaf tissue, making idle-only definitive. The override from the insect default correctly anchors them to tree-canopy foliage rather than flowers.

The striped shield bug is a boldly red-and-black patterned pentatomid bug that feeds on the developing seeds of Apiaceae (umbellifers) such as wild carrot, hogweed, and ground elder; it is almost invariably found perched on umbellifer flower heads in parks and canal-side vegetation. Flower-visitor is the dominant habitat because of its tight association with flowering umbellifers. Adults are slow-moving and spend extended periods stationary on flower heads, making idle-only the appropriate animation over the insect default of flower-bobber.

The beech blight aphid forms large, densely packed colonies on the bark of Taxodium and beech trunks, preferring the lower trunk and major branches rather than the leafy canopy tips. Their conspicuous white woolly wax and clustering behaviour make them highly visible on bark surfaces. In Amsterdam, Taxodium distichum appears in park and canal-edge plantings where this aphid can establish. Because colonies sit on the bark surface rather than within foliage, tree-bark is more accurate than tree-canopy, distinguishing them from leaf-feeding species. They are sessile and idle-only.

The double-striped pug is a small geometrid moth with two generations per year; larvae feed on the flowers and seed capsules of a wide range of plants including gorse, heather, and various herbaceous species. Adults rest motionless by day on foliage and bark, blending with the background. In Amsterdam it is recorded in parks and gardens with diverse shrubby vegetation. Tree-canopy and idle-only replace the insect defaults because adults spend daylight hours in cryptic rest on vegetation, not actively visiting flowers.

The Eurasian Oystercatcher is an increasingly common urban breeding bird in Amsterdam, famously nesting on flat rooftops across the city as well as at water edges along the IJ, the Amstel, and canal margins. In Amsterdam it is as often seen striding along canal towpaths, flat rooftop surfaces, and paved waterfronts as it is at the natural water edge — ground-urban is a genuine secondary habitat in this city specifically. Its movement is a deliberate patrol walk along linear features matching water-edge-stalker, and on urban surfaces it uses the same stride as urban-walker. The distinctive orange bill and piping alarm call make it one of Amsterdam's most identifiable urban waterbirds. The default bird tag of tree-canopy / tree-flitter is entirely wrong.

The aspen hoverfly is a Red Data Book hoverfly whose larvae develop in wet decaying wood of Populus (aspen and related poplars), particularly in sap-runs beneath the bark. Adults emerge to visit flowers for pollen and nectar, making them a dual-habitat organism: the larval stage is tree-bark anchored while adults are flower-visitors. In Amsterdam they are associated with older Populus trees in parks with some decay. Tree-bark is dominant because the breeding microhabitat drives placement, with flower-visitor secondary for adult feeding. Accordingly idle-only (larvae in bark) dominates, with flower-bobber for adults.

The Harlequin Ladybird is an invasive Asian ladybird that has become the commonest ladybird in Amsterdam, found on trees, shrubs, and herbaceous plants throughout the city wherever aphid colonies occur. The f. spectabilis form has a black background with red spots, one of the many colour morphs of this extraordinarily variable species. Adults are voracious aphid predators that move through the canopy following aphid populations, making tree-canopy the dominant habitat and tree-flitter the primary movement. Flower-visitor is added as secondary habitat because adults also aggregate on flowering plants in late summer before overwintering in building crevices; idle-only covers the extended sunny perching periods.

Harmonia axyridis f. succinea is one of the pale colour forms of the Harlequin Ladybird — biologically identical to the nominate form, differing only in elytral patterning. Its ecology in Amsterdam is the same: an aphid predator found abundantly in the canopies of linden, lime, and other deciduous street trees, moving slowly through foliage between aphid colonies. It gathers on walls, window frames, and tree bark in autumn for overwintering aggregations. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is wrong for this tree-canopy aphid predator, for the same reasons as the nominate form. Tree-canopy and idle-only are the correct tags.

The Harlequin Ladybird is the dominant ladybird species in Amsterdam, introduced from Asia and now found abundantly on linden, lime, and other deciduous street trees where aphid colonies provide its primary food source. Adults spend most of their active season clambering slowly through foliage and along twigs in tree canopies, pausing frequently — movement is too slow and non-directional to qualify as tree-flitter, so idle-only with positional drifting better represents the sprite animation. In autumn large aggregations gather on walls, window frames, and tree trunks to overwinter. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is wrong; this is an aphid predator of tree canopy, not a flower visitor.

The Marbled Orchard Tortrix is a small tortricid moth whose larvae are leaf-rollers and bud-borers on apple, pear, hawthorn, and other rosaceous trees, making tree-canopy the clear dominant habitat. Adults rest by day on bark and leaves with characteristic tortrix wing posture (roof-like), relying on their marbled grey-and-white pattern for camouflage against lichen-covered bark. The default insect placement of flower-visitor is incorrect for a species entirely dependent on tree foliage and buds. Adults fly in June and July; larvae overwinter as first instars in bark crevices. Amsterdam observations come from orchard gardens, hawthorn-lined avenues, and parks with mature rosaceous trees.

Hedya salicella is a tortricid leaf-roller moth whose larvae feed on willows, alders, and birch — all trees common along Amsterdam's canal banks and in city parks. Adults are white with a characteristic dark-flecked forewing pattern and are typically encountered resting on leaves or bark in sheltered spots during the day. Willows overhanging canals are a primary habitat, making tree-canopy the most accurate placement. The default flower-visitor tag does not apply; this is a bark-and-foliage resting moth. idle-only reflects daytime motionless resting behaviour.

The Common Emerald is a delicate pale-green geometrid moth that rests with wings fully spread flat against leaves, making it almost invisible against summer foliage in Amsterdam parks and wooded canal margins. Larvae feed on a wide variety of broadleaf trees and shrubs — oak, hawthorn, bramble, and others — so adults are most likely found near mature canopy trees. It flies at night and comes readily to light traps; during the day it is completely still on a leaf surface. The default flower-visitor / flower-bobber is wrong for a resting foliage moth: tree-canopy / idle-only fits the adult resting behaviour, with ground-park as secondary for individuals found resting on low shrub vegetation.

Giant Hogweed is a massive invasive umbellifer from the Caucasus that reaches 3–5 metres in height, established along Amsterdam's canal banks, park margins, and river-adjacent wasteground where it dominates the herbaceous layer. Its huge white flower plates in June–July attract extraordinary numbers of insects — beetles, hoverflies, wasps, and flies — making individual plants temporary biodiversity hotspots. Contact with its sap in sunlight causes severe phytophotodermatitis and the plant is subject to active management programmes in the Netherlands. Ground-park is the dominant habitat; water-edge is a genuine secondary because it colonises canal banks and water-adjacent disturbed ground preferentially.

Hogweed is a tall, robust umbellifer of roadsides, river banks, and park margins, producing large flat-topped white flower heads that are among Amsterdam's most important insect refuelling stations in early summer. It is a ground-level herbaceous plant, firmly ground-park, with no canopy or aquatic associations. Despite its value as a flower-visitor magnet it is itself a plant and carries the standard plant tags. The plant default (ground-park, none) is the exact right assignment.

The Large-headed Resin Bee is a small, cavity-nesting solitary bee found in Amsterdam's parks and gardens wherever composite flowers — particularly black-eyed Susan, cornflower, and other Asteraceae — are present to provide pollen. It collects pollen almost exclusively from composites and is highly flower-faithful, visiting the same flower patches repeatedly in short bobbing flights between blooms. It nests in pre-existing cavities in dead wood and hollow plant stems, placing resin plugs between cells — cavity nesting is placement-equivalent to tree-canopy per the taxonomy, but the observable adult behaviour is pure flower-visitor / flower-bobber. The insect category default fits correctly.

Heterarthrus and Fenusa are leaf-mining sawflies whose larvae feed between the leaf layers of Betula, creating blotch mines that turn leaves brown by late summer. Birch trees are among the most common urban trees in Amsterdam and these sawflies are widespread on them. As with all leaf-miners, the larvae are sessile within the mine and idle-only is the correct movement class. The override from the insect default anchors them correctly to tree-canopy rather than the flower-visitor default.

This record represents an unidentified moth (Heterocera is a informal grouping of non-butterfly Lepidoptera) and cannot be assigned to a specific ecological niche. Most moths recorded in Amsterdam parks rest on vegetation or bark during the day and are inactive unless disturbed. ground-park is the most conservative and broadly applicable placement for an unidentified moth in a park context. idle-only reflects the characteristic daytime resting posture shared by the vast majority of moth species. Flower-visitor and flower-bobber defaults are inappropriate without species-level identification.

The Nettle Ground Bug is a small ground bug (Heterogastridae) that feeds almost exclusively on the seeds of Common Nettle, spending most of its life clustered at the base of nettle patches or among fallen seed heads on the soil surface. In Amsterdam it is found wherever nettles grow — park edges, allotment borders, canal banks, and rough urban ground. Adults and nymphs move only short distances and spend most time stationary. The flower-visitor default does not apply; habitat overridden to ground-park with ground-urban secondary; movement overridden to idle-only.

The barn swallow is an aerial insectivore that spends almost all daylight hours in continuous flight, hawking insects over open water, parks, and farmland; it rarely perches in open view and never forages on the ground or in tree canopies. In Amsterdam it is a common summer visitor seen sweeping low over canals and open park spaces. Sky-only and sky-looper replace the bird defaults of tree-canopy and tree-flitter entirely, as this species is functionally synonymous with aerial, never-landing movement in the simulation context.

The Brown House Moth is a synanthropic moth that lives almost exclusively indoors or in sheltered urban structures, colonising stored organic material, wool, feathers, and dried foodstuffs. Its adult stage is short-lived and rests motionless on walls and ceilings during the day, making it effectively an urban-interior species rather than a flower-visitor. The default insect flower-visitor / flower-bobber pairing is biologically wrong here: this moth has no meaningful relationship with flowers and is never found in open park vegetation. Ground-urban with idle-only best captures its wall-resting adult behaviour in Amsterdam's dense built environment.

Yorkshire-fog is a softly hairy perennial grass that is extremely common in Amsterdam's parks, meadow strips, and roadside verges. Its pinkish-green flower heads and distinctly velvety leaves make it identifiable even from a distance. It tolerates a wide range of soil conditions and is one of the most frequently recorded grasses in urban green space across the Netherlands. It provides cover and nesting material for small invertebrates. Default ground-park and none are appropriate.

This entry captures the assemblage of honeybees and various flies observed feeding on aphid honeydew secreted onto Juglans foliage, rather than visiting flowers directly. In Amsterdam walnut trees in parks produce sticky honeydew-coated leaves that attract Apis mellifera and calyptrate flies in large numbers. The habitat is firmly tree-canopy (honeydew is collected on foliage and branches) rather than flower-visitor despite honeybees being classic pollinators. Flower-bobber is the closest movement analogue as the insects flit between sticky leaf clusters in a similar pattern to flower-to-flower visits. The override reflects the atypical non-floral food source driving their canopy placement.

Liquidambar styraciflua (sweetgum) produces extrafloral nectaries on its stems and petioles that attract honeybees and hoverflies seeking sugar outside of the flowering period. This Amsterdam street tree is increasingly planted as a climate-adapted species and its EFNs are actively exploited by pollinators visiting the canopy zone. The habitat is tree-canopy because nectary secretions occur on foliage and young stems rather than flowers, but flower-bobber movement fits the short flit-and-feed pattern these insects show between EFN sites. The override distinguishes this non-flower canopy foraging from the standard flower-visitor default.

The Uncertain is a common noctuid moth found across urban Amsterdam, active at night and resting motionless on bark, fence posts, and rough vegetation surfaces by day. Its cryptic grey-brown patterning provides excellent camouflage against bark and lichen-covered surfaces. Adults fly from June to August, coming readily to light traps, which accounts for many Amsterdam observations. The larvae feed on low-growing herbaceous plants including docks and plantains in parks and verges. Placing it on tree bark captures the typical daytime resting posture that observers actually photograph.

Wall Barley is an annual grass of disturbed, nutrient-rich urban ground: pavement cracks, road verges, bases of walls, and waste places throughout Amsterdam. Its common name reflects its preference for urban habitats rather than managed park grassland, so ground-urban replaces the plant default of ground-park. The species is one of the most characteristic plants of Amsterdam's streets and is particularly abundant along canal-side pavements. Movement remains none.

The common yellow-face bee is a small, wasp-like solitary bee that visits a wide range of flowers for nectar and pollen, which it transports internally rather than in pollen baskets. It is one of the most frequently recorded small bees in Amsterdam parks and gardens. Nests are built in hollow stems, old beetle borings in wood, and similar narrow cavities. The insect default of flower-visitor and flower-bobber matches its observed foraging behaviour well. No override is needed.

Hylesinus and Leperisinus fraxini are bark beetles that bore into the bark and cambium of Fraxinus ash trees to breed, creating distinctive D-shaped exit holes and gallery systems. Amsterdam has extensive street plantings of Fraxinus excelsior and F. pennsylvanica, and these bark beetles (along with the ash dieback pathogen Hymenoscyphus fraxineus) are significant threats to the urban ash stock. Adults are short-lived and rarely seen in flight; placement on tree bark is definitive and movement is idle-only for the sedentary bark-boring life stage. The override from insect default is necessary given their strict bark-dwelling ecology.

Perforate St John's-wort is an upright perennial herb of open, sunny ground — found on dry banks, roadsides, park margins, and rough grassland throughout Amsterdam. Its bright yellow five-petalled flowers, which open around midsummer (historically around St John's Day, 24 June), are visited by a wide range of bees and hoverflies. The leaves have characteristic translucent oil glands visible when held to the light, giving the "perforatum" name. The plant-default ground-park placement is correct; this species does not associate strongly with water edges or wall surfaces.

Cat's-ear is a common rosette-forming perennial of park lawns, meadow edges, and grassy path margins throughout Amsterdam. Its dandelion-like yellow flowers on branched stems are a reliable summer sight in Vondelpark and the Amsterdamse Bos. The default ground-park / none placement fits exactly: it grows in well-established grassy sward and does not favour bare pavement. The solid rosette and branched flowering stem would make a clear sprite for this species.

Ichneumon xanthorius is a parasitoid wasp that parasitises moth pupae; adults are commonly found on umbellifer flowers in parks and verges where they feed on nectar to fuel flight. Unlike the moths in this batch the adult does visit flowers as an energy source, warranting flower-visitor as a secondary habitat. Ground-park is dominant because the wasp hunts low in park vegetation for host pupae rather than being canopy-tied; idle-only is dominant movement (it perches and searches methodically) with flower-bobber secondary during nectar-foraging bouts.

The Riband Wave is a small geometrid moth common in Amsterdam's parks, hedgerows, and tree-lined streets, where adults rest by day on bark, wooden fences, and shaded leaf litter blending in with their subtly patterned pale wings. Its larvae feed on low herbs including dock, dandelion, and similar plants found abundantly in Amsterdam's verges and park understorey. The moth does not feed at flowers — it takes no adult nectar and its flight activity is entirely nocturnal and triggered by darkness rather than bloom. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber does not fit; tree-bark (dominant daytime resting surface in urban Amsterdam) and idle-only reflect its real ecology.

The Dwarf Cream Wave is a tiny pale geometrid moth whose larvae feed on low herbaceous plants and possibly algae on bark in damp, shaded habitats. Adults rest with wings flat against bark or dead wood in parks and garden edges. Like other small wave moths, the flower-visitor / flower-bobber defaults are inappropriate; ground-park is dominant for the larval foodplant context and tree-bark covers adult resting sites. Idle-only is the correct movement as this moth is cryptically still by day and only moves at night.

The Least Carpet is a small geometrid moth whose pale straw-and-brown wings make it near-invisible when resting against old masonry, bark, or dry plant stems in summer. In Amsterdam it inhabits warm, sun-exposed urban habitats — old walls, railway cuttings, and overgrown urban lots — where its larval foodplants (Galium, Rumex, and low herbs) grow. Resting adults are exclusively idle-only, sitting flattened against surfaces during the day. Wall-and-roof is the dominant habitat because it is most often found pressed against old brick and stone; ground-park is secondary for the larval foodplant zone. Overridden from the default flower-visitor/flower-bobber as this moth does not feed as an adult.

The Small Dusty Wave is a small geometrid moth whose larvae feed on dead and decaying plant matter, algae, and lichens on walls, fences, and bark. Adults rest by day on lichen-covered walls, wooden fences, and the bark of trees where their pale dusty pattern provides camouflage. The default flower-visitor / flower-bobber profile is completely wrong for a resting moth; ground-park is retained as a secondary for larvae in leaf-litter, while wall-and-roof or tree-bark would be equally valid for adults. Idle-only is the correct movement class.

Illinoia liriodendri is an aphid specialist on Liriodendron tulipifera (tulip tree) and Magnolia, feeding on the undersides of leaves in the canopy and excreting copious honeydew. Both Liriodendron and Magnolia appear in Amsterdam parks and as ornamental street trees, making this aphid a genuine urban Amsterdam species. Like all sedentary phloem-feeding aphids, it is idle-only in movement terms — colonies sit motionless on leaf undersides. The override from the insect default correctly places it in tree-canopy as a leaf-feeding specialist rather than a flower-visitor.

Himalayan Balsam is a tall, fast-growing annual from the Himalayas that has naturalised aggressively along Amsterdam's canal edges, river banks, and ditch margins. It forms dense stands up to two metres tall that shade out native waterside vegetation. The large pink-purple flowers are a rich nectar source for bumblebees from July to October. Its explosive seed pods are distinctive and audible in late summer. water-edge overrides the default ground-park because this species is almost exclusively a riparian coloniser, rooted at the margins of water bodies. Movement is none.

Small Balsam is an annual herb introduced from Central Asia that has become one of the most widespread invasive plants in Amsterdam's parks, woodland understories, and shaded canal banks. It grows rapidly in moist, shaded soil and produces abundant small pale yellow flowers from summer to autumn, explosively ejecting seeds on touch. Unlike the taller Indian Balsam, this species tolerates deeper shade and drier conditions. The plant defaults of ground-park and none apply without modification.

Ips typographus is the most destructive spruce bark beetle in Europe, boring through the outer bark of Picea to lay eggs and construct breeding galleries in the phloem. In Amsterdam, ornamental Picea trees in parks can be colonised particularly when weakened by drought, and the beetle has expanded its range under climate warming. The organism lives its entire productive life on and beneath the bark surface — tree-bark is the definitive habitat. Movement is idle-only as the beetle is visible only as an exit-hole cluster on bark; the boring activity is below the animation threshold. The override is necessary given the bark-dwelling lifestyle versus the insect flower-visitor default.

The Common Bluetail is the most abundant and ubiquitous damselfly in Amsterdam, found along virtually any canal, pond, or slow-moving ditch with emergent vegetation. Adults perch on waterside plant stems — the distinctive blue tail tip making males easy to spot — then make short hunting flights over the water surface. Water-edge is the dominant habitat as they cling to reed stems and bank vegetation; water-surface captures their patrolling flights just above the canal. The idle-only movement represents the characteristically long, motionless perching bouts between hunts. Larvae are aquatic predators that develop over one or two seasons in submerged vegetation. Adults fly from May to September.

Issus coleoptratus is a small, squat planthopper in the family Issidae that feeds on phloem sap from the stems and bark of ivy, elder, and various deciduous shrubs and trees. Its mottled brown-grey coloration is cryptic against bark surfaces, where it rests and feeds, making tree-bark the dominant habitat. It moves very little when disturbed, relying on camouflage rather than flight, which makes idle-only the correct movement class. The default insect habitat of flower-visitor does not apply as this species is entirely bark- and stem-associated throughout its life. Amsterdam observations are typically found on ivy-clad walls and elder bushes in parks and gardens.

Dune Ragwort is a subspecies of Common Ragwort adapted to dry, sandy soils — historically restricted to coastal dunes but increasingly found in Amsterdam on the sandy embankments, rail-side verges, and park edges derived from old dune material. Its bright yellow composite flower heads are an important late-summer nectar source for the Cinnabar moth and various pollinators. The default ground-park / none pairing holds; it is a rooted perennial of open ground that needs no habitat override.

Common Ragwort is a biennial to short-lived perennial with bright yellow daisy-like flowers that is widespread on park margins, railway embankments, sandy verges, and unmown grassland across Amsterdam. It is the obligate foodplant of the orange-and-black Cinnabar moth caterpillar, making it an ecologically important species despite being legally controlled in some contexts. The ground-park default fits accurately; the plant needs open, often disturbed or nutrient-poor ground in vegetated settings.

Jadera haematoloma is a seed-feeding rhopalid bug originally from North America that has become established in Europe wherever its host Koelreuteria paniculata (goldenrain tree) is planted. In Amsterdam, Koelreuteria is used as an ornamental street and park tree and Jadera aggregates in large numbers on fallen seed pods beneath the trees, feeding on seeds at ground level. Ground-park is the dominant habitat because the bugs feed on fallen seeds under the tree rather than in the canopy. They are sedentary seed-suckers and idle-only in movement terms. The override moves them from the insect flower-visitor default to reflect their ground seed-feeding ecology.

Prickly Lettuce is a tall annual or biennial composite with small pale yellow flowerheads and prickly leaves that orient themselves north-south to minimise afternoon sun exposure. It is a classic urban ruderal, abundantly colonising roadsides, building edges, rubble, pavement cracks, and disturbed ground across Amsterdam. The wild ancestor of cultivated lettuce, it thrives on nutrient-rich disturbed soils in full sun. The dominant habitat is ground-urban due to its strong preference for built surfaces and rubble, with ground-park secondary; movement stays none.

Lagria beetles are soft-bodied, somewhat hairy beetles in the family Tenebrionidae, found as adults on flowers, foliage, and stems of herbaceous plants and low shrubs in Amsterdam parks and rough grassland from late spring onwards. Adults feed on pollen and plant tissue and are commonly observed on umbellifer and composite flower heads, making flower-visitor the appropriate dominant habitat. Larvae live in soil and leaf litter feeding on decaying plant material, so ground-park is a secondary. The flower-bobber primary movement fits adult flower-foraging; idle-only secondary covers the prolonged resting periods on stems between flower visits.

The poplar hawk moth is a large sphingid moth whose larvae feed exclusively on Populus and Salix foliage in the canopy, resting motionless during the day on branches or leaves with their cryptic bark-grey wings. Adults are crepuscular and nocturnal, flying over tree canopies in long looping paths to locate mates and host trees. Tree-canopy is the dominant habitat for both larval feeding and adult daytime roosting, making it the correct anchor. Idle-only is dominant (capturing the motionless daytime resting posture that would be the primary visible state), with sky-looper secondary for nocturnal adult flight. The override from the insect default is necessary as this is a tree-canopy specialist rather than a flower-visitor.

Nipplewort is a slender annual composite that favours shaded and semi-shaded ground: the bases of park hedges, woodland edges, the dappled floor beneath canal-side trees, and shaded garden corners throughout Amsterdam. Its small pale-yellow flowerheads are easily overlooked but consistent through summer. The default ground-park placement is accurate, as it needs vegetated shaded soil rather than bare paved ground. It is often found growing alongside goosegrass and nettles in the shadier corners of Vondelpark.

The European Herring Gull is one of Amsterdam's most visible large birds, nesting on flat rooftops across the city and foraging opportunistically on pavements, canal edges, and open water. Its vocal breeding colonies on urban rooftops make wall-and-roof a genuine secondary habitat, while anywhere captures its true generalist nature. Adults are powerful fliers, regularly soaring over the city and IJ waterfront, earning sky-looper as the dominant movement. On the ground it struts confidently along streets and market squares scavenging scraps, well represented by urban-walker. Observations peak in spring and summer when rooftop colonies are active and noisy.

The Lesser Black-backed Gull is a large, highly adaptable gull that breeds on Amsterdam rooftops and forages across the entire urban fabric — canals, parks, streets, markets, and rubbish sites — making it one of the city's most genuinely omnipresent birds. It soars on thermals above open water and city squares, and descends to walk and peck on pavements and waterfront areas with the characteristic pause-and-stride gait of larger gulls. The default bird tag of tree-canopy / tree-flitter is entirely inappropriate; anywhere captures its urban-generalist nature, with water-surface as a secondary reflecting regular loafing on the IJ and canal surfaces. Sky-looper covers its aerial searching, urban-walker its ground-foraging.

Halictid sweat bees are small ground-nesting solitary bees observed visiting Gleditsia (honey locust) flowers in Amsterdam, a tree common along streets and in parks. While adults visit flowers for pollen and nectar — fitting the flower-visitor default — females also nest in bare or sparsely vegetated ground in parks, requiring a secondary ground-park tag. Flower-bobber is the dominant movement for foraging adults, with park-roamer secondary for ground-level nest-searching and nest approach behaviour. The override adds the ground-park secondary to the otherwise default-fitting insect tags.

Lasius niger (black garden ant) is the most ubiquitous ant in Amsterdam, nesting in pavement cracks, wall bases, and garden soil across the city, and observed on a diverse set of host trees including Laburnum, Styphnolobium, Taxodium, Thuja, Tilia and Zelkova. Workers forage across paved and unpaved surfaces, making them a genuine dual-habitat insect: ground-urban for their typical pavement-foraging and nest-siting, ground-park for green space foraging. Urban-walker is the dominant movement (workers patrolling pavements), with park-roamer for grass and park foraging trails. The six-tree host list reflects their generalism rather than tree-canopy specificity, so an override from the insect default is correct.

This entry covers Lasius and Formica ants observed specifically on the extrafloral nectaries of Ailanthus altissima (tree of heaven) and Liquidambar styraciflua in Amsterdam. Both trees are widespread urban species — Ailanthus is abundant on disturbed urban ground and Liquidambar is increasingly planted as a climate-adaptive street tree. Ants exploit the sugar secretions on stems and leaf petioles within the canopy zone rather than at ground level, making tree-canopy the correct dominant habitat. Their crawling on bark and stems is below animation threshold, so idle-only applies. The override is needed because EFN-tending ants are canopy-anchored, not flower-visitors.

This entry captures agromyzid fly larvae and various micro-moth caterpillars that produce leaf mines in Laburnum foliage, visible as pale blotch or linear trails on the leaflets. Laburnum anagyroides and L. x watereri are planted as ornamental trees in Amsterdam parks and private gardens, and leaf-miners frequently exploit them. As with all leaf-mining insects, larvae are entirely immobile within the mine tissue and idle-only is definitive. The override from the insect default correctly places them in tree-canopy rather than flower-visitor.

These hawthorn-feeding Lepidoptera caterpillars (lappet, lackey, vapourer, light emerald) live and feed directly on the foliage and bark of Crataegus trees growing in Amsterdam parks and street plantings. The larvae cling to branches and leaves, barely moving once settled on a feeding site, making idle-only the correct animation class. Tree-canopy is dominant because most species feed on foliage; tree-bark is added as secondary because egg batches and resting pupae are anchored to the bark surface. In Amsterdam these species are occasional visitors on ornamental hawthorn rows, most visible in spring and early summer when the caterpillars are active.

This catch-all entry covers rare or incidental geometrid loopers and generalist caterpillars observed on Ginkgo and other Amsterdam street trees. These larvae are leaf-miners or free-feeding defoliators that spend nearly all their larval life anchored to foliage, inching very slowly and spending long periods motionless — hence idle-only movement. Tree-canopy is the sole habitat because the caterpillars feed in the leaf layer rather than on bark. In Amsterdam Ginkgo biloba is a popular ornamental street tree and records of loopers on it are rare and opportunistic.

This entry covers four caterpillar species observed on Corylus, Fagus, Fraxinus and Nyssa in Amsterdam parks: Colocasia coryli (Nut-tree Tussock), Geometra papilionaria (Large Emerald), Plagodis pulveraria (Barred Umber) and Asthena albulata (Small White Wave). All four are foliage-feeding larvae that cling to leaves or rest motionlessly on branches during the day, perfectly matching tree-canopy and idle-only. Their cryptic resting behaviour means they are essentially static sprites in the canopy. The override from the insect default is required because these are caterpillar-stage defoliators, not flower-visitors.

The Speckled Bush-cricket is a small, wingless katydid that lives almost entirely in dense shrub and low tree foliage, where its vivid green colour provides excellent camouflage. It does not visit flowers and is not a ground forager; its entire life — feeding on leaves and small insects, singing, mating — takes place within canopy and hedgerow vegetation. Tree-canopy is therefore the appropriate habitat override from the insect default of flower-visitor. It moves very little and only slowly between leaf surfaces, so idle-only is a better movement class than flower-bobber. It is a common late-summer species in Amsterdam parks and gardens.

The European hare (Lepus europaeus) and roe deer (Capreolus capreolus) are occasional browsers on young laburnum shoots and bark in Amsterdam's larger green areas such as the Amsterdamse Bos. Both are ground-dwelling mammals that range through open parkland and semi-wild green zones in search of woody browse. The park-roamer movement class fits perfectly: they walk and pause within vegetated polygons, pausing to feed. Roe deer are rare inside the ring-road but hares are regularly recorded in the Bos and city-edge parkland.

The Obscure Wainscot is a noctuid moth whose larvae are specialist feeders on Common Reed (Phragmites australis), boring into the stems, making it tightly associated with reedbeds and canal margins throughout Amsterdam. Adults are straw-coloured, cryptically matching reed stems, and rest immobile on reed stalks during the day. The flower-visitor/flower-bobber defaults do not apply at all; habitat overridden to water-edge (dominant, for its reedbed association) with ground-park secondary for migration stops; movement overridden to idle-only.

Oxeye daisy is a perennial herb of open grassland, park meadow areas, road verges, and sunny banks throughout Amsterdam. Its conspicuous white-rayed flowers are valuable for pollinators in early summer. The plant default of ground-park and none describes its placement accurately. No override is needed.

Limnephilus marmoratus is a caddisfly (order Trichoptera) whose larvae are entirely aquatic, building portable cases from plant fragments in still or slow-moving water — Amsterdam's canals and ponds are ideal habitat. Adults are moth-like and nocturnal, resting during the day on waterside vegetation, tree trunks, or walls near water (idle-only as adults). Habitat is overridden from the insect default: water-edge is dominant (adults rest at water margins; larvae emerge there) with water-body secondary for the larval aquatic phase. The flower-visitor default is inappropriate for a caddisfly with non-feeding adults.

Common Toadflax is a slender perennial with pale yellow snapdragon-like flowers that colonises dry, disturbed, and open ground throughout Amsterdam — roadsides, railway embankments, waste ground, and park margins. It spreads by both seed and rhizome and tolerates poor, calcareous soils, making it equally common on urban rubble and in sandy park edges. Ground-park is dominant but ground-urban is a genuine secondary because it is conspicuously found on pavement edges, railway track ballast, and urban brownfield sites. The plant default of none movement is correct.

Lobarion and Graphidion are epiphytic lichen communities that colonise the smooth to furrowed bark of mature Fraxinus (ash) and other broadleaved trees in damp, well-lit urban parks. In Amsterdam these crusts and foliose thalli are found on older park trees with high air-quality scores, particularly in quieter canal-side and cemetery settings. They are entirely static — no movement whatsoever — and fully bound to the bark surface where they grow. The lichen category default of tree-bark + none fits perfectly and no override is needed.

The hawthorn leaf beetle is a small chrysomelid that feeds exclusively on Crataegus foliage both as larva and adult, making tree-canopy its clear dominant habitat in Amsterdam's park and street hawthorn plantings. Adults are slow walkers across leaf surfaces; they sit motionless for long periods and do not take meaningful positional flight between trees often enough to warrant tree-flitter. Idle-only correctly captures this almost stationary leaf-surface existence. Populations in Amsterdam track closely with ornamental hawthorn availability and are most visible from late spring through summer.

The Clouded Border is a geometrid moth with distinctive white wings boldly edged and blotched with dark brown, closely associated with willows and poplars — both abundant along Amsterdam's canals. Adults rest during the day with wings spread flat against leaves or bark (idle-only), and are remarkably well-camouflaged against dappled light through willow canopy. Larvae feed on Salix and Populus foliage in the tree canopy, making tree-canopy the dominant habitat with tree-bark as secondary for resting adult placement. Overridden from flower-visitor default as this is a foliage-dependent moth with no nectar-feeding adult stage.

Common Bird's-foot-trefoil is a low-growing perennial clover-relative with bright yellow-and-orange flowers that is widespread on dry, short-grass areas in Amsterdam's parks, dune edges, and unmown road verges. Its common name refers to the claw-shaped seed pods that resemble a bird's foot. The default ground-park placement fits well: it grows in open grassy sward on well-drained soils and is an important nectar plant for bumblebees and the foodplant of Common Blue butterfly larvae.

Greater Bird's-foot-trefoil is a larger, more robust relative of common bird's-foot-trefoil that is strongly associated with wet, marshy habitats — it grows at pond edges, canal banks, wet meadow margins, and in damp ditches. Unlike the common species it is rarely found in dry grassland. Water-edge replaces the plant default of ground-park to reflect its preference for permanently damp to waterlogged bankside ground in Amsterdam. Movement remains none.

The Common Crossbill is an irruptive finch that descends on Amsterdam in invasion years to exploit the cone crops of Larix and Pinus in the Amsterdamse Bos and Vondelpark conifers. It spends virtually all its time in the upper canopy, twisting cones open with its crossed bill, and moves between nearby conifers in short darting flights — a classic tree-flitter pattern. The default bird tags of tree-canopy and tree-flitter are an excellent fit. Crossbills are irregular visitors: absent in most years, then suddenly present in noisy flocks when continental cone crops fail.

The Eurasian otter (Lutra lutra) and water vole (Arvicola amphibius) are semi-aquatic mammals strongly tied to Amsterdam's canals, ditches, and waterway edges lined with alder. Otters hunt in the water body itself but emerge and patrol along canal banks, while water voles build burrows in earthen water-edges and forage at the bank. Water-edge is dominant for placement since both are most visually present at the bank interface; water-body is added because otters swim and hunt below the surface. Movement alternates between bank-patrolling (water-edge-stalker) and in-water swimming (water-drifter).

The spotted lanternfly is an invasive planthopper native to Asia that feeds on phloem sap of Ailanthus altissima (tree of heaven) and other woody plants. In areas where it has established, nymphs and adults spend most time pressed flat against bark surfaces sucking sap, only briefly hopping to new positions. Tree-bark is dominant since this is where they congregate in mass; tree-canopy is secondary for adults that feed on foliage tips. Idle-only matches their characteristic motionless sap-feeding posture. Not yet established in Amsterdam but listed here as a potential invasive on the city's Ailanthus street trees.

Gypsy moth larvae are voracious defoliators of Ostrya and many other broadleaved trees, feeding openly on leaves in the tree canopy during the day and descending to the bark to hide at night. In Amsterdam the species is a notifiable pest; outbreaks on hop-hornbeam are rare but significant defoliation events. Tree-canopy is dominant because feeding and most visible life stages occur in the foliage; tree-bark is secondary for the nocturnal bark-resting behaviour. The caterpillars barely move during feeding bouts — idle-only correctly captures this suspended-on-leaves animation.

Purple Loosestrife is a tall, magenta-flowered wetland perennial that lines the margins of Amsterdam's canals, ponds, and ditches, thriving with its roots in saturated or seasonally flooded soil. It is one of the most visually striking waterside plants in late summer and a major nectar source for bumblebees. The default ground-park placement is too dry; water-edge is the biologically correct dominant habitat. Ground-park serves as secondary for populations on damp park meadow edges away from open water.

Hawkmoths such as the hummingbird hawk-moth (Macroglossum stellatarum) and other sphingids are powerful hovering pollinators that visit the trumpet flowers of Catalpa and other nectar-rich plants in Amsterdam gardens and parks. While feeding they hover motionless at blooms — flower-bobber — before making fast direct transit flights — sky-looper — between flower patches. Flower-visitor is the dominant habitat because their activity is tightly coupled to blooms; tree-canopy is the secondary resting habitat for adults that roost by day in foliage. In Amsterdam they are mainly summer migrants and passage visitors, most frequent in warm years.

The honey locust leafhopper is a specialist sap-sucker on Gleditsia triacanthos foliage, a tree increasingly planted as a street tree in Amsterdam's newer districts. Adults and nymphs sit on the undersides of leaflets, piercing phloem and barely moving during long feeding bouts. Tree-canopy is the sole relevant habitat since the entire life cycle plays out in the leaf layer. Idle-only fits because leafhoppers are virtually motionless while feeding, only making small sideways jumps when disturbed — below the threshold for a distinct movement animation.

Magnoliopsida is the class of dicotyledonous flowering plants — a broad taxonomic grouping encompassing the majority of herbaceous and woody non-monocot plants observed in Amsterdam. As a catch-all class entry rather than a single species, it serves as a placeholder for observations that were not identified to a finer level. In a simulation context it is best treated as a generic ground-park forb, stationary by definition. No override is needed since the category default (ground-park, none) is the safest and most representative choice for the group.

The forest tent caterpillar is a North American lasiocampid whose Dutch presence is limited to ornamental Nyssa (tupelo) plantings; it would be an introduced curiosity rather than an established pest. Like other Malacosoma species, the larvae feed communally in foliage and rest on bark in silken mats. Tree-canopy is dominant for the feeding stage; tree-bark is secondary for the resting silk mats. The caterpillars are slow-moving leaf-feeders best represented by idle-only. Any Amsterdam observation would be exceptional and likely on an isolated ornamental tree.

The lackey moth is a native European lasiocampid whose gregarious caterpillars spin communal silk tents on Cornus, Prunus, and other woody shrubs and trees found in Amsterdam parks and hedgerows. Larvae feed on foliage and are most conspicuous as large silken colonies on branches — tree-canopy dominant — but the egg rings and resting clusters wrap bark — tree-bark secondary. Adult moths are nocturnal and short-lived; the conspicuous life stage is the caterpillar, which barely migrates from its feeding branch, making idle-only the right animation class.

Musk-mallow is a perennial herb of grassy verges, meadow edges, and park clearings, recognised by its deeply cut leaves and pale pink flowers with a faint musky scent. It is a ground-level plant with no canopy, wall, or aquatic associations, and its attractive flowers are important nectar sources for bumblebees and hoverflies. The plant default (ground-park, none) applies directly and no override is needed. In Amsterdam it occurs in meadow-style park plantings and along road verges in outer districts.

Common Mallow cultivars are ornamental selections of the native Malva sylvestris, grown for their showy purple-veined flowers in public plantings, garden borders, and park beds across Amsterdam. The species itself also naturalises on rough ground and roadsides. Cultivated forms tend to remain closer to planted park and garden settings. Flowers are visited by a range of bees, particularly common carder bees. Default ground-park and none apply; the cultivar tag indicates horticultural rather than wild provenance.

Common Mallow is a robust, deep-rooted biennial or short-lived perennial typical of Amsterdam's urban margins: roadsides, pavement bases, waste ground, and sunny wall bases. Its large pink-purple veined flowers are attractive to bees and produced prolifically from June through October. Ground-urban better captures its preference for the disturbed, ruderal habitats of the built environment rather than managed parkland. The edible leaves and medicinal reputation have kept it near human settlements throughout history. Amsterdam observations cluster along sunny south-facing road edges and in community garden surrounds where soil disturbance is frequent.

The Meadow Brown is the commonest grassland butterfly in Amsterdam parks, nectaring on knapweed, bramble, and other open flowers through the long summer flight period. Its larvae feed on fine grasses in unmown park margins and verges. The default flower-visitor / flower-bobber pairing fits well; ground-park secondary reflects the grass-feeding larval stage and the time adults spend resting with wings closed on grass stems between nectar visits. It is reliably seen in Vondelpark, the Plantage, and the Amsterdamse Bos from June through August.

The Gadwall is a subtly plumaged dabbling duck — the male grey-brown with a black rear, the female resembling a small Mallard — that has become a regular breeding bird on Amsterdam's larger water bodies and canal-fringed parks. It forages by dabbling and up-ending for aquatic vegetation on the water surface (water-drifter dominant) and rests on the bank or emergent vegetation at the water edge (idle-only secondary with water-edge secondary habitat). It is a quieter, less urbanised duck than the Mallard and rarely begs on pavements, so no ground-urban tag is warranted. Overridden from tree-canopy default as it is entirely water-associated.

The Fencepost Jumping Spider is a flat-bodied salticid specialized for life on sun-warmed vertical wooden surfaces — fence posts, boardwalks, and tree bark. In Amsterdam it is most often found on the timber structures of park fences and on the coarser-barked trees such as oak and ash. Its cryptic mottled brown pattern blends perfectly with weathered wood. Tree-bark is the dominant habitat; wall-and-roof covers its preference for man-made timber surfaces. Like all jumping spiders it sits in ambush rather than moving continuously, fitting idle-only.

The pine marten is a highly agile arboreal mustelid that climbs and leaps through the canopy of Pinus and Sorbus in woodland parks, using branches as travel routes and hunting sites. In Amsterdam it is an extreme rarity; occasional records come from the Amsterdamse Bos where mature pines exist. Tree-canopy is the dominant habitat because martens spend more time aloft than on the ground; ground-park is secondary for nightly terrestrial hunting forays. Movement alternates between tree-flitter (leaping between canopy trees) and park-roamer (ground-level hunting at night).

Meconema bush-crickets (most likely Meconema thalassinum, the Oak Bush-cricket, in Amsterdam) are slender, pale-green katydids that live almost exclusively in the canopy of broadleaved trees — oak, lime, ash, and hazel — where they hunt other invertebrates at night. By day they rest motionless among leaves, making tree-canopy the obvious dominant habitat and idle-only the correct movement class. The flower-visitor / flower-bobber insect default does not apply; this is a predatory tree-canopy insect rather than a pollinator.

Black Medick is a low-growing annual or short-lived perennial legume of lawns, path edges, and grassy open areas, producing small yellow clover-like flower heads that are a useful nectar source for bees. It is a ground-hugging plant that thrives in disturbed, nutrient-poor soils — classic of park margins, verges, and trampled grassland throughout Amsterdam. The plant default (ground-park, none) is the appropriate assignment; no override is needed.

Alfalfa is a perennial legume naturalised on dry, nitrogen-poor soils along roadsides, railway banks, and open park margins in Amsterdam, where it persists as a relic of historical cultivation and spontaneous colonisation. Its purple-blue flowers are particularly attractive to long-tongued bumblebees that trigger the explosive pollen-release mechanism. The plant fixes atmospheric nitrogen through root nodules, enriching impoverished urban soils. The default ground-park / none assignment is correct for this rooted herb.

This invasive bruchid seed weevil develops entirely inside the pods and seeds of Gleditsia triacanthos, completing its larval stage concealed within the bean-like fruits hanging in the tree canopy. Adults emerge in summer, mate on the pods, and lay eggs — all without leaving the tree crown. Tree-canopy is the sole relevant habitat since the insect's visible stages (adults on pods) occur there. Idle-only fits because adults are slow-moving and spend most time resting on pods rather than flying. The species is now widespread in Dutch cities wherever honey-locust is planted as a street tree.

The Asian giant resin bee is the largest megachilid in Europe, an invasive species now established in the Netherlands that collects resin and pollen from flowering trees including Styphnolobium japonicum (Japanese pagoda tree), commonly planted as a street tree in Amsterdam. Like other leafcutter bees it is a dedicated flower-visitor and its movement between blooms is best represented by flower-bobber. Its large size makes it conspicuous while hovering at flowers. It nests in pre-existing cavities in wood or walls, but its observable activity is entirely at the flower level.

Leafcutter bees (Megachile spp.) are specialist pollinators that carry pollen on their abdominal scopae rather than corbiculae, visiting a range of flowers in parks and urban gardens. Females are characteristic for cutting neat discs from rose and other smooth leaves to line their nest cells in hollow stems and bee hotels. The default flower-visitor / flower-bobber combination fits perfectly, with a sky-looper secondary for the between-patch transit flights that are visible in Amsterdam's community gardens and park flower beds. Several Megachile species are regularly recorded in central Amsterdam.

Willughby's leafcutter bee is a large, robust solitary bee that visits a wide range of flowers for pollen and nectar, and is particularly attracted to Fabaceae and various garden plants. Females cut neat semicircular sections from leaves to line their nest cells, which are built in hollow plant stems or soil cavities. In Amsterdam it is regularly observed in parks and gardens throughout summer. The insect default of flower-visitor and flower-bobber fits the species' foraging behaviour well, and no override is needed.

Leafcutter bees (Megachile) are solitary bees that visit flowers for nectar and pollen while also cutting neat discs from leaves — notably from Cercis siliquastrum (Judas tree) in Amsterdam parks and city gardens — to line their nest cells. They are strongly tied to flowering plants as their food source, making flower-visitor the dominant and sufficient habitat class. The flower-bobber movement class captures their characteristic short bobbing flights between blooms. In Amsterdam leafcutter bees are common visitors to garden flowers and the ornamental Cercis trees that bloom in late spring.

The Eurasian badger is a nocturnal omnivore that digs setts in earthen banks and forages across parkland and woodland edges for earthworms, roots, and fruit — including fallen Prunus plums and cherries. In Amsterdam badgers occur at the city fringe, particularly in the Amsterdamse Bos where sett systems persist in the sandy wooded banks. Ground-park is the right habitat since their entire above-ground activity is on vegetated soil. Park-roamer fits their slow nocturnal shuffle across park polygons, pausing frequently to root in the ground.

White Melilot is a tall biennial legume of disturbed ground, waste lots, railway embankments, and open park margins throughout Amsterdam. Its strongly honey-scented white flowers are an important late-summer nectar source for bees, and the plant fixes atmospheric nitrogen through its root nodules. Like its yellow-flowered relative Melilotus officinalis it colonises open, nutrient-poor soils quickly after disturbance. The default ground-park / none pairing is accurate for this rooted herb.

The Narcissus Bulb Fly is a stout, bumblebee-mimicking hoverfly whose larvae are internal parasites of narcissus, snowdrop, and bluebill bulbs. Adults are strong fliers that visit flowers for nectar, particularly in spring when their bulb-plant hosts are in bloom. In Amsterdam parks and gardens planted with narcissus, this species can be abundant in April and May. The bumblebee mimicry is convincing enough to fool casual observers. Default flower-visitor and flower-bobber tags fit the adult behaviour accurately.

These phoretic mesostigmatid mites ride on bark beetles (particularly Ips typographus) that breed under the bark of Picea (Norway spruce) and are carried passively into new galleries when their beetle host colonises a new tree. Their own movement is negligible — they cling to the beetle's body or walk slowly within the bark gallery — making idle-only the correct animation. Tree-bark is the dominant habitat because the entire life cycle occurs in the subcortical space. In Amsterdam this mite community appears wherever spruce bark beetles attack stressed ornamental spruces.

The lime hawk-moth is one of Amsterdam's most familiar sphingids, whose large green caterpillars feed exclusively on the foliage of Tilia (lime/linden) — the dominant street tree of the city. Larvae live and feed openly in the canopy, hanging from twigs in a characteristic oblique posture and barely moving during feeding bouts. Tree-canopy is the correct dominant habitat; no secondary is needed since even resting adults settle in tree foliage or nearby walls. Idle-only captures both the near-motionless caterpillar and the perched adult moth; the moth's nocturnal flight is short-lived and not the primary visible state.

Mimela junii is a shiny green scarab beetle whose larvae develop in the soil of lawns and grassy areas, feeding on grass roots in much the same way as the related garden chafer — it is a grassland and park-lawn species tied to vegetated, well-drained soil. Adults emerge in summer and are active at dusk, flying to deciduous trees and shrubs, but they spend most observable daylight hours resting on the ground or low herbage. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber does not fit a chafer beetle with soil-dwelling larvae and a brief adult flight period; ground-park captures the larval and resting habitat and idle-only the daytime stillness of the adult.

Mononychus punctumalbum is a small weevil (Curculionidae) that is an oligophagous specialist on Yellow Iris (Iris pseudacorus), with larvae developing inside the seed capsules. It is therefore tightly bound to the water margins and ditches where Yellow Iris grows throughout Amsterdam's canal system and park ponds. Adults are found crawling on iris leaves and seed pods at the water's edge. Habitat overridden to water-edge (dominant) with ground-park secondary for adults found on adjacent vegetation; movement overridden to idle-only.

The Old Lady is a large, dark noctuid moth that rests by day pressed flat against tree bark, wooden fences, or sheltered walls where its mottled grey-brown patterning provides near-perfect camouflage. Larvae feed on herbaceous plants and low shrubs at night. The species is regularly found in Amsterdam parks and canal-side trees, always in a motionless, pressed-flat resting posture during daylight hours. Tree-bark and idle-only replace the insect defaults of flower-visitor and flower-bobber to reflect this characteristic roosting behaviour.

The white wagtail is a characteristic bird of open urban and peri-aquatic habitats in Amsterdam: it forages by walking rapidly along pavements, car parks, and rooftops, as well as patrolling canal banks and water margins for invertebrates. Its constant tail-pumping walk is one of its most recognisable features. Ground-urban is the dominant habitat because it is most often observed on hard, open surfaces in the city; water-edge is secondary for canal-bank foraging. The bird default of tree-canopy and tree-flitter does not apply at all to this ground-walking species.

The hazel dormouse is a highly arboreal small mammal that climbs and forages among the branches of Corylus, Crataegus, and Prunus in Amsterdam's wooded parks, rarely descending to the ground except during autumn nut-gathering or hibernation. Tree-canopy is the dominant habitat because dormice move primarily through the shrub and tree layer; ground-park is secondary for ground-level foraging in autumn. Movement alternates between nimble canopy hops between branches (tree-flitter) and brief ground foraging bouts (park-roamer). The dormouse is a red-listed species in the Netherlands and any Amsterdam record would be exceptional, most likely from the Amsterdamse Bos.

Wall Lettuce is a slender annual or biennial composite that specialises in colonising old brick walls, shaded wall bases, and rocky urban surfaces across Amsterdam, its name reflecting this preference for masonry habitats. It produces small pale-yellow dandelion-like flowers and thrives in the mortar of canal-side walls, churchyard boundaries, and building bases where little other vegetation can grow. Despite the plant default of ground-park, Wall Lettuce is distinctively wall-associated and wall-and-roof is the correct dominant habitat; ground-park is a secondary where it grows at the base of walls in shaded park soil. Movement is none as for all plants.

The Grouse Wing is a caddisfly (Trichoptera) whose larvae build portable cases and live in the sediment of slow-moving canals and ditches — classic Amsterdam water-body habitat. Adults emerge in summer and rest near the water's edge on waterside vegetation and canal walls, making water-edge the dominant surface placement. The default flower-visitor / flower-bobber profile does not fit aquatic insects; water-edge-stalker captures the adult's slow resting-and-drifting behaviour along the canal margin, with idle-only as secondary for extended resting bouts.

The Smoky Wainscot is a noctuid moth whose larvae feed on grasses and reeds, making it strongly associated with damp grassland, fen edges, and reed-bed margins along Amsterdam's canal and polder fringes. Adults are pale straw-coloured with indistinct streaking, resting pressed against grass stems during the day where they are nearly invisible. The species is attracted to light traps in large numbers in July and August. The insect default flower-visitor / flower-bobber is wrong for a grass-feeding moth: ground-park / idle-only reflects the adult resting behaviour in grassland; water-edge is a genuine secondary because it is particularly associated with reed beds and wet ditchside grass in Amsterdam's polder edges.

Myzocallis carpini is a specialist aphid that forms colonies on the undersides of Carpinus betulus (hornbeam) leaves throughout the growing season. Hornbeam is common in Amsterdam parks and cemeteries as both a street tree and a hedge plant. The aphids are essentially immobile during feeding — apterae cling to leaf veins and exude honeydew — making idle-only the only appropriate movement class. Tree-canopy is the sole habitat since the colony lives entirely within the leaf layer. Winged alates disperse briefly in summer but this flight is invisible at the pixel-art scale.

Cherry blackfly (Myzus cerasi) forms dense, conspicuous black colonies on the curled shoot tips and leaf undersides of Prunus trees — ornamental cherries are among the most widely planted street trees in Amsterdam. The aphids remain stationary during feeding, with apterae virtually welded to the plant tissue, justifying idle-only movement. Tree-canopy is the dominant habitat because all feeding and colony growth happens in the upper leaf and shoot layer. The colonies are most spectacular in May–June on ornamental cherry avenues throughout the city, before natural enemies reduce populations.

Nematinae sawflies — especially the Pontania gallers — induce distinctive bean-like leaf galls on Salix (willow) species growing along Amsterdam's canals and in wetland parks. The larvae develop entirely enclosed within the gall tissue, making them invisible and completely immobile at the sprite scale; adults are short-lived and rarely observed on the tree. Tree-canopy is the sole habitat since the galls form on leaves. Idle-only is correct: the most visible sign of these insects is the static gall on the leaf surface, which barely changes day to day.

Nematus tibialis is a tenthredinid sawfly whose larvae feed gregariously on Robinia pseudoacacia (black locust) foliage, a tree widely planted on Amsterdam's sandy soils and disturbed sites. The larvae cluster on leaflets, consuming them from the margins inward, and barely move between feeding bouts — idle-only is the right animation. Tree-canopy is the dominant and sufficient habitat; no bark or ground stage is visible or relevant at this scale. Outbreaks can cause dramatic defoliation on Amsterdam's Robinia rows, making the species conspicuous to city residents in summer.

The Common Awl Robber Fly is a medium-sized predatory fly in the family Asilidae, recognised by its dark body and striking blue-grey abdomen tip. It perches prominently on bare twigs, fence posts, or low vegetation — waiting motionless to launch aerial attacks on passing insects. In Amsterdam it frequents open park areas, scrubby margins, and sunny clearings. The default flower-visitor tag does not apply; this is an ambush predator, not a pollinator. ground-park reflects the open vegetated habitat; idle-only captures the prolonged motionless perching that characterises its hunting strategy.

Magnolia scale is one of the largest soft scales in Europe and a conspicuous pest of Magnolia trees in Amsterdam parks and botanical collections, forming glossy dome-shaped brown bodies directly on the bark of twigs and branches. The settled scale insects are entirely immobile — they fuse to the bark surface and feed by suctioning phloem sap through a fixed stylet. Tree-bark is the dominant habitat since the scales live on the woody stem surface rather than the leaf layer. No movement animation beyond idle-only is appropriate. The species is readily identified on ornamental magnolias in summer by the distinctive waxy bumps and honeydew drip beneath.

Nephopterix angustella is a pyralid moth whose larvae feed inside the berries and shoots of spindle (Euonymus europaeus) and occasionally other shrubs, making it associated with scrubby park edges, hedgerows, and gardens where spindle is planted or grows wild. Adults rest on the foliage or bark of host shrubs during the day. In Amsterdam it is found in parks and cemeteries where spindle occurs. The flower-visitor default does not apply; habitat overridden to tree-canopy (dominant, for its shrub association) with ground-park secondary; movement overridden to idle-only.

The Four-spotted Cranefly is a large, distinctively patterned cranefly with yellow-and-black banding that rests on low vegetation, leaves, and flower heads in parks, meadows, and damp grassland in Amsterdam. Its larvae (leatherjackets) live in moist soil eating roots. Adults are predominantly idle-only, sitting on stems and leaves during the day with characteristic drooping legs, and fly weakly when disturbed. Overridden from the insect flower-visitor default as this species does not visit flowers for nectar and is better placed in the ground vegetation stratum (ground-park) with idle-only as its sole movement class.

The Large Yellow Underwing is one of the most abundant noctuid moths in Amsterdam, drawn to park lawns, canal-side verges, and any patch of herbaceous vegetation where its larvae feed on low-growing plants. Adults rest motionless during the day on leaf litter, soil, or low bark, relying on camouflage; the vivid yellow hindwings are a startle display only revealed in flight. At night they fly to light sources — a familiar sight at Amsterdam street lamps in late summer — but do not feed significantly at flowers and are not flower-visitors in any meaningful sense. The category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is wrong for this moth; ground-park and idle-only reflect its actual daytime resting ecology in the city.

Yellow Water-lily is a large aquatic perennial rooted in the mud of slow-moving and still water, with floating leaves and distinctive yellow, globe-shaped flowers that float on or just above the surface. It is native to Dutch waterways and is found in Amsterdam's canals, ditches, and park ponds wherever the water is sufficiently clear and shallow. The floating leaves provide basking platforms for frogs and perches for damselflies. water-surface placement is correct — the visible sprite should sit on the water polygon. The default ground-park is inappropriate for this obligate aquatic species.

The noctule is the largest common bat in the Netherlands and a classic tree-hole roosting species, using cavities in mature Betula and other deciduous trees in Amsterdam's parks and cemeteries. By day it roosts motionless in tree holes (idle-only, tree-canopy); at dusk it launches into high fast aerial foraging circuits over parks and open water (sky-looper, sky-only). Tree-canopy is dominant for placement because that is where the roosting colony is anchored; sky-only is secondary for the foraging flight. Noctules are often the first bats visible each evening in Amsterdam, flying high before dark.

This entry covers bat species (Noctule, Leisler's, various Myotis) that roost in cavities and crevices of mature Castanea and other veteran trees in Amsterdam parks, emerging at dusk to hunt insects over open water and parkland. Tree-canopy is the dominant habitat placement because the roost is in the tree; sky-only is added for the nightly foraging flights over open ground and water. Sky-looper is the primary movement class — bats follow long curved aerial paths — with idle-only as secondary for the daytime roosting state hidden inside the tree. Amsterdam's park trees, especially older ones, host significant bat roost populations.

The Black-crowned Night Heron is a compact, stocky heron that roosts in trees and shrubs by day and forages at water edges from dusk onwards. Amsterdam has a significant breeding colony, particularly around the Vondelpark and the Herengracht area, and the species is regularly seen hunched motionless on canal railings or overhanging branches during daytime hours. Its nocturnal behaviour means it is more often encountered as a static silhouette than as an active hunter. water-edge is the dominant placement; idle-only is the primary movement reflecting daytime roosting, with water-edge-stalker as a secondary for dusk/night animation. The default tree-canopy / tree-flitter does not apply.

The Camberwell Beauty is a large, striking nymphalid butterfly whose larvae feed gregariously on Celtis (hackberry) and Salix foliage, while adults are long-lived and frequently visit willow catkins and tree sap for nourishment rather than flowers. In Amsterdam it is a scarce but regular autumn migrant and occasional breeder on riverside willows. Tree-canopy is the dominant habitat because larvae feed in foliage and adults bask and roost on tree bark and branches; flower-visitor is secondary for adults seeking nectar and sap sources.
