Movement alternates between gliding flights between canopy trees (tree-flitter) and brief landings at food sources (flower-bobber).

The black locust gall midge is a tiny cecidomyiid fly whose larvae induce characteristic leaf-roll galls on Robinia pseudoacacia, an ornamental street tree found across Amsterdam. The adult midges and their larvae spend their entire life cycle within the folded leaf margins of the host canopy, never visiting flowers or foraging on the ground. Default insect tags (flower-visitor, flower-bobber) are overridden because this species is strictly a tree-canopy dweller with no flower-foraging behavior. The larval stage is entirely sedentary inside the gall, making idle-only the correct movement class.

Oecetis ochracea is a leptocerid caddisfly whose aquatic larvae live in still and slow-moving water, constructing portable cases from plant material on canal and pond bottoms. Adults emerge in summer and rest during the day on waterside vegetation close to their emergence site. As an aquatic insect the default flower-visitor / flower-bobber is wrong; water-edge captures both the adult's bankside resting behaviour and the connection to the aquatic larval habitat. Idle-only is appropriate for the motionless daytime resting posture on canal-edge reeds and willowherb in Amsterdam.

Oedemera lurida and Oedemera virescens are closely related false blister beetles (Oedemeridae) that are obligate flower visitors as adults, feeding on pollen from open flowers — particularly umbellifers, composites, and roses in Amsterdam's parks. They are reliable and easy to observe on warm summer days nectaring alongside bees and hoverflies. The default flower-visitor / flower-bobber combination fits perfectly, with idle-only secondary for resting bouts on flower heads between pollen-feeding sessions. Their shiny metallic green elytra make them distinctive among flower visitors.

The Swollen-thighed Beetle is an unmistakable slender green beetle named for the grotesquely enlarged hind femora of the male, commonly found in Amsterdam parks and gardens feeding on pollen in daisy and buttercup flowers from May to August. It is one of the most reliably flower-associated beetles in the Netherlands, spending nearly all adult life on open bowl-shaped flowers — particularly Leucanthemum, Ranunculus, and Rosa — feeding on pollen. The insect default flower-visitor / flower-bobber is an excellent fit for this species; ground-park is added as secondary for the larval stage which develops in dead plant stems at ground level. Idle-only secondary reflects the prolonged pollen-eating posture on a single flower.

Evening primroses are tall biennial or perennial herbs of disturbed sandy and gravelly ground, widely naturalised along Amsterdam's roadsides, rail embankments, dyke tops, and wasteland where their large yellow flowers open at dusk to attract moths. Multiple Oenothera species occur in Amsterdam, all sharing a preference for open, nutrient-poor, dry, and disturbed substrates rather than managed park lawns. Ground-urban is the dominant habitat reflecting the disturbed urban and peri-urban substrates where they are most frequent; ground-park is a secondary for drier sandy park edges and gravel paths. Movement is none.

Intermediate Evening-Primrose is a hybrid evening-primrose that, like its parents, thrives on dry, disturbed, and often sandy or rubble-strewn ground. It is characteristic of railway embankments, roadsides, dune edges, and wasteland rather than managed park lawns. In Amsterdam it colonises degraded urban ground, road margins, and demolition sites. Its large yellow flowers open in the evening and are visited by hawk-moths. ground-urban better reflects its preference for disturbed urban substrates over maintained park vegetation. Movement is none.

Oligia minors are small noctuid moths whose larvae bore into the stems of grasses and sedges at the base, making them essentially ground-level insects of park meadows, rough grassland, and reed-edge habitats in Amsterdam. Adults are compact, grey-brown moths that rest pressed flat against bark or stems during the day and fly at night. The insect default flower-visitor / flower-bobber is not appropriate for a grass-stem borer: ground-park / idle-only fits the larval microhabitat and the resting adult posture, with tree-canopy as secondary for adults that occasionally rest on tree trunks or low woody stems.

This entry covers the assemblage of geometrid caterpillars — primarily winter moth but also related species — recorded on Ostrya carpinifolia (hop-hornbeam) in Amsterdam. These caterpillars feed in the leaf canopy during spring and early summer, moving only slowly between feeding sites. They have no flower-foraging role and do not visit the ground during their larval phase, making the default insect tags inappropriate. Idle-only is correct because caterpillars spend most daylight hours stationary, relying on camouflage rather than movement for survival.

Winter moth and mottled umber caterpillars are early-spring defoliators observed across a wide range of Amsterdam street trees including Amelanchier, Malus, Platanus, Prunus, Pyrus, and Quercus. The larvae hatch in sync with bud burst and feed exclusively in the canopy, looping slowly between leaves but never descending to the ground to forage. The default insect category tags (flower-visitor, flower-bobber) do not apply since these are foliage-feeding caterpillars, not pollinators. Idle-only captures the near-stationary caterpillar posture during the day, when they rest motionless on leaves to avoid predation.

Opilio canestrinii is an invasive harvestman originating from the southern Alps that has spread rapidly through urban western Europe in recent decades, now very common in Amsterdam. Unlike many arachnids it is strongly synanthropic, favouring low vegetation, walls, fences, and the ground-level zone of urban gardens, parks, and building perimeters. Ground-urban is dominant over the arachnid default of anywhere because the species shows a clear preference for disturbed, built-up habitats; ground-park secondary covers its park margin presence. Idle-only fits: harvestmen sit motionless for long periods before making slow walks.

The brimstone moth (Opisthograptis luteolata) and grey dagger moth (Acronicta psi) are both recorded as caterpillars on Amelanchier and Prunus in Amsterdam. Their larvae feed on foliage in the tree canopy throughout spring and summer, making tree-canopy the accurate placement habitat. Neither species is a flower-forager in its caterpillar stage, so the default insect tags are overridden. Adult moths are nocturnal and short-lived; the larval stage that appears in the observations is sedentary, justifying idle-only movement.

The Black-tailed Skimmer is a robust dragonfly that breeds in still and slow-moving water across Amsterdam, with males establishing territories over open water surfaces and bare sunny banks. Males characteristically perch motionless on bare ground, stones, or low vegetation at the water's edge — the idle-only posture that observers most often photograph — then launch into brief aerial patrols. The sky-looper secondary captures these fast hunting and territorial sorties over open water. Larval development takes one to two years in the muddy bottom of ponds and slow canals. Adults are on the wing from May to August.

Orthosia moths (Common Quaker, Hebrew Character, and their relatives in the family Noctuidae) are early-spring moths recorded on Salix in Amsterdam. Their caterpillars feed on the fresh foliage of willows and other broadleaved trees in the canopy during April and May. Adults are nocturnal and rest motionless on bark or foliage by day, while caterpillars also remain largely still while feeding. The default flower-visitor/flower-bobber tags do not apply to these foliage-feeding noctuids, so tree-canopy and idle-only are the correct overrides.

The European Rabbit is a common sight in Amsterdam's larger parks and dune edges, particularly in Amsterdamse Bos and Vondelpark, where it grazes on open lawns and shelters in burrows dug into sandy soil. It is a ground-dwelling species entirely dependent on vegetated open space, making ground-park the clear dominant habitat. Its movement is a characteristic stop-start grazing pattern — a short hop, a pause to listen, another hop — which maps directly onto the park-roamer class. No override is needed as the mammal defaults fit well.

Mason bees (Osmia) and mining bees (Andrena) are solitary bees that are conspicuous spring pollinators in Amsterdam, observed on Cercis and Prunus blossoms. They forage energetically between flowers, collecting pollen and nectar for their underground or cavity nests. The default insect tags of flower-visitor and flower-bobber are a perfect fit — these bees are quintessential flower visitors that bob between blooms in season. Outside the flowering window they are absent from view as adults, consistent with the flower-visitor fallback behavior.

The Black Vine Weevil is a dull black, flightless weevil that is one of the most damaging horticultural pests in Amsterdam, with larvae attacking the roots of a very wide range of ornamental plants — rhododendron, Euonymus, strawberry, and many others — in parks and gardens. Adults are nocturnal, hiding in soil or leaf litter by day and emerging at night to feed on notched leaf margins of their host plants. Since it is completely flightless all movement is on foot, crawling slowly through the leaf litter and up stems; ground-park dominates as the larval soil habitat and adult daytime shelter, with tree-canopy secondary for its nocturnal leaf-feeding on shrubs. Idle-only is dominant for the resting daytime posture; park-roamer secondary covers the slow nocturnal foraging walk.

Hackberry psyllids are tiny jumping plant lice (family Psyllidae) that induce nipple galls, blister galls, and bud galls on Celtis (hackberry) trees in Amsterdam. Their entire feeding and reproductive cycle is spent within the leaf tissue of the host canopy, making tree-canopy the only appropriate habitat class. Unlike flower-foraging insects, psyllids are phloem-feeders firmly anchored to their gall structures and rarely move once settled. Idle-only captures this stationary lifestyle; the default insect flower-visitor/flower-bobber tags are not applicable.

The Green Shieldbug is a phytophagous pentatomid that feeds on a wide range of herbaceous plants and shrubs in parks and garden edges, puncturing plant tissue to extract sap. It does not visit flowers for nectar and is not a pollinator, so the default flower-visitor / flower-bobber pairing does not fit. Adults spend most of their visible time sitting motionless on leaves — idle-only is the right movement. It uses both low park vegetation and shrubby tree-canopy margins, so a dual habitat of ground-park (dominant) plus tree-canopy reflects its full range without overstating mobility.

The large walnut aphid is a conspicuous, bright-yellow aphid that colonises the upper sides of walnut (Juglans) leaves in Amsterdam from spring to autumn. Colonies cluster on leaf veins and midribs in the canopy, feeding on phloem sap without ever descending to flowers or ground. The default insect tags (flower-visitor, flower-bobber) are completely inapplicable to this obligate tree-canopy phloem-feeder. Individuals within a colony barely move, making idle-only the correct animation class; winged dispersal forms are brief and infrequent.

The German Scorpionfly is a distinctive insect of shaded, damp hedgerows and woodland edges, where adults scavenge on dead insects and over-ripe fruit on or near the ground. Males are recognised by the upturned, scorpion-like tip of the abdomen. It is not a flower visitor — it feeds on carrion and soft fruit — so the insect defaults of flower-visitor and flower-bobber are inappropriate. Ground-park captures the damp, shaded park-edge habitat; idle-only fits because adults perch on vegetation with occasional short flights that are below the threshold for a distinct movement class. Observations in Amsterdam come chiefly from Amsterdamse Bos and cemetery edges.

The Common Scorpionfly is a distinctive insect with the male's scorpion-like upturned abdomen tip and long "beak." It inhabits shaded, humid, and vegetated areas — woodland edges, dense hedgerows, and shrubbery in parks. Adults are scavengers and kleptoparasites rather than flower visitors, feeding on dead insects and fruit. In Amsterdam they are found in the more sheltered, shrubby parts of larger parks. The default flower-visitor tag does not apply to this scavenging species. ground-park and idle-only reflect its shrubby low-vegetation resting habitat and sedentary adult behaviour.

The Corn Poppy is an annual archaeophyte of disturbed, nutrient-poor soils — roadsides, park edges, building sites, and managed wildflower strips — that has been reintroduced extensively in Amsterdam's green corridors. Its brilliant scarlet petals are an important early-summer nectar source for bees and hoverflies, but the plant itself is stationary and rooted in the ground. The category-default ground-park / none assignment is accurate; the species is exactly the kind of ruderal herb these Amsterdam disturbed-soil habitats support.

Opium Poppy is a tall annual with distinctive glaucous (blue-grey) leaves and large flowers in shades of white, pink, purple, or red, followed by large ornamental seed capsules. It is widely grown as a garden plant in Amsterdam and frequently escapes to parks, waste ground, and roadsides, naturalising readily on disturbed soils. The flowers are rich in pollen and attract bumblebees and other bees in large numbers. It self-seeds prolifically and can persist in the same location for many years. Default ground-park and none tags are appropriate.

Paracolopha morrisoni is a specialist gall-forming aphid that creates leaf-fold galls on Zelkova serrata, an increasingly popular Amsterdam street tree. The foundress and her colony spend the season inside the protective gall on the leaf, with no external movement visible. Zelkova does not produce the floral resources that flower-foraging insects seek, confirming that the default insect tags are unsuitable. Tree-canopy placement and idle-only movement correctly represent this highly sedentary, host-specific phloem-feeder.

The Speckled Wood is a woodland-edge butterfly perfectly adapted to Amsterdam's park interiors and tree-lined canal paths, where males famously occupy sun-flecks on leaf litter or low foliage and spiral upward in territorial chases with rivals. It is one of the few Amsterdam butterflies strongly associated with shade rather than open flowers, and does not need nectar as an adult, relying instead on honeydew from aphids. Its movement alternates between sustained perching in a sun-fleck (idle-only) and short spiralling sorties between trees (tree-flitter), so both values are genuinely needed. The dominant habitat is tree-canopy for the adult's perching spots and larval grass tussock feeding; ground-park is secondary for sun-fleck perching on leaf litter.

Parectopa robiniella and the closely related Macrosaccus robiniella are North American leafminer moths introduced to Europe; their larvae create distinctive upper-surface blotch mines in Robinia pseudoacacia leaves across Amsterdam parks and streets. The tiny caterpillars are confined inside the leaf tissue throughout their larval development and cannot be seen without close inspection. There is no flower-visiting or ground-foraging phase for the larval stage captured in tree observations, so the default insect tags are overridden to tree-canopy and idle-only. Adults are small enough that their brief flights go unrecorded in the tree dataset.

This entry groups the core tit assemblage — blue tit (Cyanistes caeruleus), great tit (Parus major), and long-tailed tit (Aegithalos caudatus) — observed together on Crataegus in Amsterdam parks and gardens. All three species are archetypal canopy insectivores that move in restless mixed flocks, hopping and flitting between branches and trees throughout the year. The default bird tags of tree-canopy and tree-flitter capture this behavior precisely. Long-tailed tits also use hedgerow-style shrubs, but Crataegus qualifies as tree-canopy placement in the taxonomy.

The European fruit lecanium scale is a soft scale insect (family Coccidae) that encrusts the twigs and small branches of Taxodium and many other trees in Amsterdam. Adult females are permanently fixed to the bark surface under their waxy scale covering, making tree-bark the precise habitat class rather than tree-canopy. There is no flower-visiting, no ground presence, and no meaningful movement at any life stage visible in the tree dataset. Idle-only is the only appropriate movement class for this bark-anchored, entirely sedentary scale insect.

Great tit and blue tit are among the most familiar birds in Amsterdam, observed year-round in parks, gardens, and street trees including Juglans. They forage actively through the canopy for caterpillars, insects, and seeds, moving frequently between nearby trees with short arcing flights. The default bird tags — tree-canopy habitat and tree-flitter movement — are a perfect fit for these small, agile canopy birds. Both species readily use nest boxes in Amsterdam, but their visible behavior is canopy-foraging and inter-tree flitting.

This entry represents the mixed observation of cavity-nesting tits (Parus) and roosting pipistrelle bats (Pipistrellus) associated with hollow sections of Pterocarya (Caucasian wingnut) trees in Amsterdam. The observation context is the roost or nest cavity rather than active foraging, so idle-only better captures the roosting/resting state than the default tree-flitter. Bird is used as the dominant category since the taxon group is recorded primarily under avian observations; the bat component is a secondary occupant of the same cavities. Tree-canopy placement snaps correctly to the host tree where the cavity is located.

The Green Pug is a delicate geometrid moth with fresh green patterning that fades with age, associated with apple, pear, and other rosaceous trees in gardens and parks. Larvae feed on the flowers and developing fruitlets of apple, hawthorn, and related trees, making urban orchards and ornamental fruit trees the core habitat. Adults fly in June and July and rest by day on bark or leaves within or near the host tree canopy. The default flower-visitor placement is incorrect; this is a tree-canopy species dependent on rosaceous foliage and flower structures. Amsterdam observations typically come from garden moth traps near fruit trees and hawthorn hedges.

House sparrows and Eurasian tree sparrows are both recorded on Ginkgo in Amsterdam, but their key characteristic is their tight association with the built environment — nesting in wall cavities and roof spaces, then foraging on pavements and squares below. Wall-and-roof is the dominant habitat because both sparrow species are closely tied to buildings for shelter and nesting; ground-urban captures their pavement-foraging behavior. Urban-walker is the appropriate movement class, as both species hop along streets and squares picking up seeds and crumbs rather than flitting between trees. The default bird tags are overridden for this urban-specialist pair.

This entry records house sparrows and wood pigeons using Catalpa and Paulownia trees in Amsterdam specifically as roost sites, rather than in active foraging. Both species are common Amsterdam birds that spend the night roosting in trees, remaining motionless in the canopy for long periods. The tree-canopy habitat is correct for roosting birds perched in the crown, but the typical movement default (tree-flitter) is replaced with idle-only to reflect the roosting context of this observation. Wood pigeon and house sparrow differ in their active-phase habitats, but both roost in canopy foliage.

This entry covers the combined observation of house sparrows and great tits associated with Koelreuteria (golden rain tree) in Amsterdam. House sparrows are quintessential urban birds nesting in building crevices and foraging on pavements, while great tits forage in the canopy — the two species thus pull the habitat in different directions. Wall-and-roof is listed as dominant because house sparrow is the more characteristically urban component and is the species that gives this slug its name; tree-canopy is added as the secondary. Urban-walker suits the sparrow's pavement-foraging, and tree-flitter suits the tit, giving a two-value movement array.

Pauesia anatolica and related braconid parasitoid wasps are specialist parasitoids of aphids living on cedar (Cedrus) trees in Amsterdam. The adult wasps search the canopy for aphid colonies in which to lay their eggs, spending their active time within the tree canopy rather than visiting flowers. While adults have short flight bursts between searching bouts, the observation context is the parasitised aphid colony on the host tree. Tree-canopy and idle-only are the correct overrides, as these wasps are not flower-foragers but tree-canopy hunters whose presence is only detectable by the mummified aphids they leave behind.

Green Alkanet is a robust bristly perennial borage-relative with vivid blue forget-me-not-like flowers, widespread in Amsterdam as a garden escape established along canal banks, wall bases, shaded roadsides, and park margins. It thrives in the nitrogen-rich disturbed soil common at the foot of walls and fences in residential streets. The dominant habitat is ground-urban due to its characteristic presence at wall bases and pavement edges throughout the city, with ground-park secondary for its presence in park shaded margins. Movement stays none.

The Red-legged Shieldbug is a large, handsome shield bug with a dark bronze-green body and distinctively red-orange legs; it is strongly associated with broadleaved trees — oak, alder, hazel, and beech — making tree-canopy the dominant habitat in Amsterdam's parks and street trees. Adults and nymphs feed on developing seeds and buds in the canopy and descend to bark to overwinter under loose flakes (tree-bark secondary). They are almost entirely idle-only, sitting motionless on leaves or bark for extended periods. Overridden from the insect flower-visitor default: this bug feeds on tree material, not flower nectar.

The Willow Beauty is a well-camouflaged geometrid moth that rests with wings spread on rough bark, relying on its grey-brown cryptic patterning to disappear against tree trunks and wooden fences across Amsterdam parks and gardens. Its larvae feed on a broad range of trees and shrubs including yew, privet, ivy, and various Rosaceae, so it can be found anywhere mature trees or dense shrubby garden hedging exists. Adults are nocturnal and come to light traps; during the day they are pressed flat against bark and essentially immobile. Tree-bark is the dominant habitat for its cryptic bark-resting adults; tree-canopy is secondary for the larval feeding stage. Movement is idle-only: it is the archetypal bark-resting moth.

The coal tit is a small conifer-specialist tit that regularly forages in the needle canopy of cedars (Cedrus), false cypresses (Chamaecyparis), dawn redwood (Metasequoia), spruces (Picea), and pines (Pinus) in Amsterdam parks. It is an agile canopy bird that flits quickly between branches and adjacent trees, often in mixed tit flocks during winter. The default bird tags — tree-canopy and tree-flitter — are a precise fit for this small, highly active songbird. Coal tits also cache seeds in bark crevices but their primary visible behavior is canopy foraging and inter-tree movement.

The Great Cormorant is a large piscivorous bird that dives into Amsterdam's canals and the IJ to catch fish, then hauls out on jetties, canal walls, or low branches to dry its wings with a characteristic outstretched pose. Water-edge is the dominant habitat for map placement, with water-body as secondary because it spends significant time diving below the surface. The tree-canopy / tree-flitter default is entirely inappropriate; water-edge-stalker captures its slow patrol of canal edges, and idle-only reflects the prolonged wing-drying posture that is the species' most visible behaviour.

The Common Pheasant is a large, heavy ground bird that forages by walking slowly through grassland, scrub, and woodland edges — it almost never perches in the canopy and is firmly ground-oriented. Although GBIF classifies it as a bird, the default tree-canopy / tree-flitter pairing is wholly wrong for a pheasant; it needs ground-park and park-roamer to match its actual behaviour. In Amsterdam it appears occasionally in larger parks and peri-urban green edges rather than inner-city gardens. Its leisurely foraging stride maps well onto the park-roamer animation.

The Common Froghopper is a small sap-sucking homopteran whose nymphs produce the well-known "cuckoo spit" foam on plant stems, while adults are capable of remarkable leaping. Nymphs feed on a very wide range of herbaceous plants and are a familiar sight in Amsterdam parks, roadsides, and rough ground from late spring. Adults are cryptically brown or patterned and sit motionless on plant stems and flower heads. Habitat overridden to ground-park (dominant) with flower-visitor secondary for adults found on flower stems; movement overridden to idle-only as adults sit still despite their jumping ability.

Philodromus running crab spiders (family Philodromidae) are swift, flat-bodied hunters that patrol bark surfaces, twigs, and leaves of trees and shrubs rather than spinning webs. In Amsterdam they are most frequently recorded on the bark and foliage of park trees, making tree-bark the dominant habitat with tree-canopy as secondary. The genus-level identification here (Philodromus spec.) means a single species cannot be pinned down, but all members share this arboreal hunting lifestyle. The anywhere default undersells the bark-and-foliage preference; idle-only remains correct as the animated sprite will show the spider in a stalking pause.

The Common Striped Woodlouse is a terrestrial isopod crustacean, not a true insect, and is recorded in the GBIF category row as taxon group 13 (Crustacea). It lives under leaf litter, bark, and damp logs in parks and gardens, placing it firmly in ground-park. Its movement is negligible at any sprite-resolution — it shuffles a few millimetres when disturbed, which folds into idle-only per the taxonomy note on slow-creep. The category is overridden from insect to other because woodlice are crustaceans; the habitat default for other (anywhere) is further overridden to ground-park since this species has a clear damp-litter preference.

Timothy is a tall, tufted perennial grass with a distinctive cylindrical, spike-like flower head — one of the most recognisable grasses in Amsterdam's parks, road verges, and unmown green strips. It is widely used in amenity grass seed mixes and establishes readily in park lawns that are not closely mown. The abundant pollen makes it a major hay-fever trigger in summer. It provides seed food for finches and cover for small invertebrates. Default ground-park and none are correct.

Phloeosinus aubei is a small scolytine bark beetle whose adults and larvae bore beneath the bark of Metasequoia (dawn redwood) and cypress-family trees in Amsterdam. Both the egg galleries and larval tunnels are entirely within the cambium and inner bark, making tree-bark the correct placement habitat. The beetle's presence is detected by its exit holes and frass rather than by visible surface activity, confirming idle-only as the movement class. The default insect tags (flower-visitor, flower-bobber) have no relevance to this bark-boring specialist.

This Metasequoia-specialist bark beetle (Curculionidae: Scolytinae) is a close relative of Phloeosinus aubei that bores into the bark of dawn redwood trees in Amsterdam. Like all scolytine bark beetles, it spends its entire life cycle — from egg laying through larval gallery construction to pupation — inside the bark, with only brief surface exposure during dispersal flights. Tree-bark is the sole relevant habitat class, and idle-only represents the typical invisible-in-bark lifestyle captured by tree observations. The default insect category tags are overridden for this obligate bark-dwelling species.

Turkish Sage is a robust ornamental perennial of Mediterranean origin, widely planted in Dutch public parks and road-side beds for its architectural whorled yellow flowers and large felted leaves. It is a ground-level herbaceous plant with no canopy or aquatic habitat component. In Amsterdam it appears commonly as a designed planting in park borders, cycle-path medians, and traffic-island beds, where its dried seed heads persist through winter and provide food for seed-eating birds. The plant default (ground-park, none) applies directly.

The Black Redstart is a striking urban specialist that nests in gaps and crevices of buildings, industrial rooftops, and along railway embankments in Amsterdam. Unlike the default small-songbird profile, it rarely forages in tree canopies; instead it drops from walls or low perches onto paved ground to snatch invertebrates, then returns to a rooftop vantage point. Its characteristic tail-quivering habit makes it visually distinctive even when perched still. The wall-and-roof habitat and urban-walker movement fit this species far better than the tree-canopy default.

Common Reed is a tall perennial grass forming extensive reedbeds along Amsterdam's canal margins, park ponds, ditches, and the IJ waterfront, one of the most ecologically important wetland plants in the Netherlands. It grows in standing or slow-moving water with roots in waterlogged soil, sending up dense stands of stems to 3 m or more. Reedbeds provide nesting habitat for reed warblers, bitterns, and marsh harriers, and winter roosting for Starlings. Habitat overridden from ground-park to water-edge (dominant) with water-body secondary for its rhizomes in submerged sediment; movement stays none.

The woolly beech aphid is a distinctive species whose white waxy secretions make colonies immediately visible on the undersides of beech (Fagus) leaves in Amsterdam parks. Dense colonies cover the leaves from spring until early summer, feeding on phloem sap without any ground-foraging or flower-visiting behavior. Tree-canopy is the precise habitat since colonies are restricted to the foliage of Fagus, and idle-only reflects the sedentary clustered lifestyle of the apterous colony stages. The default flower-visitor and flower-bobber tags are completely inapplicable to this specialist tree-canopy aphid.

Phyllobius intrusus is a leaf-notching weevil recorded on Thuja (arborvitae) in Amsterdam, where adults feed on the scale-like foliage in the canopy. Adults are slow-moving, cryptic beetles that cling to foliage and move only short distances when disturbed. Unlike the default insect category of flower-visitors, this species is a foliage-feeder with no pollinator role, so tree-canopy and idle-only are the correct tags. Adults drop to the ground to overwinter in the soil, but their observable phase on Thuja is canopy-based.

Phyllonorycter esperella and similar gracillariid leafminers are recorded on Carpinus betulus (hornbeam) in Amsterdam. Their larvae live inside the hornbeam leaf, forming the blotch mines that identify this family, remaining completely enclosed within the leaf tissue until pupation. The default flower-visitor and flower-bobber insect tags have no relevance to these concealed leafminers, so tree-canopy and idle-only are used instead. Carpinus-associated leafminers are often among the most abundant insects by biomass on hornbeam street trees, though individual larvae are tiny and invisible without leaf-dissection.

This entry covers the assemblage of leafminer micro-moths (Phyllonorycter and relatives) observed on Castanea (sweet chestnut) in Amsterdam. Their larvae mine inside the leaf lamina, creating characteristic blotch or tentiform mines visible by holding leaves to the light; the caterpillar is entirely hidden within the leaf throughout its development. There is no flower-visiting stage recorded in this tree-associated observation, making tree-canopy and idle-only the appropriate overrides. Adults are tiny night-flying moths whose brief adult lives are not captured in tree-dataset observations.

The Common Chiffchaff is one of the earliest spring migrants to arrive in Amsterdam, and its persistent two-note song is a fixture of the city's parks and tree-lined canals from March through October. It is a classic leaf-warbler, foraging almost exclusively in the canopy and mid-storey of deciduous trees and shrubs, gleaning small insects from foliage and making short aerial sallies between branches. Its movement pattern is exactly the tree-flitter archetype — constant short hops between branches and between nearby trees, rarely descending to the ground. The bird category default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter is a perfect fit.

Phytomyza ilicis is the holly leaf miner fly, whose larvae create the distinctive yellow-brown blotch mines visible on Ilex (holly) leaves throughout Amsterdam. The single larva develops entirely inside the leaf between the upper and lower epidermis, making its habitat strictly tree-canopy by placement. Adults are tiny, inconspicuous flies that mate and oviposit quickly in spring; the larval mines are the persistent visible evidence. The default insect tags are overridden to tree-canopy and idle-only, as this species has no flower-foraging role and the larval stage — the observable one — does not move.

Phytophthora lateralis is an oomycete root and crown pathogen that infects and kills Chamaecyparis (false cypress) trees in Amsterdam by rotting the roots and basal stem tissue. Although oomycetes are not true fungi, they are assigned to the fungus category as the closest practical equivalent in the taxonomy. The default fungus tag of tree-bark is overridden to tree-rooted because this pathogen operates belowground and at the root collar, not on the aerial bark. Movement is none, as all Phytophthora propagules move only with water flow and have no animated life stage.

Phytoptus avellanae, known as the big bud mite or hazelnut gall mite, infests the buds and developing shoots of Corylus (hazel) in Amsterdam, causing the characteristic abnormally swollen "big bud" symptom. These eriophyid mites are microscopic and spend their lives inside buds or beneath bud scales in the tree canopy, never descending to the ground or visiting flowers. The default arachnid tags (anywhere, idle-only) are partially overridden: idle-only stays, but anywhere is replaced with tree-canopy for precise placement on the host Corylus. The mites' entire ecology is bound to the aerial parts of their host tree.

Magpie (Pica pica) and carrion crow (Corvus corone) are two of Amsterdam's most successful urban corvids, observed together on Koelreuteria (golden rain tree) and across the entire city. Both species forage omnivorous on pavements, lawns, and rubbish, while also using trees as perch and nest sites — making anywhere the correct dominant habitat as specified for corvids in the taxonomy. The two-value movement array (tree-flitter, urban-walker) reflects their genuine alternation between tree perching with inter-tree flights and ground foraging walks on urban surfaces. This is the canonical example of the anywhere/multi-movement pattern from the taxonomy guide.

This catch-all grouping covers woodpeckers, nuthatches, and other cavity-excavating birds alongside small bats that roost in the same hollows in Amsterdam's urban trees. Woodpeckers are active on bark surfaces and in the canopy of large trees in parks like Vondelpark and the Amsterdamse Bos, while bats emerge at dusk to use hollows for daytime roost. The dominant tag is tree-canopy for animated placement, with tree-bark as secondary because woodpeckers cling vertically on trunks. Movement is primarily tree-flitter for the avian component, with idle-only capturing roosting bats that sit entirely still during daylight.

The Large White is the most conspicuous of Amsterdam's white butterflies, abundant in parks, allotment gardens, and any area where brassica crops or nasturtiums grow — the larval host plants. Adults are active nectar-feeders visiting a wide range of flowers from spring to autumn and are a familiar sight over Amsterdam's community gardens and park flower beds. Their flight between blooms is a classic flower-bobber pattern: purposeful but short, with frequent pauses to feed. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is correct for this species.

The Green-veined White is one of Amsterdam's most familiar butterflies, found in parks, gardens, cemetery verges, and along canal-side vegetation throughout the flying season from April to October. It differs from the Large and Small Whites by preferring damp, vegetated habitats and by having green-veined underwings that provide superb camouflage when resting on leaves. Larvae feed on garlic mustard, cuckooflower, and other wild Brassicaceae, plants common in Amsterdam's shadier green spaces. Adults are avid flower visitors, nectaring at a wide range of blooms, making the default flower-visitor placement well suited. Multiple broods are produced each year with populations peaking in late spring and late summer.

The Small White is the most abundant white butterfly in Amsterdam, present in multiple generations from March to October across parks, allotments, and canal-side plantings. Adults are active nectar feeders visiting a wide range of flowers, while larvae feed on brassicas and Tropaeolum (nasturtium), which are common in Amsterdam's community gardens. Its flight between flowers is the classic flower-bobber pattern — purposeful short hops between blooms with occasional wider transits. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is correct for this species.

Fox-and-cubs is a vivid orange-flowered hawkweed introduced from central Europe, now naturalised on lawns, verges, and park edges throughout Amsterdam. The clustered orange-red flower heads on hairy stems are immediately distinctive and unlike any native species. It spreads by stolons and seeds and can form dense patches in amenity grass. It is frequently recorded in Vondelpark and other larger green spaces. Default ground-park and none tags are correct.

This pairing covers two bat species recorded roosting in the same Fagus tree in Amsterdam: the Common Pipistrelle and the larger Noctule, both of which use hollow branches and bark crevices as daytime roosts. The Noctule is one of Europe's highest-flying bats, often hunting above tree-canopy level well into open sky, while the Pipistrelle takes lower circuits; both share the sky-looper animation at dusk. Placement snaps to the tree bark during idle hours and transitions to sky-only during active flight. The mammal default does not fit either species, requiring a full override.

The Common Pipistrelle is Amsterdam's most frequently recorded bat, roosting by day in tight bark crevices of street trees such as Platanus, Populus, and Acer before emerging at twilight to hunt midges and moths. During active hours it flies fast looping circuits at 2–10 m above canals, parks, and lit streets — a textbook sky-looper. The tree-bark habitat tag places the sprite at a trunk during daylight with an idle-only animation, while the sky-only secondary and sky-looper movement dominate the dusk render. The mammal default of ground-park / park-roamer is entirely wrong for a bat, hence the override.

The Brown Long-eared Bat is a slow, highly manoeuvrable hunter that gleans insects from leaves and bark surfaces in wooded Amsterdam parks, while the Common Pipistrelle favours open aerial hunting; both species roost in Fraxinus cavities and bark crevices. Their shared tree-bark placement during daylight hours captures the roost site accurately, with sky-only as the active-flight habitat. The sky-looper animation is the dominant movement for the rendered dusk phase, and idle-only covers the stationary daytime roost. Both species override the mammal default of ground-park and park-roamer.

Roosting bats in Amsterdam use bark crevices and cavities of mature urban trees — Aesculus, Quercus, Tilia, and others — as daytime shelters, making tree-bark the correct resting placement. At dusk they leave to hunt insects on long curved aerial paths above canals, parks, and open water, which drives the sky-only secondary and the dominant sky-looper animation. The idle-only secondary captures the daytime roosting phase when no movement occurs. The mammal default of ground-park / park-roamer does not apply to any bat species, so this is a full override.

The Nursery-web Spider is a hunting spider that stalks prey in tall grass, nettles, and low vegetation in parks, meadows, and woodland edges — it does not build a catching web and sits low in vegetation with a characteristic flat, body-to-substrate posture. Ground-park is the correct override from the arachnid default anywhere, since this spider is consistently found in ground-level and knee-height vegetation rather than on walls, bark, or flowers. It is largely sedentary between hunts, making idle-only the right movement class. In Amsterdam it is a common summer presence in longer grass in Vondelpark and Amsterdamse Bos, often seen carrying an egg sac.

Ribwort plantain is one of the most widespread plants in Amsterdam, found in virtually every park lawn, grass verge, and rough grassland patch in the city. Its narrow, ribbed leaves form basal rosettes and it tolerates mowing well. The plant default of ground-park and none describes its placement and animation accurately. No override is needed.

Platyedra subcinerea is a small gelechiid micro-moth whose larvae feed inside the seed capsules of mallow family plants (Malvaceae), particularly Malva and Althaea, which are ground-level park plants. Adults are nocturnal and rest cryptically during the day on low vegetation or substrate, making them invisible in day simulation. Neither flower-visitor nor flower-bobber is appropriate since this is a moth that conceals itself at rest and visits flowers only for nocturnal nectaring not easily represented. Ground-park captures both the larval host-plant habitat and the adult resting microhabitat. Idle-only reflects the resting adult posture that would be visible in daytime rendering.

The Diamond-back Moth is one of the world's most abundant moths, a tiny (15 mm wingspan) species whose larvae are serious pests of brassica crops globally. In Amsterdam it occurs wherever cabbages, mustards, and related Brassicaceae grow, from allotment gardens to ruderal wasteland patches. Adults rest with wings folded to show the characteristic diamond pattern along the back, and are most active at dusk. It is not a flower visitor; the default insect placement of flower-visitor is incorrect. The moth is continuously brooded in warm years, with multiple generations from spring through autumn. Its exceptionally rapid evolution of insecticide resistance has made it a model organism in pest management research.

The Great Crested Grebe is a diving waterbird that is a regular presence on Amsterdam's larger park ponds, the Amstel, and the IJ margin, famous for its elaborate courtship dances in early spring. It spends the majority of its time on or in open water, diving repeatedly to pursue fish under the surface; water-body is the dominant habitat as it spends more time under water than at the surface when actively foraging. Between dives it rests and drifts on the surface (water-drifter), and when loafing between foraging bouts it sits virtually motionless (idle-only). The default bird tag of tree-canopy / tree-flitter is completely wrong; grebes are foot-propelled diving birds that cannot walk on land.

The European Paper Wasp is a slender social wasp common in Amsterdam parks and urban gardens, readily recognised by its yellow-and-black banding and habit of hanging long legs in flight. It builds small open paper nests under eaves, in shrubs, or in wall cavities, and forages widely on flowers for nectar as well as hunting caterpillars to feed larvae. Unlike bees it moves between flower patches but also walks along stems and ground-level vegetation hunting prey, so a secondary ground-park placement captures that foraging behaviour. The flower-visitor dominant habitat and flower-bobber primary movement cover the nectar-foraging animation, with urban-walker as secondary for its ground-level prey-hunting passes.

Four-leaved Allseed is a tiny annual weed of southern European origin that has colonised Amsterdam's warmest urban micro-habitats: pavement cracks, wall bases, cobbled squares, and the dry sandy margins of tram tracks. It requires bare or disturbed mineral ground rather than vegetated park soil, so the default ground-park placement is too green; ground-urban is the biologically accurate choice. The plant is inconspicuous — reaching only 5–15 cm — but consistent with Amsterdam's expanding thermophilic pavement flora.

The Green Immigrant Leaf Weevil is a small, bright metallic-green weevil that feeds on the fresh foliage of a broad range of deciduous trees including alder, willow, and hazel in Amsterdam parks and canal plantings. Adults are found directly on leaves in the canopy from spring through summer, making tree-canopy the clear dominant habitat. The default flower-visitor / flower-bobber tags do not fit a bark-and-leaf-feeding weevil; idle-only is appropriate because these beetles move slowly and spend most of their time chewing foliage rather than flying between flowers.

The Comma is a distinctive butterfly with ragged, notched wing edges that provide superb dead-leaf camouflage when resting with wings closed. It is a common sight in Amsterdam parks, gardens, and along canal-side vegetation, nectaring freely at flowers from early spring through autumn. It produces two broods per year and adults overwinter in hollow trees and dense vegetation, so it can be encountered in any month during mild spells. Larvae feed on nettles and hops, plants common in Amsterdam's green spaces. The default flower-visitor placement is appropriate as nectar-seeking is the most photogenic and frequent observed behavior.

The Common Rough Woodlouse is a terrestrial isopod crustacean — often mislabelled an insect in GBIF taxon groups — that thrives in damp, decaying organic matter across Amsterdam's parks, cemeteries, and urban gardens. It shelters under bark, rotting logs, leaf litter, and stones during the day, becoming marginally more active at night to feed on decomposing plant material. Although biologically a crustacean it is catalogued under the insect taxon group here; the category is kept as insect per the GBIF source. Ground-park is dominant because most sightings are under leaf litter and soil; tree-bark is a genuine secondary because it commonly hides beneath loose bark on fallen or standing dead wood. Movement is idle-only since its crawl is below the renderer's frame-rate threshold (folded in per taxonomy rules).

Silverweed is a prostrate perennial with feathery silver-green pinnate leaves and bright yellow flowers, spreading by long surface runners. It favours damp, compacted, or disturbed ground — footpath edges, trampled lawns, and damp depressions in parks. The silvery leaf undersides are diagnostic. In Amsterdam it is commonly found on the margins of park paths and in areas of light foot traffic where grass is thin. Default ground-park and none are appropriate; it is a low herb of open, often slightly disturbed vegetated ground.

The Yellow-flowered Strawberry (Mock Strawberry, formerly Duchesnea indica) is a low mat-forming perennial originally from Asia that has naturalised widely in Amsterdam's park lawns, cemetery margins, and grassy verges. Its creeping runners, trifoliate leaves, and small yellow flowers closely resemble a wild strawberry. The ground-park default fits well: it grows in established grassy or lightly shaded sward and does not favour paved surfaces. It is increasingly common as a lawn weed in Vondelpark and community gardens.

The Red Swamp Crayfish is an invasive North American freshwater crayfish, not an insect — the GBIF taxon-group column is incorrect for this row. It is now established in Amsterdam's canals and ditches, where it is a significant ecological concern and regularly observed. Its primary habitat is water-body (submerged in canals and ponds), with a secondary presence at water-edge where it emerges onto banks at night. Movement is water-drifter as the dominant locomotion (walking along the bottom, which at surface-view renders like drifting), with idle-only as a secondary for when it sits still on the bank. Category is overridden to other since it is a crustacean.

Prociphilus species form waxy woolly colonies on the roots and bark of Fraxinus, while Aphis fabae feeds on young shoots and leaves in the canopy of the same tree in Amsterdam street plantings. Both are sedentary once settled, forming dense stationary colonies rather than flying between flowers, so the insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber does not apply. Tree-canopy is dominant because the most visible infestations coat new growth and leaf undersides, with tree-bark as secondary for the bark-root colonies. Movement is idle-only: aphid movement is imperceptible at map scale.

The 14-spot Ladybird is one of the more common small ladybirds in Amsterdam, recognisable by its rectangular black-and-yellow chequered pattern rather than the classic round spots. Unlike many ladybirds it is strongly associated with low shrubs, tall herbs, and tree canopy rather than purely with flowers, hunting aphid colonies along stems and leaves. It overwinters in bark crevices or leaf litter and becomes active in spring when aphid populations explode. Habitat is overridden to tree-canopy dominant (where aphid colonies concentrate) with flower-visitor secondary for warm-season foraging on open flower umbels.

Mealybugs are soft-bodied scale insects that settle in bark crevices and leaf axils of Catalpa, forming waxy white colonies that are essentially immobile once established. In Amsterdam they are occasional warm-season pests on ornamental Catalpa street trees, visible as woolly clusters on younger branches and bark. Because they feed anchored to the bark surface and do not visit flowers, the insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is entirely wrong. Tree-bark placement and idle-only movement correctly represent a sessile bark-sucking insect.

Pseudoscorpions are tiny arachnids (1–5 mm) that live concealed in the deep bark furrows and under loose bark flakes of large urban trees such as Parrotia in Amsterdam, hunting mites and springtails within their micro-habitat. They are cryptic and virtually stationary from the perspective of any map-scale animation, sitting motionless in bark crevices for most of their lives. The arachnid default of anywhere / idle-only already captures their movement correctly, but the habitat needs an override from anywhere to tree-bark, which more precisely reflects where they are found. Idle-only movement remains unchanged.

The Alexandrine Parakeet is the largest naturalised parakeet in Amsterdam, closely related to the more abundant Ring-necked Parakeet. Like its congener it roosts and nests in tree cavities and dense canopy, flying in noisy flocks between park trees. Tree-canopy is the clear dominant habitat; wall-and-roof secondary covers the cavity-nesting behaviour in building facades and nest boxes. Movement is primarily tree-flitter between canopy perches with idle-only during the prolonged vocal resting bouts that make the species so audible in Vondelpark and Beatrixpark.

The Rose-ringed Parakeet is now an established Amsterdam bird, with a growing population centred on parks with mature Platanus and other large trees where it excavates or usurps cavity roosts. It forages almost entirely in the canopy — feeding on buds, fruits, and seeds — and moves noisily between trees in small groups, matching the tree-flitter movement exactly. In Amsterdam it is most conspicuous in Vondelpark, Sloterpark, and Beatrixpark. The bird default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter fits without modification.

Barklice (Psocoptera) and bark-dwelling springtails (Collembola) are millimetric arthropods that graze algae, fungal spores, and organic films on the outer bark of Taxus and other urban trees in Amsterdam. They are among the most abundant but least visible bark micro-fauna, forming thin drifts visible only under magnification. Their movement is imperceptible at map scale and they never visit flowers, making the insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber inappropriate. Tree-bark placement with idle-only movement is the correct override for sedentary bark-grazing microarthropods.

Bagworm moths spend most of their larval life encased in a silk-and-debris bag anchored to the foliage or bark of conifers such as Thuja in Amsterdam gardens and parks. The bag-carrying larva moves very slowly along branches and twigs, effectively appearing stationary at map scale, while the wingless adult female never leaves her case at all. Tree-canopy is the dominant placement because bags are most visible hanging from leafy branchlets; tree-bark is secondary for larvae descending to pupate. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber does not apply to a sessile bark-and-foliage feeder, so idle-only is the correct movement override.

The 22-spot Ladybird is a tiny yellow-and-black species whose diet is unusual among ladybirds: it feeds almost exclusively on powdery mildew fungi rather than aphids, and is therefore found low on herbaceous plants, meadow vegetation, and umbellifers rather than in tree canopies. It is a frequent flower-head visitor in parkland and rough ground, where its mildew hosts grow on low plants. Its movement is best described as short bobs between nearby flower heads (flower-bobber dominant) with long idle-only resting periods on leaf surfaces. Overridden from the default flower-visitor/flower-bobber because the secondary habitat is genuinely ground-park rather than tree-canopy.

Pterocallis alni is a specialist aphid restricted to the undersides of Alnus leaves in Amsterdam canal-side and park plantings, where colonies feed on phloem sap from spring through summer. Like all tree-canopy aphids, colonies are sessile once settled — individuals may walk short distances between leaves but this movement is invisible at map scale. The secondary tree-bark tag covers the bark-overwintering phase when eggs are laid on Alnus twigs in autumn. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber does not fit a phloem-sucking leaf aphid, requiring an override to tree-canopy and idle-only.

The Caucasian Wingnut is a large deciduous tree in the walnut family (Juglandaceae), grown as an ornamental street and park tree in Amsterdam. Despite the GBIF taxon_group placing it alongside other plants, it is unambiguously a tree — it reaches 25–30 m tall with a massive spreading crown. The category is overridden to tree to match its biology and to ensure the game engine places it at the tree-rooted coordinate from the city trees dataset. Its long pendulous fruit chains are a distinctive ornamental feature.

Pulvinaria regalis is a soft scale insect that coats the bark of Aesculus (horse chestnut) and Platanus in Amsterdam streets with conspicuous waxy white egg masses in early summer. Adults and nymphs are glued to the bark surface beneath their waxy shield for virtually their entire lives, crawling only millimetres as first-instar crawlers before permanently settling. Bark is the exclusive habitat and movement is below map-scale perception, so tree-bark / idle-only is the precise override. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber has no relevance to a bark-feeding scale insect.

The Firebug is a striking red-and-black true bug that congregates in dense clusters at the base of linden trees and mallow plants, feeding on fallen seeds. It is a ground-dwelling species that barely moves — groups sit motionless in sunny patches on bare soil or leaf litter — so idle-only is strongly appropriate and flower-bobber is entirely wrong. The habitat is ground-park rather than the insect default flower-visitor because firebugs are seed-feeders on the ground, not nectar-seekers on blooms. Amsterdam observations are frequently at the bases of Tilia trees in parks.

The Bullfinch is a stocky, shy finch that visits Amsterdam parks and tree-lined streets in autumn and winter, feeding on buds, berries, and seeds of Fraxinus, Sorbus, and Ulmus. It moves quietly through the mid- to upper canopy, typically in pairs or small family groups, flitting deliberately between trees rather than making long open-air crossings. In Amsterdam it is a scarce but regular winter visitor in places like Vondelpark and the Amsterdamse Bos wherever fruiting Sorbus or seeding ash are present. The bird default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter fits the Bullfinch without modification.

This qualitative grouping covers various moth and butterfly larvae that can feed on Styphnolobium japonicum (Japanese pagoda tree), a relatively toxic leguminous street tree in Amsterdam that most herbivores avoid. The caterpillars feed in the canopy foliage and move slowly along branches — imperceptible at map scale — making idle-only the correct movement class. Tree-canopy is the exclusive habitat because these are foliage-feeding larvae with no association with flowers or bark surfaces. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber does not fit leaf-feeding caterpillars, requiring an override.

The Brown rat is Amsterdam's most abundant and widespread urban mammal, foraging nightly along canal sides, under street furniture, and in pavement gaps throughout the city. Ground-urban is dominant because the bulk of activity and visibility is on paved surfaces near food sources — cafés, markets, bins, and canal banks — while water-edge is secondary because Amsterdam's canal network provides ideal burrowing banks and foraging margins. Urban-walker fits the short-distance scurrying along streets and alleys, with park-roamer as secondary for rats that range into park green. The mammal default of ground-park / park-roamer understates the urban pavement dominance for this species.

The Firecrest is a scarce but regular winter visitor to Amsterdam, favouring dense evergreen plantings of Chamaecyparis and similar conifers in parks and cemetery gardens. Like its close relative the Goldcrest, it is an energetic needle-gleaner that barely pauses between tree canopies, perfectly fitting the tree-flitter animation. It is smaller than a Blue Tit and even more hyperactive, flicking through foliage at short intervals and rarely descending to ground level. The bird default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter accurately represents this species without modification.

The Goldcrest is Europe's smallest bird and a conifer specialist strongly associated with Cedrus, Chamaecyparis, Metasequoia, Picea, Taxus, and Thuja in Amsterdam parks and private gardens. It forages ceaselessly through dense needle canopies hunting micro-insects and spiders, making rapid short flits between branches every few seconds. Amsterdam records show it present year-round in mature conifer stands in Vondelpark, the Hortus Botanicus, and larger private gardens. The bird default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter is an exact fit for this species; no override is needed.

This grouping covers two very small birds — the Goldcrest and the Coal Tit — that are closely associated with conifers, particularly Larix in Amsterdam parks. Both species are restless canopy foragers, working through needle clusters hunting tiny insects and spiders, and making short flitting hops between branches and nearby trees at high frequency. The Coal Tit joins mixed tit flocks in autumn and winter, while the Goldcrest is an active year-round conifer specialist; both are well served by the bird default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter without any modification.

The dogwood gall midge lays its eggs in the leaf margins and shoot tips of Cornus, inducing distinctive pod-like leaf-roll galls in the canopy of ornamental Cornus trees used in Amsterdam street plantings and parks. The larvae develop entirely within the gall tissue and are immobile; the adult midges are tiny and short-lived. Tree-canopy is the correct placement because galls form on foliage, and idle-only captures the effectively stationary appearance of gall-bound larvae. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber does not apply to a gall-inducing leaf miner, requiring the override.

The Walnut husk fly is a specialist fruit fly whose larvae develop inside the green husk of Juglans nuts, staining the kernel black and rendering it inedible. In Amsterdam it has established on ornamental and productive walnut trees, with adults resting cryptically on foliage in the canopy and larvae spending their entire feeding phase inside the ripening fruit. Adults do occasionally visit flowers for nectar but their defining ecological role is canopy-based oviposition on ripening fruit, so tree-canopy and idle-only better represent the animal than the insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber.

The Common Red Soldier Beetle is one of Amsterdam's most conspicuous summer beetles, with its bright orange-red elytra and black tip making it immediately recognisable on umbellifers — hogweed, angelica, and cow parsley — where adults feed on pollen and small insects during July and August. Mating pairs are frequently seen on flower heads, making this genuinely a flower-visitor insect rather than a pure herbivore. The insect default flower-visitor / flower-bobber fits the dominant adult behaviour well; idle-only is added as a secondary movement because adults also spend long periods sitting motionlessly on flowers or stems. Ground-park is a secondary habitat reflecting the larval stage which lives in soil hunting worms and other invertebrates.

The Mottled Shieldbug is a large, grey-mottled pentatomid bug that feeds on the seeds and foliage of trees — particularly limes, elms, and oaks — making it a genuine tree-canopy species. It overwinters as an adult in bark crevices or leaf litter and resumes feeding in spring when new growth appears. In Amsterdam it is most likely to be seen on the foliage of large park and street trees. The default flower-visitor and flower-bobber tags are incorrect; this is a tree-dwelling plant-feeder with essentially static adult behaviour. idle-only captures its typical motionless resting posture on leaves.

Rhinophora lepida is a small tachinid-family blowfly (Calliphoridae) whose adults are flower visitors, frequently found on umbellifers and composite flowers in parks and meadow margins. Its larvae are parasitoids of woodlice, which are abundant in damp ground-park habitats, so the adult is closely tied to vegetated open ground where both host and flower are available. The dominant habitat is ground-park rather than flower-visitor because adult sightings are often on or near ground-level vegetation rather than in the canopy. Movement is flower-bobber since adults make short flights between flower heads when foraging for nectar.

This grouping covers two moth species associated with Ilex aquifolium in Amsterdam: Rhopobota naevana (holly tortrix), whose larvae spin and roll holly leaves in the canopy, and Acasis viretata (yellow-barred brindle), a geometrid whose caterpillars feed on holly flowers and foliage. Both are canopy-bound in their larval stage and rest cryptically on foliage as adults, barely moving at map scale. They are leaf- and flower-tissue feeders rather than flower visitors in the pollinator sense, so the insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber does not apply. Tree-canopy and idle-only are the correct override.

Rhynchaenus fagi is a tiny jumping weevil (2–3 mm) whose larvae mine blister-like galleries in beech leaves, producing characteristic brown blotches visible from below Fagus canopies in Amsterdam parks such as the Amsterdamse Bos. Adults overwinter in leaf litter and ascend to the canopy in spring to feed and oviposit; their distinctive leaf-mines persist through summer. The entire life cycle is canopy-centred on Fagus foliage, with no association with flowers or open ground, making tree-canopy / idle-only the correct override from the insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber.

The Sand Martin is a small aerial insectivore that arrives in Amsterdam in spring and hunts low over open water and wetlands, catching insects on the wing in sweeping curved flights. It nests colonially in sandy riverbanks and canal embankments, making water-edge a genuine secondary habitat where it perches briefly near nest entrances. Almost all visible time is spent in continuous aerial loops (sky-looper dominant), with brief idle perches at nest holes or wires (idle-only secondary). Overridden from the default tree-canopy/tree-flitter because this species barely interacts with trees at all; it is a true sky bird that shares the sky-only + sky-looper profile of the swift family.

This grouping represents the mixed assemblage of small songbirds — tits, finches, sparrows, and thrushes — that use dense Cupressocyparis hedges and conifers in Amsterdam gardens as communal winter roosts after dark. During roosting hours the birds are entirely still, huddled in the foliage for warmth, so idle-only replaces the usual tree-flitter movement. Tree-canopy placement in a nearby evergreen is correct for a roosting group; the birds are not flitting but are genuinely present and stationary in the canopy. This is a nocturnal / crepuscular phenomenon, so the override from tree-flitter to idle-only is necessary.

Broad-leaved Dock is one of the Netherlands' most ubiquitous plants, thriving in Amsterdam parks, roadsides, pavement cracks, canal banks, and disturbed ground wherever soil is enriched with nitrogen. Its large paddle-shaped leaves are the traditional folk remedy for nettle stings, and in autumn the rusty red seed spikes are a conspicuous sight along urban paths. The plant is a true generalist of both vegetated and paved ground, warranting a ground-urban secondary alongside the dominant ground-park habitat. Movement is none as for all plants.

The Zebra Spider is a small but conspicuous jumping spider named for its bold black-and-white striped abdomen, most commonly seen hunting on sunlit exterior walls, window sills, fences, and paved surfaces in urban Amsterdam. It is a well-known city spider that actively stalks small flies by day, rotating its large forward-facing eyes to track prey before leaping. The default anywhere / idle-only partially fits, but wall-and-roof is more precise as the dominant habitat given its near-obligate use of built vertical surfaces. The BEHAVIOR_TAXONOMY notes it explicitly as a wall-and-roof example.

Elder is a fast-growing shrub or small tree found at the edges of parks, canal banks, waste ground, and beneath taller trees throughout Amsterdam; it is categorised as plant rather than tree in GBIF because it is a shrub-forming species. It produces flat-topped clusters of white flowers in June and purple-black berries in autumn, both highly attractive to insects and birds. The plant default of ground-park and none fits its placement in park margins and hedgerow edges. No override is needed.

Flesh flies (Sarcophaga spp.) are viviparous flies whose larvae develop in carrion, excrement, or rotting organic matter; adults are common on urban pavements, rubbish areas, sunlit walls, and any exposed organic material in the city. They are not flower foragers, so the insect defaults of flower-visitor and flower-bobber are replaced with ground-urban and urban-walker, reflecting the characteristic pattern of short walks across paved surfaces followed by pauses. The genus-level identification means this covers multiple sibling species, all with similar urban ecology.

The White-letter Hairstreak is a specialist butterfly almost entirely dependent on Ulmus, whose larvae feed on elm flowers and young leaves in the canopy in spring. Adults spend most of their time resting on elm foliage and visiting aphid honeydew high in the canopy, rarely descending, so tree-canopy is the dominant habitat. Flower-visitor is a secondary because adults do take nectar from bramble, privet, and other flowers when these are accessible near elms. Movement is primarily idle-only (perching motionless for long periods on leaves) with flower-bobber as a secondary for the brief nectaring excursions — a different profile from the insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber alone.

Schoenobius gigantella is a large crambid moth whose larvae are specialist stem-borers in common reed (Phragmites australis) and other emergent water-margin grasses. The entire lifecycle is tied to reedbed and water-edge habitats — adults rest on reed stems during the day, camouflaged against the pale stems. Water-edge is the correct habitat override from the insect default of flower-visitor, since this species is strictly associated with emergent marginal vegetation rather than open flowers. Adults are day-resting on reed stems, making idle-only the right movement class. It is recorded in Amsterdam in the Amstelpark reedbeds and along canal margins with standing Phragmites.

The Eastern Grey Squirrel has established itself in Amsterdam parks, particularly in areas with mature Quercus, Platanus, Aesculus, and Ulmus, where it is now the dominant squirrel in some locations. It is highly arboreal, leaping confidently between canopies and descending frequently to bury and retrieve food caches in park lawns, making tree-canopy dominant and ground-park secondary. Tree-flitter fits the arboreal phase well, and park-roamer captures the characteristic stop-start ground foraging. The mammal default of ground-park / park-roamer alone misses the equal arboreal importance; both habitat and movement need a two-value override.

This entry records squirrel activity in a single Ginkgo tree in Amsterdam, likely Grey Squirrel given the current Amsterdam population distribution. Squirrels use Ginkgo for its fruit (the nut inside the malodorous outer seed coat) in autumn, foraging in the canopy and on the ground beneath the tree. Tree-canopy is dominant as the feeding and travel phase; ground-park is secondary for the nut-caching and retrieval on park lawns. Tree-flitter and park-roamer cover the respective movement phases. The mammal default of ground-park / park-roamer alone omits the arboreal component, requiring the same override applied to other squirrel entries.

This pairing covers both the native Eurasian Red Squirrel (now very rare in Amsterdam) and the introduced Grey Squirrel, observed together in stands of Juglans, Picea, Pinus, and Pterocarya. Both species share an essentially identical ecology for placement purposes: dominant time in the canopy leaping between conifers and large deciduous trees, with regular descents to cache and retrieve nuts on park ground. Tree-canopy is dominant and ground-park secondary for habitat; tree-flitter and park-roamer cover the two movement phases. The mammal default of ground-park alone does not capture the strongly arboreal lifestyle, requiring the override.

This pairing groups grey squirrels with bank voles, both recorded in the same diverse tree stands (Castanea, Cedrus, Nyssa, Taxus, and others) in Amsterdam parks and wooded areas. Squirrels are the dominant visual element, spending much of their time in the canopy leaping between branches, with frequent descents to the ground to cache food — hence tree-canopy dominant and ground-park secondary. Bank voles are ground-dwelling and nocturnal, better captured by the park-roamer secondary. Tree-flitter is the dominant animation for the squirrel aerial phase, and park-roamer covers both vole ground movement and squirrel foraging on the grass. The mammal default of ground-park / park-roamer alone misses the arboreal component, requiring an override.

Elm bark beetles bore directly into the bark and cambium of Ulmus trees, spending almost their entire adult and larval life beneath or on the bark surface rather than visiting flowers. In Amsterdam they are associated with the many surviving elm street trees, where the galleries they carve under the bark are the primary sign of their presence. They are also the main vector of Dutch elm disease fungus, making them ecologically significant beyond their feeding habits. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber does not apply; tree-bark captures their anchor point and idle-only reflects the minimal surface movement between gallery entrances.

Scoparia ambigualis is a small grey crambid moth whose larvae feed in mosses and liverworts on tree trunks and damp walls. Adults rest cryptically pressed flat against bark or lichen-covered surfaces during the day, making them nearly invisible. In Amsterdam they are recorded from parks with old trees and moss-covered walls. The default flower-visitor/flower-bobber combination does not apply; this moth never visits flowers and spends its adult life motionless on bark surfaces. Habitat overridden to tree-bark (dominant) with ground-park secondary; movement overridden to idle-only.

Crown Vetch is a sprawling, scrambling perennial legume with attractive pink-and-white bicoloured flowers, originally from southern Europe but widely naturalised in Amsterdam on road verges, railway banks, and park edges. It fixes atmospheric nitrogen via root nodules, allowing it to thrive on poor soils and outcompete native flora in disturbed habitats. The flowers are visited by bumblebees and other long-tongued bees from June through August. Its vigorous rhizomatous spread means it can form extensive ground-covering patches in amenity grassland and urban green margins. The default ground-park placement is appropriate for most Amsterdam sightings.

Narrow-leaved Ragwort is an invasive South African neophyte that has spread rapidly across the Netherlands along railway lines, roadsides, and industrial sites, strongly favouring the dry, compacted, low-competition substrates of urban infrastructure. Its bright yellow daisy flowers appear almost year-round in mild winters, making it conspicuous on Amsterdam's pavement cracks, rail sidings, and tarmac margins. Ground-urban is the dominant habitat as it is fundamentally a pioneer of built environments rather than park vegetation. It can be mildly toxic to livestock but thrives unchecked in city environments away from grazing pressure. The default ground-park is retained as a secondary because it also occurs in neglected park margins.

The Brown Chafer is a small, nocturnally active scarab beetle widespread in Amsterdam's parks and sandy green spaces where its larvae spend two or three years feeding on grass roots and decaying organic matter in the soil. Adults emerge in summer on warm evenings and fly to deciduous trees and shrubs to feed on foliage, but during daylight hours they bury into loose soil or remain hidden at the base of vegetation. The larval stage — lasting the vast majority of the life cycle — is entirely subterranean in park lawns, anchoring this species firmly in ground-park. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber does not fit a largely nocturnal, soil-larval chafer beetle; idle-only captures the cryptic daytime resting behaviour of the adult.

The hornet moth is a clearwing moth whose larvae tunnel deep into the base and roots of Populus trees, spending two to three years inside the wood before the adult emerges. Adults are mimics of hornets and rest cryptically on bark or low stems near their emergence holes; they do not visit flowers in any meaningful way. In Amsterdam poplars are widespread along canal banks and park edges, giving the hornet moth a consistent set of host trees. Tree-bark is the correct habitat class because the larval galleries and adult emergence are both anchored to the trunk surface; idle-only reflects the adult's tendency to rest still on bark rather than move actively.

The Ferruginous Bee-grabber is a conopid fly whose adults are faithful flower visitors, feeding on nectar from umbellifers and composites while ambushing bumblebees in which they lay their internal parasitoid eggs. In Amsterdam it is observed on patches of wildflowers in parks and rough urban ground, where it perches on flower heads or nearby vegetation waiting for bee hosts. Its movement on the map is appropriately flower-bobber as it moves between nearby blooms, with idle-only resting bouts between forays. The default flower-visitor/flower-bobber fits well and no override is needed.

Red Campion is a familiar, cheerful woodland-edge and parkland plant producing vivid deep-pink flowers from April through October in Amsterdam's parks, cemetery margins, and shaded verges. It is dioecious, with separate male and female plants, and is primarily pollinated by long-tongued bumblebees and butterflies. It thrives in the partial shade and moist, fertile soils typical of Amsterdam's older park plantings and tree-lined canal sides. Hybridisation with the rarer White Campion can produce pale pink intermediate forms. The default ground-park placement is accurate for virtually all Amsterdam observations.

White Campion is a tall annual or short-lived perennial of disturbed ground, road verges, wasteland, and park margins, producing large white flowers that open in the evening and are pollinated by moths. It is a ground-level plant with a preference for recently disturbed or nutrient-enriched soils — common on the edges of Amsterdam construction sites, canal bank restorations, and park border treatments. The plant default (ground-park, none) fits exactly and no override is needed.

Hedge Mustard is a tall, wiry annual mustard with small yellow flowers and slender upward-pointing seed pods, one of the most characteristic weeds of Amsterdam's pavements, building bases, and disturbed urban ground. It thrives on compacted, nitrogen-rich urban soil — appearing in pavement cracks, at the foot of walls, and on trodden path edges — making ground-urban the more accurate dominant habitat than the plant default ground-park. It is edible and was historically used as a remedy for voice problems (the "singers' plant"). Overridden to swap the dominant habitat to ground-urban reflecting its strongly urban ecology.

Both the Eurasian nuthatch and the great spotted woodpecker forage almost exclusively on bark and woody stems, probing crevices and excavating for invertebrates rather than flitting between canopy foliage like a typical songbird. They are recorded on the Corylus and other mature deciduous trees in Amsterdam's parks such as Vondelpark and the Amsterdamse Bos. The bird default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter is wrong for these bark-specialists; tree-bark places them on the trunk and larger branches where they actually operate. Idle-only captures their characteristic freeze-in-place posture on vertical bark rather than any continuous locomotion.

This composite entry covers the small mammals most commonly associated with Morus and other fruiting trees in Amsterdam parks: urban foxes foraging beneath them, grey squirrels and red squirrels climbing into the canopy for fruit, and wood mice gleaning fallen mast from the ground.
