The dominant habitat is ground-park because foxes and mice are predominantly ground-level; a secondary tree-canopy tag captures the squirrel component that genuinely climbs. Park-roamer is the right movement class for the fox and mouse element and adequately represents the group; squirrel acrobatics can be folded into the same category given the small sprite scale.

The caterpillars of both the eyed hawk-moth and the puss moth feed openly on willow and poplar foliage, clinging to leaves and shoots in the tree canopy throughout their larval phase. Adult eyed hawk-moths and puss moths are night-flying and rest motionless on bark or foliage during the day, neither visiting flowers nor moving actively in daylight. Salix trees along Amsterdam's canal banks provide abundant larval habitat. Tree-canopy is correct because the larvae spend their entire feeding period in the foliage; idle-only reflects the cryptic resting posture of both adults and older larvae.

Prickly Sow-thistle is a common annual weed of pavements, wall bases, rubble ground, and urban disturbed habitats, as well as park edges and allotments. In Amsterdam it is one of the most abundant pavement-crack plants, found growing from joins in paving stones and at the base of walls throughout the inner city, making ground-urban the dominant habitat override from the plant default of ground-park alone. Ground-park is a genuine secondary since it also appears in parks and rough ground. Movement is none as for all plants. The species is ecologically important as an early-season pollen source for short-tongued bees.

Smooth Sow-thistle is an annual weed of disturbed, ruderal ground: pavement cracks, base-of-wall strips, waste sites, and garden edges throughout Amsterdam. It is predominantly an urban-ground coloniser rather than a park meadow plant, so ground-urban replaces the plant default of ground-park. The species germinates rapidly in any break in hard surfacing and is abundant along Amsterdam's street margins and canal-side walls. Movement remains none.

The Common Globetail is one of Amsterdam's most abundant and readily-identified hoverflies — a slim, wasp-mimic syrphid with yellow-and-black banding. Adults are avid flower visitors throughout the season, hovering over and nectaring on umbellifers, composites, and open flowers across parks, gardens, and canal-side planting. The insect defaults (flower-visitor, flower-bobber) apply exactly. Larvae are aphidophagous in plant stems. It is present in Amsterdam from April through October and is typically the commonest hoverfly in any urban flower survey.

Sphaerophoria is a genus of slender hoverflies whose adults are among the most reliable flower visitors in Amsterdam's parks and canal-side vegetation, hovering almost stationary over flower heads before darting to the next bloom. The larvae of most species are aphid predators in low herbaceous vegetation. Adults appear from spring through autumn and are particularly associated with yellow composites, umbellifers, and open sunny patches. The insect default of flower-visitor and flower-bobber fits perfectly; no override is needed for this genus-level record.

The pine hawk-moth lays its eggs on Pinus needles and the caterpillars feed gregariously in the canopy, perfectly camouflaged against the bundled needles. Adults are nocturnal and spend the day pressed flat against pine bark, resting invisibly. Amsterdam's ornamental and planted pines in parks such as Amsterdamse Bos provide the larval host; the species is uncommon but recorded. Tree-canopy is correct for the larval feeding stage, which dominates the observable part of the life cycle; idle-only reflects both the motionless daytime adult resting on bark and the slow deliberate feeding of the caterpillar.

The Eurasian siskin is an acrobatic finch that feeds primarily on the seed catkins of alder and birch, hanging from thin outer twigs while extracting seeds; it also uses larch and spruce in Amsterdam's planted groves. Flocks move between trees through the canopy in the classic flitting pattern of small passerines, making tree-canopy and tree-flitter the correct defaults. In Amsterdam siskins are most reliably seen in winter and early spring when alder and birch catkins are plentiful along park waterways. The bird default fits without any override needed.

Marsh Woundwort is a tall, hairy perennial herb of damp ground, growing characteristically along canal banks, ditch edges, and wet meadow margins throughout Amsterdam. Its preference for waterlogged or seasonally wet soil means water-edge is a more accurate dominant habitat than the plant-default ground-park, though it does also colonise moist patches in parks and rough ground. The pink-purple flowers on whorled spikes are popular with bumblebees in summer. It is rhizomatous and can form dense stands along canal margins, making it a visually distinctive bankside plant.

Hedge Woundwort is a robust perennial herb of shaded, moist ground in woodland edges, hedgerows, and park margins throughout Amsterdam. It produces distinctive deep-purple hooded flowers on tall square stems in summer, attracting various bumblebees and solitary bees. The plant spreads by underground rhizomes and forms patches in the understorey of city parks and along canal-side vegetation. The default ground-park / none combination fits perfectly: it is a non-tree vascular plant rooted in vegetated soil that does not move.

The ash weevil feeds on the buds and young leaves of Fraxinus in spring, and the larvae develop inside the leaflets where they cause characteristic leaf-roll damage. Adults are small and cryptic, sitting still on ash foliage or bark rather than visiting flowers. Amsterdam has many ash street trees and park specimens, and the weevil is regularly recorded on them. Tree-canopy replaces the insect default of flower-visitor because the entire life cycle is tied to ash foliage; idle-only replaces flower-bobber because the weevil's movement is negligible at the scale of a map sprite.

The Common Tern is a migratory seabird that breeds on pontoons and artificial platforms on Amsterdam's larger open waterways including the IJ and surrounding lakes. It spends most of its visible time hovering and plunge-diving over open water, making sky-only the dominant placement. Brief rests occur on buoys and floating platforms at water level. Its rapid, buoyant flight with deep wingbeats is very distinctive, and its piercing calls draw attention well above the canal surface. In Amsterdam it is most abundant from late April through August during the breeding season.

Stigmella microtheriella is a tiny nepticulid moth whose larvae mine the leaves of Carpinus (hornbeam), creating narrow sinuous galleries visible as pale trails through the leaf blade. The adult moths are minute, nocturnal, and rest motionless on leaf surfaces or bark during the day. Hornbeam is well-represented in Amsterdam parks and avenues, providing reliable habitat. Tree-canopy is the right class because the leaf-mining habit keeps the organism entirely in the foliage layer; idle-only reflects both the motionless adult and the slow creeping of the leaf-bound larva.

Stomaphis yanonis is an unusually large bark-feeding aphid that inserts its long stylet into the phloem of Zelkova bark rather than feeding on leaf tissue; colonies aggregate on the trunk and major branches beneath ant tents. Zelkova is a planted street and park tree in Amsterdam, and this aphid is an uncommon but notable associate. Tree-bark is correct because the colonies are anchored to the trunk surface rather than the canopy foliage; idle-only reflects the completely sessile colony life of phloem-feeding Stomaphis. A photo of a colony on Zelkova bark showing the ants tending them would best represent this species.

This composite entry groups cavity-nesting and bark-associated birds that use Fraxinus and other mature trees with natural hollows or decayed sections in Amsterdam parks. The tawny owl roosts and nests in tree cavities, largely motionless by day; the common redstart perches in canopy foliage and sallies for insects; the nuthatch and woodpeckers work the bark surface for invertebrates. Tree-canopy is dominant because the canopy-perching and cavity-entrance activity is most visible; tree-bark is the secondary because nuthatch and woodpecker spend real time on trunk surfaces. Idle-only is dominant reflecting the owl's daytime roost; tree-flitter captures the redstart's active flitting between foliage perches.

The European starling is one of Amsterdam's most abundant urban birds, foraging in noisy flocks on lawns, pavements, and squares while also roosting and breeding in tree cavities and roof spaces. It visits fruiting Morus and Nyssa trees to gorge on berries, giving it a genuine tree-canopy secondary, but the dominant activity visible on streets and parks is ground foraging. Ground-urban is the primary habitat because pavement and lawn foraging is what observers see most; urban-walker captures the characteristic striding gait, with tree-flitter as secondary for the berry-feeding and roost-approach flights.

Wild boar root for mast beneath Fagus and Robinia in forested park areas, turning over the soil surface with their snouts in classic park-roamer fashion. In Amsterdam they are occasional visitors to the edges of the Amsterdamse Bos from the surrounding polder landscape rather than permanent residents, but their association with beech mast makes the park setting correct. Ground-park and park-roamer are both accurate defaults for a large root-foraging mammal. The species is unlikely to be encountered in the inner city but is plausible in larger green zones recorded in the iNaturalist dataset.

The Eurasian blackcap is a common and conspicuous warbler in Amsterdam parks and gardens, singing loudly from shrubby canopy edges and feeding on insects in foliage as well as berries from Amelanchier and other fruiting shrubs in autumn. It is an active forager that moves continuously between perches in the canopy layer, fitting the tree-flitter movement perfectly. Both migratory birds passing through and an increasing number of overwintering birds are recorded in Amsterdam. The bird default of tree-canopy / tree-flitter fits without any override.

Hoverflies are among the most important pollinators of Amsterdam's urban trees and flowering plants, hovering at the flowers of Acer, Tilia, Aesculus, and Magnolia in spring and summer and moving between blooms with characteristic hovering pauses. Lacewings share the flower-visitor niche as adults but are also predators of aphids in the canopy; their inclusion here is as co-observers on the same tree taxa. The insect default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is an accurate fit for the dominant syrphid behavior. Both groups are abundant across all green areas of Amsterdam throughout the warmer months.

Tansy is a tall, strongly aromatic perennial herb native to temperate Europe that grows in rough grassland, roadsides, canal banks, and disturbed ground across Amsterdam. Its flat-topped clusters of bright yellow button flowers are a late-summer landmark in parks, railway margins, and brownfield sites. The plant spreads vigorously by rhizomes and can form dense stands. The ground-park/none defaults apply directly and require no override.

The yew gall midge induces distinctive artichoke-like galls in the growing tips of Taxus shoots; the larva develops entirely within the gall in the canopy of the yew tree and overwinters inside it. Adults are tiny and short-lived, emerging to mate and lay eggs without any meaningful flower-visiting activity. Taxus is widely planted in Amsterdam as a hedge and ornamental tree in parks and cemeteries. Tree-canopy is the correct habitat because the galls form on the shoot tips in the foliage; idle-only replaces flower-bobber because the gall-bound larva and resting adult are both effectively motionless.

Croesus septentrionalis and related hazel sawflies feed on the leaves of Corylus in the canopy, with the larvae often arching their abdomens in unison as a defense display when disturbed. Adults are small and dark, sitting on foliage or stems without any meaningful flower-visiting behavior. Corylus is present as an understory tree and ornamental in Amsterdam parks and hedgerows. Tree-canopy is the correct habitat because the entire observable life stage is on the foliage; idle-only is appropriate because the larvae are slow feeders and the adults rest motionless between short flights.

Tenthredinid sawflies lay their eggs into the leaf tissue of Cornus and related trees, and the larvae feed gregariously on foliage in the tree canopy, often stripping individual branches. Adults are small wasp-like insects that rest on leaves or stems rather than visiting flowers. Cornus is a common ornamental shrub-tree in Amsterdam parks and garden plantings. Tree-canopy is correct because larval feeding is confined to the foliage layer; idle-only replaces flower-bobber because sawfly adults do not feed on flowers in any significant way and the larvae are slow leaf-feeders.

Spider mites of the family Tetranychidae colonize the leaves of a very wide range of host trees — in this dataset including Cupressocyparis, Ginkgo, Gleditsia, Koelreuteria, Liquidambar, Metasequoia, Pterocarya, and Thuja — feeding by piercing leaf cells and causing characteristic bronzing or stippling. They are too small to be visible to the naked eye individually but their silken webbing and feeding damage on foliage are diagnostic. The arachnid default of anywhere / idle-only needs adjustment; tree-canopy is the correct habitat because all of these taxa are strict leaf-feeders in the foliage layer, and idle-only is retained because spider mites are effectively motionless at sprite scale.

Tetranychus urticae is one of the world's most polyphagous pest mites, feeding on the undersides of leaves of Cercidiphyllum, Parrotia, Paulownia, Robinia, and many other trees by puncturing epidermal cells and withdrawing the contents, producing a fine webbing over the affected foliage in warm dry summers. In Amsterdam it is especially prominent on ornamental trees during drought periods. Tree-canopy overrides the arachnid default of anywhere because all feeding and reproduction occurs on leaf surfaces in the canopy; idle-only is retained as the mite's movement is invisible at sprite scale.

The larch longhorn beetle lays its eggs under the bark of weakened or recently felled Larix, and the larvae bore extensive galleries through the cambium and sapwood of the trunk. Adults emerge through round exit holes in the bark and rest on the bark surface or nearby timber; they do not visit flowers. Larix is a common planted conifer in Amsterdam parks including the Amsterdamse Bos. Tree-bark is the correct habitat class because the entire larval development and adult emergence is anchored to the bark surface; idle-only replaces flower-bobber as the adult's activity is limited to resting and short searching flights close to the host tree.

The Great Green Bush-cricket is a large, predatory katydid that lives in tall herb margins, shrubs, and the lower canopy of trees in parks and urban green corridors. It is not a flower feeder: adults are partly predatory and partly herbivorous, spending most of their time sitting motionless in vegetation waiting to ambush prey or gnaw plant matter. The default flower-visitor / flower-bobber insect pairing is wrong. Ground-park is dominant (tall herbaceous vegetation and shrub margins), tree-canopy secondary (it regularly climbs into lower branches); idle-only captures the cryptic, still resting posture that makes it so hard to spot despite its size.

The Toothed Weaver is a funnel-web spider in the family Agelenidae that is strongly associated with old stone walls, rock faces, and the base of building walls rather than open ground. It builds a distinctive flat, slightly funnelled sheet web in crevices in masonry and at the junction of walls and paving. Wall-and-roof is therefore the appropriate habitat override from the arachnid default of anywhere. It is completely sedentary in its web, making idle-only the correct and default movement class. In Amsterdam it turns up in canal-wall crevices and on the rough stonework of older buildings.

The pine processionary moth is a significant public-health pest whose urticating-haired caterpillars live communally in large white silk nests in the crowns of Pinus trees, descending in distinctive nose-to-tail processions to pupate in spring. The nests in the upper canopy are the most visible sign of the species. In Amsterdam and the broader Dutch urban landscape it has been expanding northward with climate warming and is now recorded on planted pines. Tree-canopy is the correct habitat because the nests and larval feeding are in the pine crown; idle-only captures the colony's predominantly stationary nest-bound existence, with the processionary march being an occasional behavior not warranting a separate movement class.

Thelephoraceae are ectomycorrhizal fungi that form obligate symbiotic associations with the roots of Cedrus and other conifers, with their mycelium extending through the soil beneath the tree rather than on the bark surface. Small leathery or disc-shaped fruitbodies appear at ground level near the base of host trees, not on the trunk. In Amsterdam Cedrus is planted as an ornamental in parks and the ectomycorrhizal community beneath them can include Thelephora and related genera. Ground-park overrides the fungus default of tree-bark because the fruitbodies emerge from the soil rather than on the trunk surface; none is retained as the correct movement class for all fungi.

Thereva nobilitata is a stiletto fly (Therevidae) whose adults are typically seen basking on bare ground, sandy soil, or low vegetation in sunny clearings and park edges. Unlike nectar-focused pollinators, stiletto fly adults either do not feed or take minimal fluid, so flower-visitor and flower-bobber are inappropriate defaults. The larvae are predatory in the soil, further anchoring this species to ground-park. Adults remain motionless for long periods when basking, making idle-only a better fit than flower-bobber. It is an occasional but genuine Amsterdam recorder in dry, open park habitats.

Tinocallis platani and related woolly elm aphids colonize the upper surfaces of Ulmus leaves in large colonies, producing characteristic leaf curl and copious honeydew that drips onto surfaces below the tree — a familiar nuisance under Amsterdam's many elm street trees. Eriosoma species produce woolly galls on elm leaf margins. Both genera are confined to the canopy foliage and do not visit flowers. Tree-canopy is the correct habitat; idle-only replaces flower-bobber because aphid colonies are sessile feeders anchored to their leaf sites.

The pine bark beetle bores through the outer bark of Pinus to construct brood galleries in the phloem; adults also feed by mining out the pith of young pine shoots for maturation feeding, causing characteristic shoot death called "top-shot." The entire life cycle from larva to adult is spent in or very close to the bark surface. Amsterdam's planted pines provide host material, and the beetle is a primary colonizer of stressed or storm-damaged pine trees. Tree-bark is the correct habitat because all feeding and reproduction is on or immediately under the bark; idle-only replaces flower-bobber as there is no flower-visiting in this species.

Tortricid and geometrid moths encompass many species whose caterpillars roll, tie, or graze leaves in the canopy of a wide range of host trees; on Parrotia and Paulownia the larvae feed in the foliage layer during spring. Adult moths of both families are night-flying and rest cryptically on bark or foliage by day without visiting flowers. Tree-canopy is the correct habitat class because the caterpillar stage — the primary observable phase — is spent feeding on leaf tissue in the canopy; idle-only replaces flower-bobber because the resting adults are motionless and the larvae are slow leaf-rollers.

This entry covers the larval stage of generalist tortrix and geometrid moths found feeding on Liquidambar foliage in Amsterdam parks. Liquidambar styraciflua is a popular ornamental tree whose star-shaped leaves are grazed by a range of polyphagous caterpillars that web or roll the leaves for shelter. The adult moths are night-flying and rest motionless during the day, so neither the larval nor adult stage involves flower-visiting. Tree-canopy is the appropriate habitat because the caterpillars feed in the foliage; idle-only reflects the slow, cryptic feeding style of leaf-rolling caterpillars and the static resting adults.

The tulip-tree scale is a soft scale insect in the family Coccidae that anchors itself to the bark and young stems of Liriodendron tulipifera, feeding by tapping phloem sap through its stylet. Mature females are large, hemispherical, and orange-brown, forming prominent colonies on the bark of ornamental tulip trees. Liriodendron is planted as a specimen tree in Amsterdam parks and avenues. Tree-bark is the correct habitat because scale insects are entirely bark-surface organisms that never move appreciably once settled; idle-only reflects their completely sessile adult life, overriding the insect flower-visitor default.

Buprestid leaf-miners of the Trachys type lay eggs on the surface of Zelkova leaves and the larvae mine the leaf tissue, creating characteristic blotch mines visible as discoloured patches on the upper leaf surface. Adults are small, jewel-like beetles that bask on foliage in sunshine but do not visit flowers. Zelkova serrata is increasingly planted as a street and park tree in Amsterdam as an elm replacement. Tree-canopy is the correct habitat because both larval mining and adult basking are on the leaf surface in the canopy; idle-only replaces flower-bobber because neither adult nor larva has any flower-visiting behavior. A photo suggestion for this species without a photo: close-up of a Zelkova leaf showing blotch mines, or an adult beetle on a leaf in sunlight.

Goat's-beard is a perennial grassland composite recognisable by its large yellow flower heads — which close by midday, earning the folk name "Jack-go-to-bed-at-noon" — and its spectacular dandelion-like seed clock that can reach 10 cm across. It grows in meadow grassland, road verges, park lawns, and rough grass along canal banks throughout Amsterdam. It is a ground-level plant with no canopy or aquatic associations. The plant default (ground-park, none) fits exactly and no override is needed.

The Wren, Dunnock, and Robin are common small passerines in Amsterdam parks, gardens, and woodland edges. All three forage close to the ground or in low shrubby vegetation, with the Robin and Dunnock particularly known for hopping along the ground beneath hedges and in leaf litter. While they perch in tree canopy and understorey, they regularly descend to park ground to hunt invertebrates. The ground-park secondary habitat captures their frequent ground-level foraging, and park-roamer secondary movement reflects their low-hopping style, distinguishing them from pure canopy flitters.

Tuber magnatum is the prized white truffle, a subterranean mycorrhizal fungus that forms associations with the roots of deciduous trees including hornbeam (Ostrya). In Amsterdam it would grow belowground in the soil near its tree host, making it a ground-park organism rather than the bark-surface default for fungi. It is entirely sessile — a static subterranean body — so none is the correct movement class. The override from tree-bark to ground-park reflects that truffles fruit underground, not on bark surfaces.

Oak aphids (Tuberculatus, Lachnus, and Thelaxes) are sap-sucking insects that feed on the leaves and young twigs of Quercus in Amsterdam's urban parks. They are sessile organisms that sit fixed on leaf undersides or young shoots, barely moving over the course of a day. The dominant habitat is tree-canopy (leaf and twig feeding) with tree-bark as a secondary for bark-dwelling Lachnus species. Movement is idle-only since their positional motion is imperceptibly slow; flower-bobber default does not apply to these bark/leaf feeders.

Tuberolachnus salignus is one of the world's largest aphids, forming dense colonies on the bark of willow (Salix) stems and branches in Amsterdam's canal-side and park willows. Chaitophorus spp. feed on willow leaves in the canopy. Both are essentially stationary organisms — colonies cluster in one spot for days or weeks. Tree-bark is the dominant habitat for Tuberolachnus which prefers woody stems, with tree-canopy secondary for the leaf-feeding Chaitophorus component. Idle-only overrides the flower-bobber default since these aphids do not visit flowers.

Redwing (Turdus iliacus) and Fieldfare (Turdus pilaris) are winter thrushes that arrive in Amsterdam from October onwards, stripping hawthorn (Crataegus) and other berry-bearing trees. They often travel in mixed flocks, alternating between fruiting tree canopies and foraging on grassy ground in parks and verges. The tree-canopy habitat covers their berry feeding and the ground-park secondary captures their earthworm-hunting on lawns. Park-roamer secondary movement fits the Fieldfare's particularly active ground-walking style.

This row covers the full Turdus guild associated with Nyssa trees in Amsterdam — including resident Blackbirds and winter-visiting thrushes. They are attracted to the dark berry-like drupes of Nyssa (tupelo) and other fruiting trees. The group splits its time between feeding in tree canopies and foraging for invertebrates on the ground. Both secondary tags reflect this dual-zone behavior that all Turdus thrushes share in Amsterdam's urban parks.

This row groups Blackbird and Song Thrush as the primary Turdidae associated with Malus (crab apple) trees in Amsterdam. Both species feed heavily on fallen and hanging crab apples in autumn. The Blackbird dominates ground level while the Song Thrush is slightly more canopy-oriented, but both are strong ground foragers. Ground-park leads as the dominant habitat, with tree-canopy secondary. Park-roamer leads as the dominant movement, reflecting the bounding ground gait both species use across Amsterdam's parks.

The Common Blackbird is one of Amsterdam's most abundant and familiar birds, present year-round in every park, garden, and street with trees. It spends a majority of its time on the ground — flipping leaf litter, pulling earthworms, and foraging beneath Amelanchier, Cornus, Prunus, and Sorbus. Ground-park is the dominant habitat because Blackbirds are quintessential ground foragers; tree-canopy is secondary for roosting and berry feeding. Park-roamer is the dominant movement, with tree-flitter secondary for canopy movement between fruiting trees.

Song Thrush and Mistle Thrush are medium-sized thrushes associated here with Amelanchier (serviceberry), whose small berries they consume eagerly. Both species hunt snails on the ground and are famous for using stones as anvils to crack snail shells — a definitive ground-park behavior. Tree-canopy leads as habitat because berry feeding in the canopy is the primary attractant to Amelanchier, but ground-park is essential as a secondary. Tree-flitter captures movement between fruiting trees and park-roamer the anvil-stone ground foraging.

Fieldfare and Redwing are winter visitors to Amsterdam that concentrate on Malus (crab apple) when available, alongside hawthorn and rowan. Both species gather in flocks to feed on fruit in the canopy and drop to park lawns for earthworms. Their tags mirror the turdus-pilaris entry — tree-canopy dominant for fruit feeding, ground-park secondary for earthworm foraging. Tags are identical for both species in this grouping as their habitat and movement overlap almost completely in the Amsterdam urban context.

Fieldfare and Redwing are migratory winter thrushes that flock through Amsterdam from October to March, stripping Sorbus (rowan) berries. Fieldfares often travel in large flocks, colonising a tree and stripping it bare before moving on. When not in trees they work grassy park areas for earthworms, particularly after rain. Tree-canopy is dominant as the primary feeding location on Sorbus berries; ground-park secondary captures the Fieldfare's characteristic ground-foraging in open lawns.

The Mistle Thrush is the largest European thrush and is particularly associated with mistletoe and berry-bearing trees like Ilex and Sorbus, which it guards aggressively. In Amsterdam it frequents large parks, defending berry-laden trees through winter. It also forages on open lawns for earthworms and large invertebrates, striding across the ground with an upright, bold posture. Tree-canopy leads as habitat for its berry-defending territory, ground-park secondary for its substantial ground foraging. Both movement tags capture its dual lifestyle.

Song Thrush, Redwing, and Fieldfare are thrushes that visit Amsterdam parks and gardens, especially in autumn and winter when they feed heavily on berries from Cornus, Ilex, Morus, Taxus, and Magnolia. They move between fruiting trees in the canopy but spend substantial time on the ground, hunting earthworms and snails in lawn and park soil. The ground-park secondary habitat captures their frequent ground foraging, and park-roamer secondary movement reflects the characteristically bouncy hopping these thrushes use to work open ground.

The Cinnabar is a strikingly patterned day-flying moth with bright red and black wings that serve as aposematic warning coloration. It is closely associated with ragwort (Jacobaea vulgaris) and related Senecio species, where larvae feed communally and become equally conspicuous in their black-and-yellow banded coloration. Adults fly from May to July and can be found nectaring at flowers in open parks, dunes fringe, and disturbed ground. Amsterdam observations cluster around areas where ragwort grows on sunny verges and park edges. The default flower-visitor placement is appropriate since adults are genuinely seen visiting flowers.

Common nettle is ubiquitous in Amsterdam's parks, canal banks, and garden borders wherever nutrient-rich, moist soil is available. It forms dense clonal patches and is ecologically important as a larval foodplant for several butterfly species including the small tortoiseshell and peacock. The plant default of ground-park and none describes its map placement accurately. No override is needed.

Common Valerian is a tall perennial herb with clusters of pale pink flowers that grows in damp meadows, riverbanks, reed margins, and moist park corners throughout Amsterdam. It especially favours the edges of canals, ditches, and wetland areas, making water-edge a meaningful secondary habitat alongside ground-park. The plant can reach 1.5 m and is a notable nectar source for long-tongued moths and bees in summer. Movement remains none; a water-edge secondary is added because Amsterdam observations concentrate along canal margins and ditch banks.

The Red Admiral is a familiar and abundant butterfly across Amsterdam parks, gardens, and canal-side plantings throughout spring and autumn. It is a dedicated nectar feeder visiting thistles, buddleia, ivy bloom, and fallen fruit — all well represented in Amsterdam's urban green spaces. Its flight between flowering plants is typical of the flower-bobber pattern: short directional bobs of half a metre or less between nearby blooms, punctuated by basking pauses with wings spread on warm surfaces. It overwinters as an adult in mild urban winters or migrates south, returning from the Mediterranean in spring. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber fits this species perfectly.

The Painted Lady is a long-distance migrant butterfly that appears in Amsterdam in variable numbers each summer, concentrating wherever thistles, knapweed, and other open-ground composites bloom in parks, waste ground, and canal-side verges. Like other Nymphalid butterflies it is primarily a nectar feeder, moving between flowering plants in the short-bob pattern characteristic of flower-visiting butterflies. In strong migration years it can be extremely numerous across the city, using Amsterdam's canal-side plantings as fuelling stops. The insect category default of flower-visitor / flower-bobber is correct for this species.

This group covers several moth species whose caterpillars feed on Malus (apple/crab apple): the eyed hawk-moth (Smerinthus ocellata), pale tussock (Calliteara pudibunda), green pug (Pasiphila rectangulata), and Chinese character (Cilix glaucata). As caterpillars they live and feed on tree foliage and bark in the canopy. As adult moths they rest motionless on bark or foliage during the day. Tree-canopy is dominant for larval feeding on leaves; tree-bark secondary captures adult resting posture. Idle-only is correct — resting moths do not move, and the flower-bobber default does not fit.

Mice, voles, foxes, and badgers are all ground-dwelling mammals that use Amsterdam's parks, gardens, and canal-side green spaces. They forage beneath fruit trees like Malus for fallen fruit, seeds, and invertebrates. The ground-park default fits all members of this heterogeneous group — all require vegetated soil habitat and move through it by walking or running. Park-roamer captures the random-walk foraging pattern common to all four species in Amsterdam's urban green network.

The larger Welsh wave (Venusia cambrica) and autumn green carpet (Chloroclysta miata) are geometrid moths whose caterpillars feed on Sorbus (rowan) foliage. Both are nocturnal fliers that spend the day resting in complete stillness on bark or foliage, beautifully camouflaged against the tree surface. Tree-canopy is dominant for larval feeding; tree-bark is secondary for daytime adult resting. Idle-only overrides the flower-bobber default — these moths rest motionless by day and their night flight is not represented in the animation system.

Dark Mullein is a biennial or perennial with attractive yellow flowers bearing purple-hairy stamens, found in Amsterdam on dry, sunny, disturbed ground — roadsides, rail margins, dyke tops, and scrubby park edges — often on chalky or sandy substrates. It is less common than the Great Mullein but occurs regularly on the drier south-facing embankments and gravel paths of Amsterdam's outer parks and green corridors. The species prefers the drier, more disturbed ground-urban character over typical park soil, so ground-urban is dominant with ground-park as secondary. Movement is none.

Orange Mullein is a tall biennial herb of dry, sandy, and disturbed soils, favouring roadsides, embankments, and open park edges where competition is low. In Amsterdam it appears mainly on the sandy river-dune soils along the IJ-corridor and in post-industrial disturbed sites. The large woolly rosette in year one gives way to a tall spike of orange-yellow flowers in year two that attract specialist mullein bees and hoverflies. The default ground-park / none pairing is correct for this rooted non-woody plant.

Verticillium species are soil-borne pathogenic fungi that infect plant roots and vascular tissue, causing Verticillium wilt in a wide range of hosts including Catalpa (Indian bean tree). In Amsterdam they persist in park and garden soils, entering through roots. They are invisible in the soil and produce no above-ground fruiting bodies, making ground-park the appropriate placement tag. The override from tree-bark to ground-park reflects their soil-dwelling, root-infecting lifestyle rather than any bark-surface presence.

Vespidae (social and solitary wasps) and Syrphidae (hoverflies) are both common in Amsterdam and are drawn to Liriodendron (tulip tree) and Morus (mulberry) for nectar and as hunting/foraging grounds. Hoverflies are classic flower visitors, hovering between blooms; wasps are more varied but visit flowers for nectar and hunt prey in tree canopy. Flower-visitor leads as dominant habitat since both groups are observed primarily in the flowering context here. Sky-looper secondary captures the strong sustained flight both groups use in transit between flower patches.

Vespula (common and German wasps) and their parasitoid wasp associates are opportunistic predators and scavengers found across Amsterdam. Associated with Koelreuteria (golden rain tree), whose insect fauna provides prey for parasitoid wasps. Social Vespula visit flowers for sugar and hunt caterpillars in tree canopies; parasitoid wasps locate hosts in foliage and bark. Tree-canopy is dominant as the foraging zone; ground-park secondary captures ground-nesting Vespula and soil-pupating prey. Movement combines flower-bobber for nectar visits and sky-looper for the active hunting flights.

Tufted Vetch is a climbing perennial legume common in Amsterdam's grassy park borders, roadsides, and scrubby canal banks, clambering over neighbouring vegetation with tendrils to reach the light. It produces dense racemes of violet-blue flowers from June to August that are visited by bumblebees, particularly long-tongued species that can reach the nectar. As a nitrogen-fixing legume it enriches the soil in parks and meadow patches where it establishes. The plant default of ground-park / none applies without modification: it roots in vegetated ground and does not move.

Fodder Vetch is a climbing or scrambling annual legume with dense racemes of purple and white flowers, introduced across Europe as a fodder crop and now naturalised on roadsides, railway verges, and rough grassland. In Amsterdam it appears in parks, along cycle paths, and on sandy or disturbed soils. Its tendrils allow it to climb through neighbouring vegetation but it remains ground-rooted. The ground-park/none plant defaults apply without modification.

The Hornet Plumehorn is the largest hoverfly in northwest Europe and a spectacular hornet mimic with amber and black banding. It is a recent range expander in the Netherlands, now well established in Amsterdam's parks and gardens. Adults are avid flower visitors, favouring large umbellifers such as hogweed, and garden flowers including Buddleia and Angelica, making flower-visitor the dominant placement. Larvae develop in the nests of common wasps and hornets where they feed on nest debris, but the conspicuous adult flower-visiting behaviour is what observers record in Amsterdam. The sky-looper secondary captures the powerful, direct transit flights between flower patches. Adults fly from July to October.

The Red Fox and Western Hedgehog are two of Amsterdam's most-watched urban mammals. Both are crepuscular/nocturnal animals that forage through parks, gardens, and canal-side green strips. They are associated with Cupressocyparis hedges (Leyland cypress), which provide dense cover for shelter and nesting. Ground-park fits both perfectly — they require soil and vegetation rather than paved surfaces. Park-roamer captures the wandering foraging style both species use as they patrol territory through Amsterdam's fragmented green network.

This row groups Red Fox, Badger, and Wild Boar as frugivorous mammals that consume fallen fruit from Pyrus (pear) trees in Amsterdam's parks. Red Fox is common in Amsterdam's parks and suburban gardens; Badger and Wild Boar are edge-of-city visitors. All three are ground-dwelling mammals that forage across vegetated park areas. The mammal default of ground-park and park-roamer fits the fox and badger well; wild boar occasional incursions into Amsterdam's green periphery follow the same ground-park pattern.

Winter moth (Operophtera brumata) and tortrix moths (Tortricidae) are generalist leaf-feeders whose caterpillars attack the buds and young leaves of Pterocarya (Caucasian wingnut) and many other deciduous trees in Amsterdam. Caterpillars spin silken shelters on shoots and hang on silk threads. Adult winter moths are flightless females that crawl up trunks in winter. Tree-canopy is dominant for caterpillar feeding on buds and leaves; tree-bark secondary for the trunk-crawling adult females and pupation. Idle-only overrides flower-bobber — neither caterpillars nor resting moths visit flowers.

Wood Pigeon, Magpie, and Carrion Crow are three of Amsterdam's most abundant and adaptable urban birds, all found around Pterocarya (wingnut) trees for seeds and insects. All three are true urban generalists that use pavements, rooftops, lawns, and trees interchangeably. The anywhere habitat tag fits this genuinely multi-context group; tree-canopy secondary captures their regular tree use for nesting and perching. Urban-walker leads movement since all three species regularly walk on paved and grassy ground; tree-flitter secondary captures canopy movement between perches.

The Violet Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa violacea) is the largest bee in Europe and a climate-change beneficiary now appearing in Amsterdam. It is attracted to Catalpa (Indian bean tree) flowers, which it can access thanks to its size. Like all Xylocopa, females nest in dry woody materials — fence posts, structural timber — mapped to wall-and-roof here. Flower-visitor dominates as the primary foraging habitat; wall-and-roof secondary captures the nesting site. Its powerful, audible flight makes sky-looper the appropriate secondary movement for transit between flower patches.

Carpenter bees (Xylocopa) are large solitary bees that have expanded their range northward and are increasingly recorded in Amsterdam on warm, south-facing days. They visit Cercis (redbud) and Platanus flowers for pollen and nectar. Female carpenter bees nest by boring tunnels into dry wood — fence posts, wooden beams, and old timber structures count as wall-and-roof in the placement system. Flower-visitor is the dominant habitat for their foraging activity; wall-and-roof secondary captures the nest site. Sky-looper secondary reflects their powerful, long-distance transit flights between patches.

The Granulate Ambrosia Beetle (Xylosandrus crassiusculus) is an invasive bark beetle from East Asia that bores into the wood of stressed trees, including Liquidambar (sweetgum) in Amsterdam. Females excavate galleries in the bark and woody tissue where they cultivate ambrosia fungi for food. The entire lifecycle — boring, feeding, breeding — occurs inside the bark and wood. Tree-bark is the sole appropriate habitat tag. Idle-only overrides the flower-bobber default since this beetle does not visit flowers; it spends its adult life boring into and resting within bark.

Xylotrechus chinensis is a longhorn beetle (Cerambycidae) originally from Asia, recorded as a pest of Morus (mulberry) in Europe. Larvae bore deeply into the woody tissue and bark of mulberry trees, causing significant structural damage. Adults emerge in summer and rest on the bark surface or chew at twigs in the canopy. Tree-bark is dominant as the primary habitat for both larvae and resting adults; tree-canopy secondary captures adult twig feeding and egg-laying on smaller branches. Idle-only is appropriate — adults rest or move slowly on bark surfaces, not visiting flowers.

The Bird-cherry Ermine Moth (Yponomeuta evonymella) and its close relative Y. padella are small moths notorious for completely encasing Prunus (cherry, bird cherry) trees in dense communal silk webs spun by the caterpillars. In Amsterdam, silked-up Prunus trees are a common summer sight, the web shrouds covering entire crowns. Caterpillars feed on leaves within the silk tent in the tree canopy; adults rest on bark and foliage. Tree-canopy dominates for the conspicuous canopy webbing; tree-bark secondary for adult moths at rest. Idle-only overrides flower-bobber — neither the webbing caterpillars nor resting adults visit flowers.

Yponomeuta ermine moths are small white moths whose larvae are notorious for spinning dense communal silk tents over shrubs and small trees — most famously bird-cherry, spindle, and apple — entirely defoliating them during outbreaks visible across Amsterdam parks. Adults are nocturnal and rest during the day on bark or foliage, making them effectively stationary when observed. The caterpillar webs cling to tree-canopy and bark surfaces so tree-canopy is the dominant habitat with tree-bark as secondary. Resting adults warrant idle-only as the primary movement; the secondary flower-bobber covers the rare observation of adults at flowers at dusk.

The Wood Leopard Moth (Zeuzera pyrina) is a large, distinctive moth whose caterpillars bore into the woody stems of many deciduous trees including Populus (poplar). The Figure of Eight moth (Diloba caeruleocephala) also uses Populus and related trees. Zeuzera caterpillars spend up to three years tunnelling through sapwood; adults are nocturnal fliers that rest on bark and trunks during the day. Tree-bark is dominant given the deep wood-boring larval phase; tree-canopy secondary for leaf-feeding by Diloba caterpillars. Idle-only overrides the flower-bobber default as neither species is a flower visitor.

The Six-spot Burnet is a day-flying moth with striking metallic green forewings and six crimson spots, one of the few moths commonly seen visiting flowers in bright sunshine. In Amsterdam it occurs on rough grassland, cemetery margins, and park edges with Bird's-foot Trefoil (Lotus corniculatus), on which the larvae feed. Adults are strong flower visitors — particularly on Knautia, Centaurea, and thistles — moving deliberately between flower heads (flower-bobber dominant) and resting on the flower with wings half-open (idle-only secondary). Overridden from default to add ground-park secondary for the larval grassland habitat, and to note that flower-bobber fits better than the generic default for this conspicuous day-flier.
